scripture_says.text: Scripture attributes writings to Moses, including 'the law' and 'the book.'

history_records.text: Jewish tradition (Talmud, Josephus) attributes Genesis to Moses.
The earliest surviving Hebrew manuscripts of Genesis are the Dead Sea Scrolls (c. 250-100 BC).

question: How many angels are named in the Bible?

scripture_says.text: Two angels are named in the Protestant canon: Michael and Gabriel.
Michael is called 'the archangel' and 'one of the chief princes'.
Gabriel appears to Daniel, Zechariah, and Mary.

history_records.text: The name 'Michael' means 'Who is like God?' in Hebrew.
The name 'Gabriel' means 'Man of God' or 'God is my strength' in Hebrew.

scripture_says.text: Melchizedek was king of Salem and priest of God Most High (El Elyon). He brought out bread and wine and blessed Abram.
David prophesies of one who is 'a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek'.
Hebrews describes him as 'without father, without mother, without genealogy, having neither beginning of days nor end of life'.
Abraham gave him a tenth of everything.

history_records.text: 'Melchizedek' means 'King of Righteousness' in Hebrew. 'Salem' is understood to be an early name for Jerusalem.

question: How long were the Israelites in Egypt?

scripture_says.text: Exodus states the sojourning was 430 years.
God told Abraham his descendants would be sojourners in a land not theirs for 400 years.
Stephen in Acts also references 400 years.
Paul in Galatians mentions 430 years from the promise to the law.

history_records.text: The Septuagint (LXX) reads 'the land of Egypt and the land of Canaan' for the 430 years.
Josephus records two traditions: 400 years and 215 years in Egypt proper.

question: What was Paul's 'thorn in the flesh'?

scripture_says.text: Paul describes it as 'a thorn in the flesh, a messenger of Satan to buffet me, lest I should be exalted above measure'.
Paul asked the Lord three times to remove it, but was told 'My grace is sufficient for you'.
Paul wrote to the Galatians: 'If possible, you would have gouged out your eyes and given them to me.'
Paul wrote with 'large letters' in his own hand.

history_records.text: Early church fathers offered various theories: Tertullian suggested earache or headache; Chrysostom suggested persecution.

scripture_says.text: The Nephilim were on the earth in those days, and also afterward, when the sons of God came in to the daughters of man and they bore children to them. These were the mighty men who were of old, the men of renown.
The spies in Canaan reported seeing Nephilim (the sons of Anak) and felt like grasshoppers in comparison.

history_records.text: The Hebrew word 'Nephilim' may derive from 'naphal' (to fall) or 'nephil' (giant). The Septuagint translates it as 'gigantes' (giants).
Josephus records that the offspring of angels and women produced giants.

question: What is the unforgivable sin (blasphemy against the Holy Spirit)?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said blasphemy against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, either in this age or the age to come.
The Pharisees said Jesus cast out demons by Beelzebul, the prince of demons.
Mark records it as 'an eternal sin' because they said 'He has an unclean spirit'.

history_records.text: Early church fathers varied in interpretation: Athanasius linked it to attributing the Spirit's work to Satan; Augustine connected it to final impenitence.

question: Where was the Garden of Eden located?

scripture_says.text: Eden was in the east; a river flowed out of Eden and divided into four rivers: Pishon, Gihon, Tigris (Hiddekel), and Euphrates.
The Pishon flowed around the land of Havilah (where there is gold, bdellium, and onyx).
The Gihon flowed around the land of Cush.
The Tigris flowed east of Assyria.
Matthew records that Judas hanged himself after returning the thirty pieces of silver.
Acts records that Judas fell headlong in a field, his body burst open, and his intestines spilled out.
Matthew says the chief priests bought the field; Acts says Judas acquired it.

history_records.text: Papias (early 2nd century) described Judas' body swelling grotesquely before death.

sources.label: Papias, fragment preserved in Apollinaris of Laodicea

question: What day of the week was Jesus crucified?

scripture_says.text: All four Gospels state it was 'the day of Preparation' (Paraskeue).
Mark explicitly calls it 'the day before the Sabbath'.
Jesus rose 'on the first day of the week' (Sunday).
Jesus said he would be 'three days and three nights in the heart of the earth'.

history_records.text: 'Paraskeue' (Preparation) became the standard Greek word for Friday in Christian usage.

question: What does the Bible say about murder?

scripture_says.text: The sixth commandment states: 'You shall not murder.'
Jesus taught that anger toward a brother makes one liable to judgment, and calling someone a fool makes one liable to the fire of Gehenna.
John writes: 'Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer, and you know that no murderer has eternal life abiding in him.'
Genesis records the first murder: Cain killed his brother Abel.
After the flood, God instituted capital punishment for murder: 'Whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew word 'ratsach' (רָצַח) used in the commandment refers specifically to unlawful killing, distinct from killing in war or capital punishment.
The Code of Hammurabi (c. 1754 BC) also prescribed death for murder, showing this was a common ancient Near Eastern standard.

question: What does the Bible say about stealing?

scripture_says.text: The eighth commandment states: 'You shall not steal.'
Paul commanded: 'Let the thief no longer steal, but rather let him labor, doing honest work with his own hands.'
The Law required restitution for theft: if a man steals an ox or sheep, he shall repay five oxen or four sheep.
Zacchaeus vowed to restore fourfold to anyone he had defrauded.
Thieves are listed among those who will not inherit the kingdom of God.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'ganab' (גָּנַב) means to steal by stealth, while 'gazal' refers to robbery by violence.
Ancient Near Eastern law codes uniformly condemned theft. The Code of Hammurabi prescribed death for stealing from temples or palaces.

question: What does the Bible say about adultery?

scripture_says.text: The seventh commandment states: 'You shall not commit adultery.'
Jesus expanded this: 'Everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart.'
Under the Law, the penalty for adultery was death for both parties.
Proverbs warns that the adulterer 'destroys himself' and will receive 'wounds and dishonor.'
The sexually immoral will not inherit the kingdom of God.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'naaph' (נָאַף) refers specifically to sexual relations with another man's wife. The Greek 'moicheia' carries the same meaning.
In Roman law, adultery (adulterium) was criminalized under the Lex Julia de adulteriis (18 BC), with penalties including exile.

question: What does the Bible say about coveting?

scripture_says.text: The tenth commandment forbids coveting a neighbor's house, wife, servants, animals, or anything that belongs to the neighbor.
Paul identified covetousness as idolatry.
Jesus warned: 'Take care, and be on your guard against all covetousness, for one's life does not consist in the abundance of his possessions.'
Achan's coveting of spoils from Jericho brought death upon himself and his family.
Paul confessed that the commandment against coveting made him aware of sin.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'chamad' (חָמַד) means to desire or delight in. The Greek 'epithymia' refers to strong desire or lust.

question: What does the Bible say about idolatry?

scripture_says.text: The first commandment: 'You shall have no other gods before me.'
The second commandment forbids making carved images or bowing down to them.
Paul wrote that idolaters will not inherit the kingdom of God.
John concludes his first epistle: 'Little children, keep yourselves from idols.'
The golden calf incident resulted in about 3,000 deaths.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'pesel' (פֶּסֶל) refers to a carved image, while 'atsab' refers to an idol. The Greek 'eidolon' means image or likeness.
Archaeological evidence shows Canaanite worship included Baal, Asherah, and Molech, all condemned in scripture.

question: What does the Bible say about taking God's name in vain?

scripture_says.text: The third commandment: 'You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain, for the LORD will not hold him guiltless who takes his name in vain.'
Leviticus prescribes death by stoning for blaspheming the Name.
Jesus taught to pray: 'Hallowed be your name.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shav' (שָׁוְא) means emptiness, falsehood, or vanity. The commandment prohibits using God's name for worthless or deceptive purposes.
By the Second Temple period, Jews avoided pronouncing the divine name YHWH, substituting 'Adonai' (Lord) when reading scripture.

question: What does the Bible say about honoring parents?

scripture_says.text: The fifth commandment: 'Honor your father and your mother, that your days may be long in the land.'
Paul calls this 'the first commandment with a promise.'
The Law prescribed death for striking or cursing a parent.
Jesus criticized the Pharisees for using 'Corban' to avoid supporting parents.
Paul instructs: 'Children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'kabed' (כָּבֵד) means to honor, make heavy, or glorify. It implies treating parents with weight and significance.

question: What does the Bible say about lying?

scripture_says.text: The ninth commandment: 'You shall not bear false witness against your neighbor.'
Proverbs lists 'a lying tongue' and 'a false witness who breathes out lies' among things the LORD hates.
The Law required that false witnesses receive the punishment they intended for the accused.
Jesus said: 'Let what you say be simply Yes or No; anything more than this comes from evil.'
John records Jesus saying the devil 'is a liar and the father of lies.'
Ananias and Sapphira died after lying to the Holy Spirit about the price of their land.
Revelation states that all liars will have their part in the lake of fire.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'sheqer' (שֶׁקֶר) means falsehood, deception, or lie. The Greek 'pseudos' carries the same meaning.

question: What does the Bible say about money?

scripture_says.text: Jesus taught: 'No one can serve two masters... You cannot serve God and money.'
Paul wrote: 'The love of money is a root of all kinds of evils.'
Ecclesiastes observes: 'He who loves money will not be satisfied with money.'
Jesus told the rich young ruler: 'Sell all that you have and distribute to the poor.'
Proverbs states: 'Wealth gained hastily will dwindle, but whoever gathers little by little will increase it.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'mammon' (μαμωνᾶς) in Matthew 6:24 is an Aramaic loanword meaning wealth or property.

question: What does the Bible say about tithing?

scripture_says.text: Abraham gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything.
The Law commanded: 'You shall tithe all the yield of your seed that comes from the field year by year.'
Malachi records: 'Bring the full tithe into the storehouse... and thereby put me to the test, says the LORD.'
Jesus affirmed tithing while criticizing Pharisees for neglecting justice, mercy, and faithfulness.
The Levites received the tithe as their inheritance for their service.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'maaser' (מַעֲשֵׂר) means a tenth part. The practice of tithing existed in various ancient Near Eastern cultures.
The Law actually prescribed multiple tithes: annual tithe (Levitical), festival tithe, and third-year poor tithe.

question: What does the Bible say about debt?

scripture_says.text: Proverbs states: 'The borrower is the slave of the lender.'
Paul instructed: 'Owe no one anything, except to love each other.'
The wicked borrows but does not pay back.
Every seven years, creditors were to release debts owed by fellow Israelites.
Jesus told a parable of a servant forgiven a massive debt who refused to forgive a smaller debt.

history_records.text: Debt slavery was common in the ancient world. The Jubilee year (every 50 years) released Israelite debt slaves and returned ancestral land.

question: What does the Bible say about lending and interest?

scripture_says.text: The Law prohibited charging interest to a fellow Israelite.
Interest could be charged to foreigners.
Jesus taught: 'Lend, expecting nothing in return, and your reward will be great.'
Psalm 15 describes the righteous as one who 'does not put out his money at interest.'
In the parable of the talents, the master rebuked the servant for not putting money with bankers to earn interest.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'neshek' (נֶשֶׁךְ) means interest, literally 'a bite.' The Greek 'tokos' means offspring or interest (money that breeds).
Interest rates in ancient Babylon could reach 20-33%. The prohibition protected poor Israelites from exploitation.

question: What does the Bible say about generosity?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'It is more blessed to give than to receive.'
Paul taught: 'Whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and whoever sows bountifully will also reap bountifully.'
Proverbs states: 'One gives freely, yet grows all the richer; another withholds what he should give, and only suffers want.'
The widow's two small coins were commended as more than all the others, for she gave out of her poverty.
Each one should give 'as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'hilaron' (ἱλαρόν) in 2 Corinthians 9:7, meaning cheerful or joyful, is the root of the English word 'hilarious.'

question: What does the Bible say about wealth and riches?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God.'
Proverbs acknowledges: 'The blessing of the LORD makes rich, and he adds no sorrow with it.'
Jesus warned: 'Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth... but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven.'
Abraham, Job, and Solomon were described as very wealthy.
James warned the rich: 'Your riches have rotted and your garments are moth-eaten.'

question: What does the Bible say about poverty and the poor?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God.'
The Law commanded leaving gleanings for the poor and sojourner.
Proverbs states: 'Whoever oppresses a poor man insults his Maker, but he who is generous to the needy honors him.'
James writes: 'Has not God chosen those who are poor in the world to be rich in faith?'
Jesus said: 'The poor you always have with you.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'ani' (עָנִי) means poor, afflicted, or humble. The Greek 'ptochos' refers to one who crouches or cowers, indicating extreme poverty.

question: What does the Bible say about greed?

scripture_says.text: Jesus warned: 'Take care, and be on your guard against all covetousness, for one's life does not consist in the abundance of his possessions.'
Paul identified greed as idolatry.
The greedy will not inherit the kingdom of God.
Balaam is described as one who 'loved gain from wrongdoing.'
Gehazi was struck with leprosy for his greed in taking payment from Naaman.

history_records.text: The Greek 'pleonexia' (πλεονεξία) means greediness or covetousness, literally 'the desire to have more.'

question: What does the Bible say about marriage?

scripture_says.text: God said: 'It is not good that the man should be alone; I will make him a helper fit for him.'
Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife, and they shall become one flesh.
Jesus said: 'What therefore God has joined together, let not man separate.'
Hebrews states: 'Let marriage be held in honor among all, and let the marriage bed be undefiled.'
Paul compared marriage to Christ and the church.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'ishshah' (אִשָּׁה) means woman or wife. Marriage customs included a bride price (mohar) paid to the bride's father.
Jewish marriage in the Second Temple period involved betrothal (erusin) and wedding (nissuin), typically separated by about a year.

question: What does the Bible say about divorce?

scripture_says.text: God declares: 'I hate divorce.'
Moses permitted divorce certificates 'because of your hardness of heart.'
Jesus said: 'Whoever divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, and marries another, commits adultery.'
Paul instructed: 'To the married I give this charge (not I, but the Lord): the wife should not separate from her husband.'
Paul wrote that if an unbelieving spouse leaves, the believer 'is not enslaved.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'keritut' (כְּרִיתוּת) means a cutting off, referring to the divorce certificate. The Greek 'apostasion' similarly means a document of repudiation.
Jewish schools debated grounds for divorce: Shammai permitted it only for sexual immorality; Hillel permitted it for any cause.

question: What does the Bible say about children?

scripture_says.text: Children are a heritage from the LORD, the fruit of the womb a reward.
Jesus said: 'Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven.'
Fathers are instructed not to provoke children to anger, but to bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord.
Proverbs instructs: 'Train up a child in the way he should go; even when he is old he will not depart from it.'
The Law commanded teaching children diligently about God's commandments.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'na'ar' (נַעַר) can mean child, youth, or servant. The Greek 'teknon' specifically means offspring or child.
In Greco-Roman culture, paterfamilias had power to expose unwanted infants. Jewish and Christian communities rejected this practice.

question: What does the Bible say about friendship?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends.'
Proverbs states: 'A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity.'
Jonathan's soul was knit to David's soul, and Jonathan loved him as his own soul.
Proverbs warns: 'A man of many companions may come to ruin, but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother.'
Paul warned: 'Bad company ruins good morals.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'rea' (רֵעַ) means friend, companion, or neighbor. The Greek 'philos' denotes one who is beloved or dear.
Abraham is called 'the friend of God' in both James and Isaiah.

question: What does the Bible say about enemies?

scripture_says.text: Jesus taught: 'Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you.'
Paul instructed: 'If your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him something to drink.'
Jesus said: 'Bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you.'
Proverbs states: 'Do not rejoice when your enemy falls, and let not your heart be glad when he stumbles.'
David spared Saul's life twice, refusing to harm 'the LORD's anointed.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'oyeb' (אֹיֵב) means enemy or foe. The Greek 'echthros' refers to one who is hostile or hateful.

question: What does the Bible say about forgiveness?

scripture_says.text: Jesus taught: 'If you forgive others their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you, but if you do not forgive others, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses.'
Peter asked if he should forgive seven times. Jesus replied: 'I do not say to you seven times, but seventy-seven times.'
Paul instructed: 'Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you.'
Stephen, while being stoned, prayed: 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them.'
Jesus on the cross said: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'aphiemi' (ἀφίημι) means to send away, release, or forgive. The Hebrew 'salach' (סָלַח) is used only of God's forgiveness; 'nasa' (נָשָׂא) means to lift or carry away sin.

question: What does the Bible say about love?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said the greatest commandment is: 'You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.'
The second is: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself.'
John wrote: 'God is love.'
Paul described love: 'Love is patient and kind; love does not envy or boast; it is not arrogant or rude.'
Jesus said: 'By this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another.'

history_records.text: Greek distinguishes types of love: 'agape' (unconditional love), 'phileo' (friendship love), 'eros' (romantic love, not used in NT), 'storge' (family affection).
The Hebrew 'ahab' (אָהַב) covers all types of love. The Shema (Deut 6:4-5) commands love for God with heart, soul, and might.

question: What does the Bible say about submission?

scripture_says.text: Paul instructed: 'Wives, submit to your own husbands, as to the Lord.'
Paul also wrote: 'Submitting to one another out of reverence for Christ.'
Peter instructed servants to submit to masters, even harsh ones.
James wrote: 'Submit yourselves therefore to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.'
Citizens are to be subject to governing authorities, for 'there is no authority except from God.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'hypotasso' (ὑποτάσσω) means to arrange under, to subordinate, or to submit. It is used in both voluntary and hierarchical contexts.

question: What does the Bible say about hospitality?

scripture_says.text: Hebrews instructs: 'Do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares.'
Peter wrote: 'Show hospitality to one another without grumbling.'
Paul listed hospitality as a qualification for overseers.
Abraham showed hospitality to three visitors at Mamre.
Jesus said: 'I was a stranger and you welcomed me.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'philoxenia' (φιλοξενία) means love of strangers or hospitality. In the ancient world, travelers depended on hospitality as inns were rare and disreputable.
Lot's hospitality in Sodom led him to protect his guests at great personal cost.

question: What does the Bible say about the tongue?

scripture_says.text: James wrote: 'The tongue is a small member, yet it boasts of great things... It is a restless evil, full of deadly poison.'
Proverbs states: 'Death and life are in the power of the tongue.'
Jesus said: 'Out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks.'
James compared the tongue to a fire that can set the whole course of life ablaze.
Proverbs notes: 'A gentle tongue is a tree of life, but perverseness in it breaks the spirit.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'lashon' (לָשׁוֹן) and Greek 'glossa' (γλῶσσα) both mean tongue and by extension language or speech.

question: What does the Bible say about gossip?

scripture_says.text: Proverbs warns: 'A gossip betrays a confidence, but a trustworthy person keeps a secret.'
Proverbs states: 'The words of a gossip are like choice morsels; they go down to the inmost parts.'
Gossips are listed among those God gave over to a debased mind.
Paul warned about idle young widows becoming gossips and busybodies.
Proverbs notes: 'A gossip separates close friends.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'rakil' (רָכִיל) means slanderer or talebearer. The Greek 'psithyrismos' (ψιθυρισμός) means whispering or secret slander.

question: What does the Bible say about slander?

scripture_says.text: The Law commanded: 'You shall not go around as a slanderer among your people.'
Peter instructs to 'put away all malice and all deceit and hypocrisy and envy and all slander.'
Psalm 101 declares: 'Whoever slanders his neighbor secretly I will destroy.'
Slanderers are listed among those who will not inherit the kingdom of God.
Paul listed slander among works of the flesh.

history_records.text: The Greek 'blasphemia' (βλασφημία) can mean slander against humans or blasphemy against God, depending on context.
False accusation carried severe penalties in ancient law codes. The Code of Hammurabi prescribed death for false capital accusations.

question: What does the Bible say about complaining?

scripture_says.text: Paul instructed: 'Do all things without grumbling or disputing.'
The Israelites' complaining in the wilderness brought judgment.
Jude warned about 'grumblers, malcontents, following their own sinful desires.'
Paul reminded the Corinthians not to grumble 'as some of them did and were destroyed by the Destroyer.'
James instructed: 'Do not grumble against one another, brothers, so that you may not be judged.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'goggysmos' (γογγυσμός) means grumbling or murmuring, often in secret discontent. The Hebrew 'lun' (לוּן) means to murmur or complain.

question: What does the Bible say about encouragement?

scripture_says.text: Hebrews instructs: 'Encourage one another daily, as long as it is called Today, so that none of you may be hardened by sin's deceitfulness.'
Paul wrote: 'Therefore encourage one another and build one another up.'
Barnabas, whose name means 'son of encouragement,' sold a field and gave the proceeds to the apostles.
Proverbs states: 'Anxiety in a man's heart weighs him down, but a good word makes him glad.'
Paul urged: 'Let no corrupting talk come out of your mouths, but only such as is good for building up.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'parakaleo' (παρακαλέω) means to call alongside, to encourage, comfort, or exhort. It is related to 'Paraclete,' a title for the Holy Spirit.

question: What does the Bible say about oaths and swearing?

scripture_says.text: Jesus taught: 'Do not take an oath at all, either by heaven, for it is the throne of God, or by the earth, for it is his footstool... Let what you say be simply Yes or No.'
James echoed this: 'Do not swear, either by heaven or by earth or by any other oath, but let your yes be yes and your no be no.'
The Law commanded: 'You shall not swear by my name falsely, and so profane the name of your God.'
God himself swore by himself because he had no one greater to swear by.
Ecclesiastes warns: 'When you vow a vow to God, do not delay paying it, for he has no pleasure in fools. Pay what you vow.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'horkos' (ὅρκος) means an oath or solemn affirmation. Jewish tradition developed elaborate rules about binding vs. non-binding oaths.

question: What does the Bible say about boasting?

scripture_says.text: Jeremiah records: 'Let not the wise man boast in his wisdom, let not the mighty man boast in his might, let not the rich man boast in his riches, but let him who boasts boast in this, that he understands and knows me.'
Paul wrote: 'Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord.'
James warned: 'Come now, you who say, Today or tomorrow we will go into such and such a town... You boast in your arrogance. All such boasting is evil.'
Paul stated: 'By grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast.'
Proverbs warns: 'Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'kauchaomai' (καυχάομαι) means to boast, glory, or pride oneself. Paul uses it both negatively (human boasting) and positively (boasting in the Lord).

question: What does the Bible say about anger?

scripture_says.text: Paul instructed: 'Be angry and do not sin; do not let the sun go down on your anger.'
Proverbs states: 'Whoever is slow to anger is better than the mighty, and he who rules his spirit than he who takes a city.'
James wrote: 'Let every person be quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to anger; for the anger of man does not produce the righteousness of God.'
Jesus was angry when he cleansed the temple.
God is described as slow to anger.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'aph' (אַף) means nose or nostril, and by extension anger (from the flaring of nostrils). The Greek 'orge' refers to settled indignation.

question: What does the Bible say about fear?

scripture_says.text: The phrase 'fear not' or 'do not be afraid' appears frequently throughout scripture.
John wrote: 'There is no fear in love, but perfect love casts out fear.'
The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.
Paul wrote that God gave us 'a spirit not of fear but of power and love and self-control.'
Jesus told his disciples not to fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'yirah' (יִרְאָה) can mean fear, reverence, or awe. Context determines whether it refers to terror or reverent respect.
'Fear of the LORD' (yirat YHWH) is a key wisdom concept, denoting proper reverence and obedience toward God.

question: What does the Bible say about joy?

scripture_says.text: Paul instructed: 'Rejoice in the Lord always; again I will say, rejoice.'
The fruit of the Spirit includes joy.
Nehemiah declared: 'The joy of the LORD is your strength.'
Jesus spoke of his joy being made complete in believers.
James wrote: 'Count it all joy, my brothers, when you meet trials of various kinds.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'simchah' (שִׂמְחָה) means joy, gladness, or mirth. The Greek 'chara' (χαρά) refers to joy or gladness, often as a response to God's work.

question: What does the Bible say about peace?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give to you.'
The fruit of the Spirit includes peace.
Paul wrote: 'The peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus.'
Isaiah prophesied the Messiah as the 'Prince of Peace.'
Jesus said: 'Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shalom' (שָׁלוֹם) means completeness, soundness, welfare, or peace. It encompasses wellbeing in all dimensions of life.
The Greek 'eirene' (εἰρήνη) refers to peace, harmony, or tranquility. The Pax Romana (Roman Peace) was the political peace of the empire during NT times.

question: What does the Bible say about patience?

scripture_says.text: The fruit of the Spirit includes patience.
James wrote: 'Let patience have its full effect, that you may be perfect and complete, lacking in nothing.'
Paul instructed: 'Be patient, therefore, brothers, until the coming of the Lord. See how the farmer waits for the precious fruit of the earth.'
Love 'is patient and kind.'
God is described as patient, 'not wishing that any should perish.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'makrothymia' (μακροθυμία) means longsuffering or patience, literally 'long-tempered.' It refers to patient endurance under provocation.
The Greek 'hypomone' (ὑπομονή) means steadfastness or endurance, patient waiting under trial.

question: What does the Bible say about humility?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Whoever exalts himself will be humbled, and whoever humbles himself will be exalted.'
Peter instructed: 'Clothe yourselves, all of you, with humility toward one another, for God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.'
Paul described Christ who 'humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death.'
Micah declared what the LORD requires: 'to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God.'
Moses is described as 'very meek, more than all people who were on the face of the earth.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'anav' (עָנָו) means humble, poor, or afflicted. The Greek 'tapeinos' (ταπεινός) means low, humble, or of low degree.

question: What does the Bible say about pride?

scripture_says.text: Proverbs states: 'Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.'
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.
Proverbs lists 'haughty eyes' first among seven things the LORD hates.
Isaiah describes the fall of the king of Babylon: 'You said in your heart, I will ascend to heaven; above the stars of God I will set my throne.'
Nebuchadnezzar was humbled for his pride: 'Is not this great Babylon, which I have built?' Immediately he was driven from among men.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'gaon' (גָּאוֹן) means pride, majesty, or arrogance. The Greek 'hyperephania' (ὑπερηφανία) means haughtiness or arrogance.

question: What does the Bible say about self-control?

scripture_says.text: The fruit of the Spirit includes self-control.
Peter instructs believers to supplement faith with virtue, knowledge, self-control, steadfastness, godliness.
Proverbs states: 'A man without self-control is like a city broken into and left without walls.'
Paul wrote: 'Every athlete exercises self-control in all things.'
Paul told Titus that older men should be 'self-controlled, sound in faith, in love, and in steadfastness.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'enkrateia' (ἐγκράτεια) means self-control or temperance, the mastery of desires and passions. It was considered a cardinal virtue in Greek philosophy.

question: What does the Bible say about envy?

scripture_says.text: Proverbs states: 'A tranquil heart gives life to the flesh, but envy makes the bones rot.'
Envy is listed among works of the flesh.
James wrote: 'Where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there will be disorder and every vile practice.'
Love 'does not envy.'
Pilate recognized it was 'out of envy' that the chief priests delivered Jesus.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'qinah' (קִנְאָה) can mean jealousy, envy, or zeal. The Greek 'phthonos' (φθόνος) refers specifically to envy or ill-will.

question: What does the Bible say about prayer?

scripture_says.text: Jesus taught: 'When you pray, go into your room and shut the door and pray to your Father who is in secret.'
Paul instructed: 'Pray without ceasing.'
James wrote: 'The prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working.'
Jesus provided a model prayer beginning: 'Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name.'
Jesus said: 'Whatever you ask in prayer, believe that you have received it, and it will be yours.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'palal' (פָּלַל) means to pray or intercede. The Greek 'proseuchomai' (προσεύχομαι) means to pray, literally 'to speak toward' God.
Jews prayed three times daily (Dan 6:10). The Didache (c. 70-100 AD) instructed Christians to pray the Lord's Prayer three times daily.

question: What does the Bible say about fasting?

scripture_says.text: Jesus taught: 'When you fast, anoint your head and wash your face, that your fasting may not be seen by others but by your Father who is in secret.'
Jesus said: 'This kind cannot be driven out by anything but prayer and fasting.'
The Day of Atonement required afflicting oneself (fasting).
Moses fasted forty days on Mount Sinai.
Jesus fasted forty days in the wilderness.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'tsum' (צוּם) means to fast or abstain from food. The Greek 'nesteuo' (νηστεύω) carries the same meaning.
Pharisees fasted twice a week, on Monday and Thursday. The Didache instructed Christians to fast on Wednesday and Friday instead.

question: What does the Bible say about worship?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'God is spirit, and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth.'
The first commandment: 'You shall have no other gods before me.'
Jesus quoted: 'You shall worship the Lord your God and him only shall you serve.'
Psalm 95 calls: 'Oh come, let us worship and bow down; let us kneel before the LORD, our Maker!'
The four living creatures and twenty-four elders worship before God's throne.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shachah' (שָׁחָה) means to bow down or prostrate oneself. The Greek 'proskyneo' (προσκυνέω) means to prostrate oneself, to worship.
Early Christian worship included psalms, hymns, scripture reading, prayers, and the Lord's Supper.

question: What does the Bible say about the Sabbath?

scripture_says.text: The fourth commandment: 'Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy. Six days you shall labor, and do all your work, but the seventh day is a Sabbath to the LORD your God.'
God blessed the seventh day and made it holy, because on it he rested from all his work.
Jesus said: 'The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath.'
Paul wrote: 'Let no one pass judgment on you in questions of food and drink, or with regard to a festival or a new moon or a Sabbath.'
The penalty for breaking the Sabbath under the Law was death.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shabbat' (שַׁבָּת) means cessation or rest. The Sabbath runs from Friday sunset to Saturday sunset in Jewish reckoning.
Early Christians met on 'the first day of the week' (Sunday) to break bread. By the 2nd century, Sunday was called 'the Lord's Day.'

question: What does the Bible say about baptism?

scripture_says.text: Jesus commanded: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.'
Peter said: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins.'
Paul wrote: 'We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead, we too might walk in newness of life.'
Peter wrote that baptism 'now saves you, not as a removal of dirt from the body but as an appeal to God for a good conscience.'
The Ethiopian eunuch said: 'See, here is water! What prevents me from being baptized?'

history_records.text: The Greek 'baptizo' (βαπτίζω) means to dip, immerse, or submerge. Jewish ritual immersion (mikveh) predates Christian baptism.
The Didache (c. 70-100 AD) describes baptism in running water, cold preferred, with pouring permitted if immersion is impossible.

question: What does the Bible say about Communion (the Lord's Supper)?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'This is my body, which is given for you. Do this in remembrance of me.'
Jesus said: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood. Do this, as often as you drink it, in remembrance of me.'
Paul warned: 'Whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty concerning the body and blood of the Lord.'
The early church 'devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers.'
Paul wrote: 'For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'eucharistia' (εὐχαριστία) means thanksgiving, from which the term 'Eucharist' derives. 'Communion' comes from 'koinonia' (κοινωνία), meaning fellowship or sharing.
The Didache provides early instructions for the Eucharist, including prayers over the cup and broken bread.

question: What does the Bible say about repentance?

scripture_says.text: Jesus began his ministry saying: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.'
Peter preached: 'Repent therefore, and turn back, that your sins may be blotted out.'
Paul declared: 'God commands all people everywhere to repent.'
John the Baptist preached 'a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins.'
Jesus said: 'There will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shub' (שׁוּב) means to turn back or return. The Greek 'metanoia' (μετάνοια) means a change of mind or heart.
Jewish tradition emphasized 'teshuvah' (repentance/return) especially during the Days of Awe between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.

question: What does the Bible say about confession of sin?

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.'
James instructed: 'Confess your sins to one another and pray for one another, that you may be healed.'
Proverbs states: 'Whoever conceals his transgressions will not prosper, but he who confesses and forsakes them will obtain mercy.'
David confessed: 'I acknowledged my sin to you, and I did not cover my iniquity; I said, I will confess my transgressions to the LORD, and you forgave the iniquity of my sin.'
The Day of Atonement included confession of sins over the scapegoat.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'yadah' (יָדָה) means to throw or cast, and by extension to confess or praise. The Greek 'homologeo' (ὁμολογέω) means to say the same thing, to agree, or to confess.
The Didache instructs: 'In the congregation you shall confess your transgressions.'

question: What does the Bible say about death?

scripture_says.text: The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.
It is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment.
God told Adam: 'You are dust, and to dust you shall return.'
Jesus said: 'I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live.'
Paul wrote: 'The last enemy to be destroyed is death.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'maveth' (מָוֶת) means death. The Greek 'thanatos' (θάνατος) carries the same meaning.
Ancient Israelites practiced burial typically the same day of death due to climate. Cremation was rare among Jews.

question: What does the Bible say about Sheol?

scripture_says.text: Jacob said: 'I shall go down to Sheol to my son, mourning.'
The Psalmist wrote: 'For you will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption.'
Ecclesiastes states: 'There is no work or thought or knowledge or wisdom in Sheol, to which you are going.'
Isaiah describes Sheol as stirred to meet the king of Babylon at his death.
Jonah said: 'Out of the belly of Sheol I cried, and you heard my voice.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Sheol' (שְׁאוֹל) refers to the realm of the dead. It appears 65 times in the Hebrew Bible. The Septuagint typically translates it as 'Hades.'
Sheol is described as below the earth, a place of darkness and silence where both righteous and wicked go at death.

question: What does the Bible say about Hades?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'The gates of Hades shall not prevail against' the church.
In the parable, the rich man in Hades 'lifted up his eyes, being in torment.'
Peter, quoting Psalm 16, said God would not abandon Christ's soul to Hades.
Death and Hades gave up the dead who were in them, and then were thrown into the lake of fire.
The risen Christ declared: 'I have the keys of Death and Hades.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'Hades' (ᾅδης) corresponds to Hebrew Sheol. In Greek mythology, Hades was the god of the underworld, but the NT uses it simply as the place of the dead.
By the intertestamental period, Jewish thought increasingly distinguished compartments in Hades for the righteous and wicked.

question: What does the Bible say about Gehenna (hell)?

scripture_says.text: Jesus warned: 'Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Rather fear him who can destroy both soul and body in Gehenna.'
Jesus said: 'If your right eye causes you to sin, tear it out and throw it away. For it is better that you lose one of your members than that your whole body be thrown into Gehenna.'
Jesus called the Pharisees 'children of Gehenna.'
James wrote: 'The tongue is set on fire by Gehenna.'
Jesus described Gehenna as a place 'where their worm does not die and the fire is not quenched.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'Gehenna' (γέεννα) comes from Hebrew 'Ge Hinnom' (גֵּי־הִנֹּם), the Valley of Hinnom south of Jerusalem where child sacrifice to Molech occurred.
By Jesus' time, Gehenna had become a metaphor for divine judgment. Later Jewish sources describe it as a place of punishment.

question: What does the Bible say about resurrection?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'I am the resurrection and the life.'
Paul wrote: 'If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins.'
Daniel prophesied: 'Many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt.'
Jesus said: 'An hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out.'
Paul described the resurrection body: 'It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'anastasis' (ἀνάστασις) means rising up or resurrection. The Hebrew 'qum' (קוּם) means to rise or stand up.
The Pharisees believed in resurrection; the Sadducees denied it. Jesus sided with the Pharisees on this issue.

question: What does the Bible say about heaven?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'In my Father's house are many rooms. If it were not so, would I have told you that I go to prepare a place for you?'
Paul wrote of being 'caught up to the third heaven' and hearing 'things that cannot be told.'
Revelation describes the new Jerusalem descending from heaven, where God will dwell with his people.
Jesus told the thief on the cross: 'Today you will be with me in paradise.'
John saw a great multitude 'standing before the throne and before the Lamb.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shamayim' (שָׁמַיִם) and Greek 'ouranos' (οὐρανός) can mean the sky, the stellar heavens, or God's dwelling place.
Jewish tradition spoke of multiple heavens (usually three or seven). Paul's 'third heaven' reflects this concept.

question: What does the Bible say about the lake of fire?

scripture_says.text: The beast and the false prophet 'were thrown alive into the lake of fire that burns with sulfur.'
The devil was 'thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur where the beast and the false prophet were.'
Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire. 'This is the second death, the lake of fire.'
Anyone whose name was not found in the book of life was thrown into the lake of fire.
The cowardly, faithless, murderers, sexually immoral, sorcerers, idolaters, and all liars will have their portion in the lake that burns with fire and sulfur.

history_records.text: The 'lake of fire' (Greek: limne tou pyros) appears only in Revelation. It is distinguished from Hades, which is thrown into it.
The imagery of burning sulfur recalls the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah.

question: What does the Bible say about eternal life?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life.'
Jesus said: 'This is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent.'
John wrote: 'God gave us eternal life, and this life is in his Son.'
Paul wrote: 'The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.'
Jesus said: 'My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. I give them eternal life, and they will never perish.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'zoe aionios' (ζωὴ αἰώνιος) means life of the age or eternal life. 'Aion' refers to an age or era.
The concept of eternal life becomes prominent in Daniel and intertestamental literature, developed further in the NT.

question: What does the Bible say about angels?

scripture_says.text: Hebrews describes angels as 'ministering spirits sent out to serve for the sake of those who are to inherit salvation.'
The Psalmist wrote: 'He will command his angels concerning you to guard you in all your ways.'
Angels appeared to announce Jesus' birth to the shepherds.
An angel rolled back the stone at Jesus' tomb.
Angels worship before God's throne crying 'Holy, holy, holy.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'malak' (מַלְאָךְ) and Greek 'angelos' (ἄγγελος) both mean messenger. They can refer to human or divine messengers depending on context.
Jewish tradition developed extensive angelology during the Second Temple period, including hierarchies and specific angel names.

question: What does the Bible say about demons?

scripture_says.text: Jesus cast out demons throughout his ministry.
James wrote: 'You believe that God is one; you do well. Even the demons believe—and shudder!'
Paul wrote that what pagans sacrifice, 'they offer to demons and not to God.'
Jesus gave the disciples authority over demons.
The demons knew Jesus' identity: 'What have you to do with us, O Son of God?'

history_records.text: The Greek 'daimonion' (δαιμόνιον) means demon or evil spirit. In Greek culture, 'daimon' could be neutral or positive, but the NT uses it negatively.
Jewish tradition identified demons with the fallen angels of Genesis 6 or with the spirits of the giants (Nephilim) who died in the flood.

question: What does the Bible say about Satan?

scripture_says.text: Jesus called Satan 'a murderer from the beginning' and 'the father of lies.'
Peter warned: 'Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour.'
Satan tempted Jesus in the wilderness for forty days.
Revelation identifies Satan as 'that ancient serpent, who is called the devil.'
Paul described Satan as 'the god of this world' who blinds unbelievers.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'satan' (שָׂטָן) means adversary or accuser. The Greek 'diabolos' (διάβολος) means slanderer or accuser.
In Job 1-2, 'the satan' appears as a member of the heavenly court who accuses Job. Later texts developed Satan as God's primary spiritual enemy.

question: What does the Bible say about spiritual warfare?

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'We do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness, against the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly places.'
Paul described the armor of God: belt of truth, breastplate of righteousness, shoes of the gospel, shield of faith, helmet of salvation, sword of the Spirit.
James wrote: 'Submit yourselves therefore to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.'
John wrote: 'He who is in you is greater than he who is in the world.'
Paul assured: 'God is faithful, and he will not let you be tempted beyond your ability.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'pale' (πάλη) in Ephesians 6:12 refers to wrestling or struggle, a hand-to-hand combat metaphor.
Jewish apocalyptic literature frequently describes cosmic battles between good and evil spiritual forces.

sources.label: War Scroll (1QM) from Dead Sea Scrolls

question: What does the Bible say about dreams and visions?

scripture_says.text: Joel prophesied: 'Your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions.'
Joseph interpreted Pharaoh's dreams about seven years of plenty and famine.
Daniel received visions of future kingdoms and the Son of Man.
God said: 'If there is a prophet among you, I the LORD make myself known to him in a vision; I speak with him in a dream.'
Joseph was warned in a dream to flee to Egypt with Mary and Jesus.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'chalom' (חֲלוֹם) means dream. 'Chizzayon' (חִזָּיוֹן) means vision. The Greek equivalents are 'onar' and 'horama.'
Dream interpretation was common in ancient Near Eastern cultures. Egypt and Babylon had professional dream interpreters.

question: What does the Bible say about miracles?

scripture_says.text: Jesus performed many signs and wonders: healing the sick, raising the dead, walking on water, feeding multitudes.
Jesus said: 'Truly, truly, I say to you, whoever believes in me will also do the works that I do; and greater works than these will he do.'
Signs and wonders accompanied the apostles' preaching.
Paul listed 'gifts of healing' and 'working of miracles' among spiritual gifts.
Jesus warned that false prophets would perform signs and wonders.

history_records.text: The Greek 'semeion' (σημεῖον) means sign; 'teras' (τέρας) means wonder; 'dynamis' (δύναμις) means power or mighty work. These three terms often appear together.
Josephus records that many claimed to be miracle workers in first-century Palestine.

question: What does the Bible say about prophecy?

scripture_says.text: Peter wrote: 'No prophecy of Scripture comes from someone's own interpretation. For no prophecy was ever produced by the will of man, but men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.'
Paul instructed: 'Do not despise prophecies, but test everything; hold fast what is good.'
Moses expressed the wish: 'Would that all the LORD's people were prophets!'
Deuteronomy gave a test for true prophecy: 'When a prophet speaks in the name of the LORD, if the word does not come to pass or come true, that is a word that the LORD has not spoken.'
Paul listed prophecy among spiritual gifts and encouraged seeking it.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'nabi' (נָבִיא) means prophet, one who speaks for another (God). The Greek 'prophetes' (προφήτης) means one who speaks forth.
After Malachi (c. 450 BC), Jews believed prophecy had ceased until the messianic age.

question: What does the Bible say about work?

scripture_says.text: God placed Adam in the garden 'to work it and keep it.'
Paul commanded: 'If anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat.'
Colossians instructs: 'Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men.'
Ecclesiastes observes: 'There is nothing better for a person than that he should eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil.'
The fourth commandment includes: 'Six days you shall labor, and do all your work.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'avodah' (עֲבֹדָה) means work, labor, or service. It is used for both secular work and worship/service to God.
In Greco-Roman culture, manual labor was often disdained. Jewish and Christian traditions valued honest work.

question: What does the Bible say about rest?

scripture_says.text: God rested on the seventh day from all his work.
Jesus said: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.'
Hebrews speaks of a Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God.
The Sabbath commandment required rest for all: family, servants, livestock, and sojourners.
Jesus withdrew to lonely places to rest and pray.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shabbat' (שָׁבַת) means to cease or rest. 'Nuach' (נוּחַ) means to rest or settle. The Greek 'anapausis' means rest or refreshment.

question: What does the Bible say about food?

scripture_says.text: God gave humans 'every plant yielding seed' and 'every tree with seed in its fruit' for food.
After the flood, God permitted eating meat: 'Every moving thing that lives shall be food for you.'
The Law distinguished clean and unclean animals.
Jesus declared all foods clean.
Paul wrote: 'The kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking but of righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.'

history_records.text: Jewish dietary laws (kashrut) prohibited pork, shellfish, and mixing meat with dairy. These distinctions shaped Jewish identity.
The Jerusalem Council decided Gentile believers should abstain from food sacrificed to idols, blood, and strangled animals.

question: What does the Bible say about wine?

scripture_says.text: Psalm 104 says God gives 'wine to gladden the heart of man.'
Proverbs warns: 'Wine is a mocker, strong drink a brawler, and whoever is led astray by it is not wise.'
Paul instructed: 'Do not get drunk with wine, for that is debauchery, but be filled with the Spirit.'
Jesus' first miracle was turning water into wine at Cana.
Paul advised Timothy: 'No longer drink only water, but use a little wine for the sake of your stomach.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'yayin' (יַיִן) and Greek 'oinos' (οἶνος) both refer to fermented grape juice. 'Tirosh' (תִּירוֹשׁ) refers to new wine or grape juice.
Wine was a staple in the ancient Mediterranean diet, often mixed with water. Drunkenness was condemned in both Jewish and Greco-Roman cultures.

question: What does the Bible say about clothing?

scripture_says.text: God made garments of skin for Adam and Eve after they sinned.
Jesus said: 'Do not be anxious about... what you will put on... Consider the lilies of the field.'
Paul instructed women to 'adorn themselves in respectable apparel, with modesty and self-control.'
Peter wrote that beauty should not come from 'outward adorning... but the hidden person of the heart.'
The Law prohibited wearing clothing of mixed fabrics and cross-dressing.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'beged' (בֶּגֶד) means garment or clothing. 'Simlah' refers to an outer garment or cloak.
Biblical clothing typically consisted of an inner tunic and outer cloak. The cloak was essential and could not be kept as security overnight.

question: What does the Bible say about health and sickness?

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'I pray that all may go well with you and that you may be in good health.'
James instructed the sick to call elders to pray and anoint with oil.
Jesus healed all manner of sickness and disease.
Paul's thorn in the flesh was not removed despite prayer.
Isaiah prophesied: 'By his wounds we are healed.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'rapha' (רָפָא) means to heal or cure. It appears in God's name 'YHWH Rapha' (the LORD who heals).
Luke, the author of Luke-Acts, is identified by Paul as 'the beloved physician.'

question: What does the Bible say about sleep?

scripture_says.text: The Psalmist wrote: 'He gives to his beloved sleep.'
Proverbs warns: 'A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest, and poverty will come upon you like a robber.'
Jesus slept during a storm on the Sea of Galilee.
David wrote: 'In peace I will both lie down and sleep; for you alone, O LORD, make me dwell in safety.'
Paul exhorted: 'Let us not sleep, as others do, but let us keep awake and be sober.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shenah' (שֵׁנָה) means sleep. Sleep is sometimes used metaphorically for death.

question: What does the Bible say about justice?

scripture_says.text: Micah declared: 'What does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?'
Isaiah records: 'Learn to do good; seek justice, correct oppression; bring justice to the fatherless, plead the widow's cause.'
Amos proclaimed: 'Let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream.'
The Law commanded: 'You shall not pervert the justice due to your poor in his lawsuit.'
Proverbs states: 'To do righteousness and justice is more acceptable to the LORD than sacrifice.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'mishpat' (מִשְׁפָּט) means justice, judgment, or ordinance. 'Tsedaqah' (צְדָקָה) means righteousness. They often appear together.
Ancient Near Eastern kings were expected to defend the poor and orphans. The Code of Hammurabi and other law codes reflect this ideal.

question: What does the Bible say about government and authority?

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God.'
Peter instructed: 'Be subject for the Lord's sake to every human institution, whether it be to the emperor as supreme, or to governors.'
Jesus said: 'Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's.'
Paul described rulers as 'God's servant for your good' who bear the sword to execute wrath on wrongdoers.
The apostles declared: 'We must obey God rather than men.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'exousia' (ἐξουσία) means authority or power. Roman emperors claimed divine authority; Christians affirmed earthly authority derives from God.
Early Christians generally obeyed Roman law but refused to worship the emperor or deny Christ, leading to persecution.

question: What does the Bible say about kings?

scripture_says.text: Israel asked Samuel for a king 'like all the nations.'
God warned through Samuel about what a king would do: take sons, daughters, fields, and servants.
The Law gave instructions for kings: not multiply horses, wives, or silver and gold; write a copy of the Law and read it all his days.
David was called 'a man after God's own heart.'
Proverbs states: 'The king's heart is a stream of water in the hand of the LORD; he turns it wherever he will.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'melek' (מֶלֶךְ) means king. Israel's monarchy began with Saul (c. 1050 BC) and divided after Solomon into Israel and Judah.
Ancient Near Eastern kings were considered divine representatives. Israel's king was subordinate to YHWH and the covenant.

question: What does the Bible say about judges?

scripture_says.text: Moses appointed judges over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.
The Lord raised up judges who saved Israel from their enemies.
The Law commanded: 'You shall appoint judges and officers in all your towns... and they shall judge the people with righteous judgment.'
Judges were warned: 'You shall not pervert justice. You shall not show partiality, and you shall not accept a bribe.'
Jesus warned: 'Judge not, that you be not judged.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shophet' (שֹׁפֵט) means judge or ruler. The period of the judges (c. 1200-1050 BC) preceded the monarchy.
The Sanhedrin was the Jewish supreme court in the Second Temple period, consisting of 71 members.

question: What does the Bible say about vengeance?

scripture_says.text: God declares: 'Vengeance is mine, I will repay.'
Paul instructed: 'Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave it to the wrath of God.'
Jesus taught: 'You have heard that it was said, An eye for an eye and a tooth for a tooth. But I say to you, Do not resist the one who is evil.'
Proverbs warns: 'Do not say, I will repay evil; wait for the LORD, and he will deliver you.'
Peter wrote of Christ: 'When he was reviled, he did not revile in return; when he suffered, he did not threaten, but continued entrusting himself to him who judges justly.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'naqam' (נָקַם) means to avenge or take vengeance. The lex talionis ('eye for eye') limited vengeance to proportional retaliation.
Cities of refuge were established for those who killed unintentionally, protecting them from blood avengers.

question: What does the Bible say about war?

scripture_says.text: Ecclesiastes states: 'There is a time for war and a time for peace.'
Jesus said: 'You will hear of wars and rumors of wars... For nation will rise against nation.'
God commanded Israel to conduct holy war against the Canaanites.
Isaiah prophesied: 'They shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks; nation shall not lift up sword against nation.'
James asked: 'What causes quarrels and what causes fights among you? Is it not this, that your passions are at war within you?'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'milchamah' (מִלְחָמָה) means war or battle. Israel's wars were often understood as 'wars of YHWH.'
The concept of 'just war' was developed by Augustine and Aquinas, drawing on biblical and classical sources.

question: What does the Bible say about punishment?

scripture_says.text: The Law established proportional punishment: 'Life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth.'
Paul wrote that governing authority 'does not bear the sword in vain. For he is the servant of God, an avenger who carries out God's wrath on the wrongdoer.'
Proverbs states: 'Whoever spares the rod hates his son, but he who loves him is diligent to discipline him.'
Hebrews teaches: 'The Lord disciplines the one he loves, and chastises every son whom he receives.'
Jesus warned of eternal punishment for the wicked.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'musar' (מוּסָר) means discipline, correction, or instruction. It can refer to parental, educational, or judicial correction.
Capital punishment under the Law applied to murder, adultery, blasphemy, Sabbath-breaking, and other offenses.

question: What does the Bible say about oppression?

scripture_says.text: God told Moses: 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people who are in Egypt and have heard their cry because of their taskmasters. I know their sufferings.'
Isaiah proclaimed: 'Learn to do good; seek justice, correct oppression; bring justice to the fatherless, plead the widow's cause.'
James warned: 'The wages of the laborers who mowed your fields, which you kept back by fraud, are crying out against you.'
Zechariah commanded: 'Do not oppress the widow, the fatherless, the sojourner, or the poor.'
The Psalmist wrote: 'The LORD works righteousness and justice for all who are oppressed.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'ashaq' (עָשַׁק) means to oppress, wrong, or extort. The prophets consistently condemned economic exploitation.
Israel's experience of slavery in Egypt shaped its laws protecting foreigners, widows, orphans, and the poor.

scripture_says.text: Genesis opens with: 'In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.'
The psalmist wrote: 'The fool says in his heart, "There is no God."'
Paul wrote that God's invisible attributes are clearly perceived in what has been made.
Hebrews states: 'Whoever would draw near to God must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew word for God, 'Elohim' (אֱלֹהִים), appears over 2,500 times in the Old Testament.

question: If God exists, what is God like?

scripture_says.text: God declared to Moses: 'I AM WHO I AM.'
The LORD proclaimed: 'The LORD, the LORD, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness.'
Isaiah records: 'Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory!'
John wrote: 'God is light, and in him is no darkness at all.'
John also wrote: 'God is love.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'YHWH' (יהוה), the divine name, derives from the verb 'to be' (hayah).

scripture_says.text: God spoke directly to Abraham, calling him by name.
God told Moses: 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people... and have heard their cry.'
David wrote: 'O LORD, you have searched me and known me! You know when I sit down and when I rise up.'
Jesus addressed God as 'Abba, Father.'
Paul wrote that the Spirit intercedes for believers 'with groanings too deep for words.'

history_records.text: The Aramaic 'Abba' (אַבָּא) is an intimate term for father, used by children addressing their fathers.

scripture_says.text: The Shema declares: 'Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one.'
God declared through Isaiah: 'I am the LORD, and there is no other, besides me there is no God.'
At creation, God said: 'Let us make man in our image, after our likeness.'
Jesus instructed baptism 'in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.'
Paul wrote: 'There is one God, the Father... and one Lord, Jesus Christ.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'echad' (אֶחָד) in Deuteronomy 6:4 means 'one' and can denote unity (as in Gen.2.24 'one flesh').

scripture_says.text: God promised through Jeremiah: 'You will seek me and find me, when you seek me with all your heart.'
Jesus said: 'And this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent.'
Paul wrote that now 'we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face.'
Jesus declared: 'No one knows the Father except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'ginosko' (γινώσκω) used in John 17:3 denotes experiential, relational knowledge, not merely intellectual awareness.

scripture_says.text: Isaiah wrote: 'Truly, you are a God who hides himself, O God of Israel, the Savior.'
Job cried: 'Oh, that I knew where I might find him, that I might come even to his seat!'
The psalmist asked: 'How long, O LORD? Will you hide yourself forever?'
Paul wrote that God 'dwells in unapproachable light, whom no one has ever seen or can see.'
Yet Moses spoke with God 'face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.'
God declared through Malachi: 'For I the LORD do not change.'
James wrote of 'the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow due to change.'
The psalmist wrote of the heavens: 'They will perish, but you will remain... but you are the same, and your years have no end.'
Genesis records that 'the LORD regretted that he had made man on the earth.'
Jonah records that 'God relented of the disaster that he had said he would do to them.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'nacham' (נָחַם) translated 'relent' or 'regret' can mean to be sorry, to comfort, or to change one's mind.

scripture_says.text: The psalmist wrote: 'Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever you had formed the earth and the world, from everlasting to everlasting you are God.'
Peter wrote: 'With the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day.'
God declared: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega... who is and who was and who is to come.'
Isaiah records God saying: 'I am God, and there is none like me, declaring the end from the beginning.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'olam' (עוֹלָם) meaning 'everlasting' or 'eternity' appears frequently in descriptions of God.

scripture_says.text: God asked Abraham: 'Is anything too hard for the LORD?'
Jeremiah prayed: 'Ah, Lord GOD! It is you who have made the heavens and the earth by your great power... Nothing is too hard for you.'
Jesus said: 'With God all things are possible.'
Job confessed: 'I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be thwarted.'
The psalmist wrote: 'Our God is in the heavens; he does all that he pleases.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'El Shaddai' (אֵל שַׁדַּי), often translated 'God Almighty,' appears 48 times in scripture.
The Greek 'pantokrator' (παντοκράτωρ) meaning 'almighty' or 'ruler of all' appears 10 times in Revelation.

scripture_says.text: The psalmist wrote: 'Great is our Lord, and abundant in power; his understanding is beyond measure.'
David declared: 'O LORD, you have searched me and known me! You know when I sit down and when I rise up; you discern my thoughts from afar.'
John wrote: 'God is greater than our heart, and he knows everything.'
Hebrews states: 'No creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account.'
Jesus said the Father knows what you need before you ask him.
David wrote: 'Where shall I go from your Spirit? Or where shall I flee from your presence? If I ascend to heaven, you are there! If I make my bed in Sheol, you are there!'
God asked through Jeremiah: 'Am I a God at hand... and not a God far away? Can a man hide himself in secret places so that I cannot see him?... Do I not fill heaven and earth?'
Solomon acknowledged at the temple dedication: 'Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you; how much less this house that I have built!'
Paul told the Athenians that God 'is actually not far from each one of us.'
Genesis records: 'The LORD saw that the wickedness of man was great... and it grieved him to his heart.'
The LORD declares his anger: 'My wrath will burn, and I will kill you with the sword.'
Zephaniah records: 'The LORD your God is in your midst... he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing.'
God declared his love: 'I have loved you with an everlasting love.'
Isaiah describes God's compassion: 'In all their affliction he was afflicted.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'racham' (רַחַם) meaning compassion is related to 'rechem' (womb), conveying deep, maternal-like emotion.

question: Does God act in the world?

scripture_says.text: Genesis records God creating the heavens and the earth.
God delivered Israel from Egypt with 'a mighty hand and an outstretched arm.'
Jesus said: 'My Father is working until now, and I am working.'
Paul wrote that God 'works all things according to the counsel of his will.'
The psalmist proclaimed: 'Our God is in the heavens; he does all that he pleases.'
God parted the Red Sea for Israel to cross on dry ground.
God sent fire from heaven on Mount Carmel at Elijah's prayer.
Daniel was delivered from the lions' den: 'My God sent his angel and shut the lions' mouths.'
An angel released Peter from prison: 'The Lord has sent his angel and rescued me.'
James wrote: 'Elijah was a man with a nature like ours, and he prayed fervently that it might not rain, and for three years and six months it did not rain.'

question: Does God cause events or allow them?

scripture_says.text: Joseph told his brothers: 'You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good.'
God told Moses: 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart.'
Isaiah records God saying: 'I form light and create darkness; I make well-being and create calamity; I am the LORD, who does all these things.'
Amos asked: 'Does disaster come to a city, unless the LORD has done it?'
Job said: 'The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD.'
God allowed Satan to test Job within limits.
The psalmist declared: 'Oh give thanks to the LORD, for he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever!'
Jesus said: 'No one is good except God alone.'
David wrote: 'Oh, taste and see that the LORD is good!'
James wrote: 'Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights.'
Nahum declared: 'The LORD is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'tov' (טוֹב) meaning good, pleasant, or beautiful, is the word used to describe God's creation ('it was good').

scripture_says.text: Abraham asked: 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just?'
Moses declared: 'The Rock, his work is perfect, for all his ways are justice. A God of faithfulness and without iniquity, just and upright is he.'
The psalmist wrote: 'Righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne.'
Paul wrote that God is 'just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus.'
Revelation records: 'Just and true are your ways, O King of the nations!'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'mishpat' (מִשְׁפָּט) meaning justice or judgment appears over 400 times in the Old Testament.

scripture_says.text: The psalmist wrote: 'Those who know your name put their trust in you, for you, O LORD, have not forsaken those who seek you.'
Proverbs instructs: 'Trust in the LORD with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding.'
Paul wrote: 'God is faithful, by whom you were called.'
Moses declared: 'Know therefore that the LORD your God is God, the faithful God who keeps covenant and steadfast love.'
Paul assured: 'If we are faithless, he remains faithful—for he cannot deny himself.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'emunah' (אֱמוּנָה) meaning faithfulness or trustworthiness, shares the same root as 'amen.'

scripture_says.text: The psalmist pleaded: 'O God, do not keep silence; do not hold your peace or be still, O God!'
After Malachi, there was a period before John the Baptist when prophetic voices ceased.
Elijah found God not in the wind, earthquake, or fire, but in 'a still small voice.'
Habakkuk complained: 'O LORD, how long shall I cry for help, and you will not hear?'
Yet God answered Habakkuk and told him to wait for the vision.

history_records.text: Jewish tradition refers to the period between Malachi and John the Baptist as a time when prophecy ceased, approximately 400 years.

question: Why does God not make himself obvious?

scripture_says.text: God told Moses: 'You cannot see my face, for man shall not see me and live.'
Isaiah wrote: 'Truly, you are a God who hides himself.'
Paul wrote that God 'dwells in unapproachable light, whom no one has ever seen or can see.'
Jesus said: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'
Hebrews states: 'Without faith it is impossible to please him, for whoever would draw near to God must believe that he exists.'
Paul wrote that God's attributes are 'clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made.'
The psalmist wrote: 'The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.'
Peter instructed: 'Always being prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you.'
Isaiah records God saying: 'Come now, let us reason together.'
Paul reasoned in the synagogues and marketplace.

question: What does it mean to believe?

scripture_says.text: Hebrews defines: 'Now faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.'
Abraham 'believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness.'
Jesus told Thomas: 'Do not disbelieve, but believe.'
James wrote: 'Even the demons believe—and shudder!'
Paul wrote: 'If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'pisteuo' (πιστεύω) means to believe, trust, or have faith in. It implies reliance, not mere intellectual assent.
The Hebrew 'aman' (אָמַן) means to confirm, support, or be faithful—the root of 'amen.'

scripture_says.text: Hebrews 11 lists those commended for faith who acted on what they believed: 'By faith Abraham obeyed... By faith Moses... By faith the walls of Jericho fell.'
Thomas declared after seeing Jesus: 'My Lord and my God!'
Luke wrote that Jesus 'presented himself alive to them after his suffering by many proofs.'
John wrote: 'That which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we looked upon and have touched with our hands... we proclaim.'
Jesus said to the healed blind man: 'Go your way; your faith has made you well.'
Isaiah records God saying: 'Come now, let us reason together.'
Paul 'reasoned with them from the Scriptures' in the synagogues.
Peter instructed believers to be 'prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you.'
Paul wrote: 'Present your bodies as a living sacrifice... which is your reasonable service.'
Yet Paul also wrote: 'The word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'logos' (λόγος) in 1 Peter 3:15, translated 'reason' or 'defense,' is the root of 'logic.'

scripture_says.text: Hebrews states: 'Faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.'
Paul wrote: 'We walk by faith, not by sight.'
Jesus said: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'
Abraham 'went out, not knowing where he was going.'
Paul wrote: 'Now we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face. Now I know in part; then I shall know fully.'

question: What if my faith is weak?

scripture_says.text: A father cried out to Jesus: 'I believe; help my unbelief!'
Jesus told Peter, who was sinking: 'O you of little faith, why did you doubt?'
Jesus said: 'If you have faith like a grain of mustard seed... nothing will be impossible for you.'
Paul wrote to welcome 'the one who is weak in faith.'
Jude instructed: 'Have mercy on those who doubt.'
James wrote: 'The one who doubts is like a wave of the sea that is driven and tossed by the wind.'
Jesus asked his disciples: 'Why are you so afraid? Have you still no faith?'
Thomas doubted the resurrection, yet Jesus appeared to him and said: 'Do not disbelieve, but believe.'
John the Baptist sent disciples to ask Jesus: 'Are you the one who is to come, or shall we look for another?'
Paul wrote: 'Whatever does not proceed from faith is sin.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'diakrino' (διακρίνω) translated 'doubt' in James 1:6 means to distinguish, discern, or waver between two judgments.

scripture_says.text: A father said to Jesus: 'I believe; help my unbelief!'
The disciples saw Jesus after the resurrection, 'and when they saw him they worshiped him, but some doubted.'
Gideon asked God for signs with the fleece, and God answered.
The psalmist wrote: 'Why are you cast down, O my soul, and why are you in turmoil within me? Hope in God.'
Job declared: 'Though he slay me, I will hope in him.'
