question: Why do some people believe and others don't?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him.'
Paul wrote: 'So then it depends not on human will or exertion, but on God, who has mercy.'
Jesus explained the parable of the sower: seed fell on different soils—some produced fruit, some did not.
John records: 'He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him. But to all who did receive him... he gave the right to become children of God.'
Paul wrote that 'the god of this world has blinded the minds of the unbelievers.'
Joshua declared: 'Choose this day whom you will serve.'
Moses set before Israel: 'I have set before you life and death, blessing and curse. Therefore choose life.'
Jesus invited: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.'
Yet Jesus also said: 'You did not choose me, but I chose you.'
Paul wrote: 'For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God.'
Paul mentioned Hymenaeus and Alexander, 'who have made shipwreck of their faith.'
Hebrews warns against 'an evil, unbelieving heart, leading you to fall away from the living God.'
Jesus said: 'No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God.'
Yet Jesus also said: 'I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.'
Paul wrote: 'He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion.'
Peter denied Jesus three times, yet Jesus restored him: 'Simon, son of John, do you love me?'
The prodigal son returned to his father, who ran to embrace him.
James wrote: 'My brothers, if anyone among you wanders from the truth and someone brings him back, let him know that whoever brings back a sinner from his wandering will save his soul from death.'
God declared through Hosea: 'Return, O Israel, to the LORD your God.'
Elijah, after defeating the prophets of Baal, fled in fear from Jezebel and asked to die.
David's psalms move between despair and trust: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?... Yet you are holy.'
Peter walked on water toward Jesus, then saw the wind and began to sink.
Paul wrote of being 'afflicted in every way... perplexed... persecuted... struck down.'
James wrote: 'Count it all joy, my brothers, when you meet trials of various kinds, for you know that the testing of your faith produces steadfastness.'

question: Is faith emotional, intellectual, or relational?

scripture_says.text: The Shema commands: 'You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might.'
Jesus added: 'Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.'
James wrote: 'You believe that God is one; you do well. Even the demons believe—and shudder!'
Jesus defined eternal life: 'That they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent.'
Paul's desire was 'that I may know him and the power of his resurrection.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'leb' (לֵב), translated 'heart,' encompasses the whole inner person: mind, will, and emotions.

scripture_says.text: James wrote: 'Faith by itself, if it does not have works, is dead.'
James asked: 'Was not Abraham our father justified by works when he offered up his son Isaac on the altar?'
Jesus said: 'If you love me, you will keep my commandments.'
Hebrews records: 'By faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go out.'
Paul spoke of 'the obedience of faith.'
A father confessed: 'I believe; help my unbelief!'
The psalmist was honest before God: 'Why are you cast down, O my soul?'
Job spoke honestly to God about his suffering and confusion.
Jeremiah complained: 'You deceived me, LORD, and I was deceived.'
Jesus said: 'Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find.'
Jude urged believers to 'contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints.'
Paul wrote of 'one Lord, one faith, one baptism.'
Yet faith is personal: 'If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart.'
Paul testified: 'I know whom I have believed.'
Hebrews states faith has historical content: 'By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God.'
Luke wrote that Jesus 'presented himself alive to them after his suffering by many proofs.'
Paul cited over 500 witnesses who saw the risen Christ.
Peter wrote: 'We did not follow cleverly devised myths... but we were eyewitnesses.'
Yet Jesus said: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'
Hebrews defines faith: 'The conviction of things not seen.'
Jesus said: 'If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me.'
Jesus told a rich young man: 'Sell all that you have and distribute to the poor... and come, follow me.'
Paul counted everything as loss 'because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord.'
Hebrews records that some 'were tortured, refusing to accept release, so that they might rise again to a better life.'
Jesus said: 'In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world.'
Jesus declared: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life.'
Paul wrote that if Christ has not been raised, 'your faith is in vain.'
Peter declared: 'We did not follow cleverly devised myths when we made known to you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were eyewitnesses of his majesty.'
John wrote: 'That which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, which we looked upon and have touched with our hands... we proclaim also to you.'

history_records.text: The Roman historian Tacitus recorded that 'Christus' was executed under Pontius Pilate during the reign of Tiberius.

question: Why Christianity and not another religion?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'
Peter declared: 'There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.'
Paul wrote: 'For there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.'
Jesus said: 'I am the door. If anyone enters by me, he will be saved.'

question: Is Jesus who Christianity claims he is?

scripture_says.text: Jesus asked his disciples: 'Who do you say that I am?' Peter answered: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.'
Jesus declared: 'I and the Father are one.'
Thomas addressed Jesus: 'My Lord and my God!'
John wrote: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.'
Jesus said: 'Before Abraham was, I am.'

history_records.text: The Jewish historian Josephus referenced Jesus as 'a wise man' and mentioned 'the tribe of Christians.'

question: Did Jesus really rise from the dead?

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'He was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures, and... he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve. Then he appeared to more than five hundred brothers at one time.'
The angel at the tomb declared: 'He is not here, for he has risen, as he said.'
Luke records that Jesus 'presented himself alive to them after his suffering by many proofs, appearing to them during forty days.'
Peter preached: 'This Jesus God raised up, and of that we all are witnesses.'
Paul wrote: 'If Christ has not been raised, then our preaching is in vain and your faith is in vain.'

history_records.text: Paul's letter to the Corinthians (c. 55 AD) contains what scholars identify as an early creed, dating within years of the crucifixion.

scripture_says.text: Luke wrote: 'It seemed good to me also, having followed all things closely for some time past, to write an orderly account for you... that you may have certainty concerning the things you have been taught.'
John wrote: 'This is the disciple who is bearing witness about these things, and who has written these things, and we know that his testimony is true.'
Peter wrote: 'We did not follow cleverly devised myths... but we were eyewitnesses of his majesty.'

history_records.text: Papias (c. 60-130 AD) recorded that Mark wrote down Peter's recollections of Jesus' words and deeds.
The earliest Gospel manuscripts (P52, P66, P75) date to the 2nd century.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness.'

Peter wrote: 'No prophecy of Scripture comes from someone's own interpretation. For no prophecy was ever produced by the will of man, but men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.'
The psalmist declared: 'The law of the LORD is perfect... the testimony of the LORD is sure.'
Jesus said: 'Scripture cannot be broken.'

history_records.text: The Dead Sea Scrolls (c. 250 BC - 70 AD) demonstrate the careful transmission of Old Testament texts.
Over 5,800 Greek New Testament manuscripts exist, more than any other ancient document.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.'
John's vision includes 'a great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages.'
Jesus commanded: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations.'
Peter declared: 'Truly I understand that God shows no partiality, but in every nation anyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him.'

history_records.text: Christianity spread across diverse cultures within decades: Jerusalem, Antioch, Rome, Alexandria, Ethiopia.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'
Peter declared: 'There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.'
Yet Jesus also said: 'Whoever is not against us is for us.'
Jesus said: 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish.'

question: Are all religions ultimately the same?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'
Paul wrote that pagans 'exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images resembling mortal man and birds and animals.'
Paul told the Athenians about the 'unknown god' they worshiped: 'What therefore you worship as unknown, this I proclaim to you.'
Elijah challenged Israel: 'How long will you go limping between two different opinions? If the LORD is God, follow him; but if Baal, then follow him.'

Jesus said: 'Enter by the narrow gate. For the gate is wide and the way is easy that leads to destruction, and those who enter by it are many. For the gate is narrow and the way is hard that leads to life, and those who find it are few.'
Jesus said: 'Strive to enter through the narrow door. For many, I tell you, will seek to enter and will not be able.'
Yet Jesus also invited: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.'
Peter wrote that God is 'not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.'
Jude urged believers to 'contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints.'
Paul warned: 'Even if we or an angel from heaven should preach to you a gospel contrary to the one we preached to you, let him be accursed.'
Jesus promised: 'Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away.'
Paul described the church as 'built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone.'

history_records.text: The Nicene Creed (325 AD) and Apostles' Creed summarize core Christian beliefs held from early centuries.

sources.label: Nicene Creed, Council of Nicaea (325 AD)

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them.'
Luke records that Jesus, beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, 'interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself.'
Paul wrote: 'All Scripture is breathed out by God.'
Peter acknowledged that some of Paul's writings contain 'things hard to understand.'
Jesus said: 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life.'
Paul wrote: 'For by grace you have been saved through faith.'
The Philippian jailer asked: 'What must I do to be saved?' Paul answered: 'Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved.'
Hebrews states: 'Without faith it is impossible to please him, for whoever would draw near to God must believe that he exists.'
The psalmist asked: 'What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?'
Paul wrote: 'We are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them.'
Isaiah records God saying: 'Everyone who is called by my name, whom I created for my glory, whom I formed and made.'
Ecclesiastes concludes: 'Fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man.'
Jeremiah records God saying: 'For I know the plans I have for you... plans for welfare and not for evil, to give you a future and a hope.'
Paul wrote: 'We know that for those who love God all things work together for good, for those who are called according to his purpose.'
God told Jeremiah: 'Before I formed you in the womb I knew you, and before you were born I consecrated you.'
The psalmist wrote: 'Your eyes saw my unformed substance; in your book were written, every one of them, the days that were formed for me.'
Genesis records: 'So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him.'
Paul wrote that believers are 'created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand.'
Ecclesiastes observes: 'He has made everything beautiful in its time. Also, he has put eternity into man's heart.'
Revelation records: 'Worthy are you, our Lord and God... for you created all things, and by your will they existed and were created.'

question: Does God have a purpose for my life?

scripture_says.text: God told Jeremiah: 'Before I formed you in the womb I knew you, and before you were born I consecrated you; I appointed you a prophet to the nations.'
Paul wrote of being 'predestined according to the purpose of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will.'
Mordecai asked Esther: 'Who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this?'
Paul wrote: 'He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion.'
Jonah fled from the LORD's call to go to Nineveh.
Yet God pursued Jonah and brought him back to fulfill his mission.
The rich young ruler 'went away sorrowful' when Jesus called him to follow.
Paul wrote: 'I press on toward the goal for the prize of the upward call of God in Christ Jesus.'
Hebrews warns: 'See to it that no one fails to obtain the grace of God.'
Paul wrote: 'We rejoice in our sufferings, knowing that suffering produces endurance, and endurance produces character, and character produces hope.'
Joseph told his brothers: 'You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good.'
James wrote: 'Count it all joy, my brothers, when you meet trials of various kinds, for you know that the testing of your faith produces steadfastness.'
Peter wrote: 'After you have suffered a little while, the God of all grace... will himself restore, confirm, strengthen, and establish you.'
Jesus said: 'I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live.'
Paul wrote: 'If in Christ we have hope in this life only, we are of all people most to be pitied.'
Paul wrote: 'For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain.'
Revelation records: 'He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more.'

question: Who am I in relation to God?

scripture_says.text: Genesis records: 'God created man in his own image.'
John wrote: 'To all who did receive him, who believed in his name, he gave the right to become children of God.'
Paul wrote: 'The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God.'
John wrote: 'See what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God; and so we are.'
Paul wrote that believers are 'fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God.'
Genesis records that suffering entered through disobedience: 'Cursed is the ground because of you; in pain you shall eat of it all the days of your life.'
Paul wrote: 'Sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men because all sinned.'
Paul wrote: 'The whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now.'
Jesus said: 'In the world you will have tribulation.'
The book of Job records a righteous man who suffered greatly, though his friends assumed he must have sinned.
Jesus said the man born blind was not blind because of sin: 'It was not that this man sinned, or his parents, but that the works of God might be displayed in him.'
The psalmist observed: 'I was envious of the arrogant when I saw the prosperity of the wicked.'
Ecclesiastes observes: 'There is a righteous man who perishes in his righteousness, and there is a wicked man who prolongs his life in his evildoing.'
God allowed Satan to test Job within limits.
Joseph told his brothers: 'You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring it about that many people should be kept alive.'
Paul wrote: 'God has consigned all to disobedience, that he may have mercy on all.'
Peter wrote that God is 'patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.'
Isaiah records God saying: 'I form light and create darkness; I make well-being and create calamity; I am the LORD, who does all these things.'
Amos asked: 'Does disaster come to a city, unless the LORD has done it?'
Lamentations states: 'Is it not from the mouth of the Most High that good and bad come?'
Yet James wrote: 'God cannot be tempted with evil, and he himself tempts no one.'
John wrote: 'God is light, and in him is no darkness at all.'
Job's friends assumed his suffering was punishment: 'Is not your evil abundant? There is no end to your iniquities.'
Yet God declared Job righteous and rebuked his friends: 'You have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has.'
Jesus said the blind man's condition was not due to sin.
Yet Hebrews also records: 'The Lord disciplines the one he loves, and chastises every son whom he receives.'
Paul wrote of those in Corinth who were sick or had died because they partook of communion unworthily.
Paul wrote: 'We rejoice in our sufferings, knowing that suffering produces endurance, and endurance produces character, and character produces hope.'

Peter wrote: 'In this you rejoice, though now for a little while, if necessary, you have been grieved by various trials, so that the tested genuineness of your faith... may be found to result in praise and glory.'
Hebrews states: 'For the moment all discipline seems painful rather than pleasant, but later it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness.'
Paul wrote: 'This light momentary affliction is preparing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison.'
Paul asked three times for his 'thorn' to be removed, but God answered: 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.'
James wrote: 'You ask and do not receive, because you ask wrongly, to spend it on your passions.'
Isaiah wrote: 'Your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you.'
The psalmist wrote: 'If I had cherished iniquity in my heart, the Lord would not have listened.'
Jesus said: 'Whatever you ask in prayer, believe that you have received it, and it will be yours.'
The psalmist wrestled: 'I was envious of the arrogant when I saw the prosperity of the wicked... until I went into the sanctuary of God; then I discerned their end.'
Jeremiah asked: 'Why does the way of the wicked prosper? Why do all who are treacherous thrive?'
The psalmist observed: 'I have seen a wicked, ruthless man, spreading himself like a green laurel tree. But he passed away, and behold, he was no more.'
Jesus taught that God 'makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust.'
Peter wrote: 'The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish.'
Jesus told a parable: the owner allowed weeds to grow with wheat until harvest, 'lest in gathering the weeds you root up the wheat along with them.'
Habakkuk complained: 'O LORD, how long shall I cry for help, and you will not hear?'
Revelation promises: 'He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore.'
The psalmist wrote: 'Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for you are with me.'
Isaiah prophesied: 'In all their affliction he was afflicted, and the angel of his presence saved them.'
Jesus promised: 'I am with you always, to the end of the age.'
God promised through Isaiah: 'When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; and through the rivers, they shall not overwhelm you.'
Paul wrote: 'Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or danger, or sword?'
John wrote: 'Sin is lawlessness.'
James wrote: 'Whoever knows the right thing to do and fails to do it, for him it is sin.'
Paul wrote: 'Whatever does not proceed from faith is sin.'
Isaiah wrote: 'All we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned—every one—to his own way.'
Paul wrote: 'All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'chata' (חָטָא) means to miss the mark or go wrong. The Greek 'hamartia' (ἁμαρτία) carries the same meaning.

question: Are humans inherently good or flawed?

scripture_says.text: Genesis records: 'God saw everything that he had made, and behold, it was very good.'
Yet after the fall, God observed: 'The intention of man's heart is evil from his youth.'
David wrote: 'Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin did my mother conceive me.'
Paul wrote: 'None is righteous, no, not one.'
Jeremiah wrote: 'The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately sick; who can understand it?'

question: Why do I do what I don't want to do?

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'I do not do the good I want to do, but the evil I do not want to do—this I keep on doing.'
Paul described an inner war: 'I see in my members another law waging war against the law of my mind and making me captive to the law of sin.'
Paul cried: 'Wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death?'
The flesh and Spirit are in conflict: 'The desires of the flesh are against the Spirit, and the desires of the Spirit are against the flesh.'
Paul wrote that Gentiles 'show that the work of the law is written on their hearts, while their conscience also bears witness.'
Malachi records: 'For I the LORD do not change.'
Jesus said: 'Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away.'
James wrote of 'the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow due to change.'
Paul wrote that Gentiles without the law 'by nature do what the law requires... They show that the work of the law is written on their hearts.'
The psalmist wrote: 'The fool says in his heart, "There is no God." They are corrupt, they do abominable deeds.'
Solomon wrote: 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.'
Micah summarized: 'He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?'
David wrote after his sin: 'For I know my transgressions, and my sin is ever before me. Against you, you only, have I sinned.'
David described unconfessed sin: 'When I kept silent, my bones wasted away through my groaning all day long.'
Hebrews speaks of 'hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience.'
John wrote: 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'syneidesis' (συνείδησις) meaning conscience appears 30 times in the New Testament.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come.'
Ezekiel records God's promise: 'I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you.'
Paul, who persecuted Christians, became an apostle.
Paul wrote: 'Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you.'
John wrote: 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.'
The psalmist declared: 'As far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us.'
God promised through Isaiah: 'Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow.'
Paul wrote: 'There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.'
Jesus proclaimed: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.'
Peter preached: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins.'
Jesus said: 'Unless you repent, you will all likewise perish.'
Paul declared that God 'commands all people everywhere to repent.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'metanoia' (μετάνοια) means a change of mind or turning around. The Hebrew 'shuv' (שׁוּב) means to return.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote that some have their 'conscience seared' as with a hot iron.
Paul spoke of a 'good conscience' and a 'clear conscience.'
Paul described Gentiles whose 'conscience also bears witness, and their conflicting thoughts accuse or even excuse them.'
Paul wrote: 'I am not aware of anything against myself, but I am not thereby acquitted. It is the Lord who judges me.'

question: What does it mean to be saved?

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God.'
Paul and Silas told the jailer: 'Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved.'
Paul wrote: 'If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved.'
Jesus said: 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'sozo' (σῴζω) means to save, rescue, deliver, or make well. It is used for both physical healing and spiritual salvation.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.'
Jesus said he came 'to seek and to save the lost.'
Paul wrote: 'Since, therefore, we have now been justified by his blood, much more shall we be saved by him from the wrath of God.'
Revelation describes the lake of fire as 'the second death.'
Paul wrote: 'All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.'
Paul wrote: 'The wages of sin is death.'
Isaiah wrote: 'Your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God.'
James wrote: 'Whoever keeps the whole law but fails in one point has become guilty of all of it.'
Jesus told the parable of workers hired at different hours who all received the same wage.
Paul addressed the question: 'Is there injustice on God's part? By no means!'
Paul wrote: 'For God has consigned all to disobedience, that he may have mercy on all.'
Abraham asked: 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just?'
Jesus said: 'Whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life.'
Paul wrote: 'Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.'
Peter declared: 'The Lord is... not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.'
The thief on the cross was promised: 'Today you will be with me in paradise.'
Paul wrote: 'This is good, and it is pleasing in the sight of God our Savior, who desires all people to be saved.'
Paul wrote: 'For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast.'
Paul wrote: 'If it is by grace, it is no longer on the basis of works; otherwise grace would no longer be grace.'
Paul wrote: 'Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more.'
Titus records that 'the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation for all people.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'charis' (χάρις) means grace, favor, or gift freely given. It emphasizes the unmerited nature of the gift.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'For by grace you have been saved through faith... not a result of works, so that no one may boast.'
Paul wrote: 'A person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ.'
Paul wrote: 'He saved us, not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to his own mercy.'
Paul wrote: 'If it is by grace, it is no longer on the basis of works; otherwise grace would no longer be grace.'
Jesus said: 'I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.'
Paul wrote: 'Neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers... nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God.'
Yet Hebrews warns: 'It is impossible, in the case of those who have once been enlightened... and then have fallen away, to restore them again to repentance.'
Peter wrote of those who 'have escaped the defilements of the world through the knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, they are again entangled in them.'
John wrote: 'They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would have continued with us.'
James wrote: 'Faith by itself, if it does not have works, is dead.'
Paul wrote that believers are 'created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand.'
Jesus said: 'By their fruits you will recognize them.'
Paul wrote: 'Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you.'
At the judgment, Jesus will say: 'I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink.'
Paul wrote: 'If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come.'
Paul wrote: 'Be transformed by the renewal of your mind.'
Paul wrote: 'We all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another.'
Peter wrote that believers 'may become partakers of the divine nature.'
Jesus told the thief on the cross: 'Today you will be with me in paradise.'
Paul wrote of those 'who are being saved.'
Paul wrote: 'Work out your own salvation with fear and trembling.'
Peter wrote: 'You are receiving the outcome of your faith, the salvation of your souls.'
Paul wrote: 'Salvation is nearer to us now than when we first believed.'

history_records.text: Theologians distinguish: justification (past, declared righteous), sanctification (present, being made holy), glorification (future, completed salvation).

scripture_says.text: Matthew records Jesus' genealogy tracing to Abraham and David.
John wrote: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God... And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us.'
Peter declared: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.'
Jesus said of himself: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life.'

history_records.text: Tacitus recorded that 'Christus' was executed under Pontius Pilate during the reign of Tiberius.
Josephus mentioned Jesus, calling him 'a wise man' and noting the tribe of Christians.

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'The Word was God.'
Thomas addressed Jesus: 'My Lord and my God!'
Jesus said: 'I and the Father are one.'
Paul wrote: 'In him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily.'
The Father said of the Son: 'Your throne, O God, is forever and ever.'
John wrote: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.'
Jesus was hungry in the wilderness.
Jesus wept at Lazarus' tomb.
Jesus was 'wearied from his journey' and sat by a well.
Hebrews records: 'We do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are.'
Paul wrote that Jesus was 'born of woman, born under the law.'

question: Why did Jesus have to die?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.'
Isaiah prophesied: 'He was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities.'
Paul wrote: 'Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures.'
Hebrews states: 'Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.'
Peter wrote: 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree.'
Jesus prayed in Gethsemane: 'My Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will.'
Jesus told his disciples: 'Was it not necessary that the Christ should suffer these things and enter into his glory?'
Peter preached that Jesus was 'delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God.'
Hebrews states: 'Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.'

question: Did Jesus know who he was?

scripture_says.text: At age twelve, Jesus said: 'Did you not know that I must be in my Father's house?'
Jesus said: 'Before Abraham was, I am.'
Jesus prayed: 'Father, glorify me in your own presence with the glory that I had with you before the world existed.'
Jesus declared: 'I and the Father are one.'
Jesus told Peter: 'On this rock I will build my church.'
Jesus said: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'
Peter asked: 'Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life.'
Jesus promised: 'I came that they may have life and have it abundantly.'
Jesus invited: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.'
Jesus declared: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.'
Jesus said: 'The Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins.'
Jesus said: 'The Father has given all judgment to the Son.'
The crowds 'were astonished at his teaching, for he was teaching them as one who had authority.'
Jesus said: 'The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.'
On the cross, Jesus said: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do.'
Jesus told the paralytic: 'Your sins are forgiven.'
Jesus said to the woman caught in adultery: 'Neither do I condemn you; go, and from now on sin no more.'
Jesus said: 'The Father... has given all judgment to the Son.'
Paul wrote that 'we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ.'
Jesus taught: 'When the Son of Man comes in his glory... he will separate people one from another.'
Peter preached that Jesus is 'the one appointed by God to be judge of the living and the dead.'
Hebrews opens: 'Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.'
God spoke to Moses 'face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.'
The prophets declared: 'Thus says the LORD.'
At Jesus' baptism, a voice from heaven said: 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.'
God spoke through prophets: 'The word of the LORD came to me.'
God spoke through dreams and visions.
God spoke through angels.
God speaks through Scripture: 'All Scripture is breathed out by God.'
God spoke ultimately through his Son.
Paul wrote: 'All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness.'

Peter wrote: 'No prophecy of Scripture comes from someone's own interpretation. For no prophecy was ever produced by the will of man, but men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.'
Jesus said: 'Scripture cannot be broken.'
David declared: 'The Spirit of the LORD speaks by me; his word is on my tongue.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'theopneustos' (θεόπνευστος) in 2 Timothy 3:16 means 'God-breathed.'

scripture_says.text: The Bereans 'received the word with all eagerness, examining the Scriptures daily.'
The psalmist prayed: 'Open my eyes, that I may behold wondrous things out of your law.'
Paul instructed Timothy: 'Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth.'
Joshua was told: 'This Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth, but you shall meditate on it day and night.'
Jesus said: 'Scripture cannot be broken.'
The psalmist wrote: 'The sum of your word is truth, and every one of your righteous rules endures forever.'
Jesus said: 'Until heaven and earth pass away, not an iota, not a dot, will pass from the Law.'
Paul wrote: 'Let God be true though every one were a liar.'
Luke references 'the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms.'
Paul wrote of 'the oracles of God' entrusted to Israel.
Peter referred to Paul's letters as 'Scripture.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew Bible contains 24 books (39 in Protestant arrangement). The Septuagint included additional books.
The Council of Carthage (397 AD) listed the 27 books of the New Testament.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'All Scripture is breathed out by God.'
Jesus responded to Satan with 'It is written.'
Jesus said: 'Scripture cannot be broken.'
The psalmist declared: 'Forever, O LORD, your word is firmly fixed in the heavens.'
James wrote: 'The prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working.'
Jesus promised: 'Whatever you ask in prayer, you will receive, if you have faith.'
John wrote: 'If we ask anything according to his will he hears us.'
Elijah prayed and fire came from heaven.
Peter was released from prison in answer to the church's prayer.

question: Why pray if God knows everything?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Your Father knows what you need before you ask him.'
Yet Jesus immediately taught: 'Pray then like this: Our Father in heaven...'
Jesus 'would withdraw to desolate places and pray.'
Paul instructed: 'Pray without ceasing.'
James wrote: 'You do not have, because you do not ask.'
The psalmist declared: 'I love the LORD, because he has heard my voice and my pleas for mercy.'
Peter wrote: 'The eyes of the Lord are on the righteous, and his ears are open to their prayer.'
John wrote: 'If we ask anything according to his will he hears us.'
God told Solomon: 'I have heard your prayer.'
The psalmist wrote: 'The LORD is near to all who call on him, to all who call on him in truth.'
Jesus said: 'You shall worship the Lord your God and him only shall you serve.'
God declared: 'I am the LORD your God... You shall have no other gods before me.'
The psalmist invited: 'Oh come, let us worship and bow down; let us kneel before the LORD, our Maker!'
In Revelation, elders and creatures continually worship before God's throne.
The elders in Revelation declare: 'Worthy are you, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power, for you created all things.'
The psalmist wrote: 'Great is the LORD, and greatly to be praised.'
David wrote: 'I will bless the LORD at all times; his praise shall continually be in my mouth.'
Jesus told the Samaritan woman: 'The Father is seeking' those who worship in spirit and truth.
Paul told the Athenians: 'The God who made the world and everything in it... is not served by human hands, as though he needed anything.'
God declared through the psalmist: 'If I were hungry, I would not tell you, for the world and its fullness are mine.'
Job asked: 'Can a man be profitable to God?'
Yet God 'is seeking' worshipers.
David wrote: 'Oh, taste and see that the LORD is good!'
Moses asked to see God's glory, and God showed him his goodness.
Paul spoke of knowing Christ and 'the power of his resurrection.'
John wrote: 'We proclaim to you what we have seen and heard, so that you also may have fellowship with us. And our fellowship is with the Father and with his Son.'
Paul prayed that believers 'may have strength to comprehend... the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge.'
The psalmist cried: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? Why are you so far from saving me?'
Isaiah wrote: 'Your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God.'
Yet God promised: 'I will never leave you nor forsake you.'
The psalmist asked: 'How long, O LORD? Will you forget me forever?'
James promised: 'Draw near to God, and he will draw near to you.'

question: What is Christianity, in simple terms?

scripture_says.text: Jesus summarized: 'You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind... You shall love your neighbor as yourself.'
Paul summarized: 'Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures... he was buried... he was raised on the third day.'
John wrote: 'God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life.'

history_records.text: The term 'Christian' (Χριστιανός) was first used in Antioch around 40-44 AD.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'For me to live is Christ.'
Jesus said: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life.'
Paul wrote: 'He is before all things, and in him all things hold together.'
Hebrews instructs: 'Looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'Christos' (Χριστός) means 'anointed one,' equivalent to Hebrew 'Messiah.'

scripture_says.text: Jesus instructed his disciples: 'You will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth.'
The church began at Pentecost in Jerusalem when the Spirit came.
Three thousand were added to the church on that day.

history_records.text: Jerusalem, in Roman Judea, was the birthplace of the Christian movement around 30-33 AD.

scripture_says.text: Luke records Jesus' ministry beginning 'in the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar.'
The church was established at Pentecost following Jesus' death, resurrection, and ascension.

history_records.text: Tiberius' fifteenth year was approximately 28-29 AD. Jesus' crucifixion is dated to approximately 30-33 AD.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness.'
Peter wrote: 'Men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.'
Jesus referred to 'the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms.'

history_records.text: The word 'Bible' comes from Greek 'biblia' (βιβλία), meaning 'books.'
The Protestant Bible contains 66 books: 39 Old Testament, 27 New Testament.

scripture_says.text: Peter wrote: 'No prophecy was ever produced by the will of man, but men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.'
Moses wrote the law.
David wrote psalms.
Paul wrote letters to churches.

history_records.text: The Bible was written over approximately 1,500 years by about 40 different authors.

sources.text: From Moses (c. 1400 BC) to John (c. 95 AD)

question: Why is the Bible divided into Old and New Testaments?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood.'
Jeremiah prophesied: 'Behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel.'
Hebrews explains: 'In speaking of a new covenant, he makes the first one obsolete.'
Paul wrote: 'Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes.'

history_records.text: The Latin 'testamentum' translates the Greek 'diatheke' (διαθήκη), meaning covenant or testament.

question: Do Christians follow the Old Testament?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them.'
Paul wrote: 'Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes.'
Paul wrote: 'You are not under law but under grace.'
The Jerusalem council did not require Gentiles to follow the whole law.
Paul wrote: 'All Scripture is... profitable for teaching.'

question: What do Christians believe about God?

scripture_says.text: The Shema declares: 'Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one.'
Jesus instructed baptism 'in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.'
John wrote: 'God is love.'
God is described as 'merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love.'

history_records.text: The Nicene Creed (325 AD) affirms belief in 'one God, the Father Almighty, maker of heaven and earth.'

question: What do Christians believe about Jesus?

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'The Word was God... And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us.'
Paul wrote: 'Christ died for our sins... was buried... was raised on the third day.'
Peter declared: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.'
Paul wrote that Jesus 'will come again.'

history_records.text: The Nicene Creed affirms Jesus as 'true God from true God... of one Being with the Father.'

question: What do Christians believe about humans?

scripture_says.text: Genesis records: 'God created man in his own image.'
The psalmist wrote: 'You have made him a little lower than the heavenly beings and crowned him with glory and honor.'
Paul wrote: 'All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.'
God's desire is 'that all should reach repentance.'
Paul wrote: 'For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God.'
John wrote: 'From his fullness we have all received, grace upon grace.'
Paul wrote: 'Where sin increased, grace abounded all the more.'
God told Paul: 'My grace is sufficient for you.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'charis' (χάρις) means favor, kindness, or a gift freely given.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.'
Jesus said he came 'to seek and to save the lost.'
Peter preached: 'There is salvation in no one else.'
Paul wrote: 'If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved.'
Jesus proclaimed: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.'
Peter preached: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins.'
Paul wrote of 'godly grief' that 'produces a repentance that leads to salvation.'
Jesus said: 'There will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'metanoia' (μετάνοια) means a change of mind. The Hebrew 'shuv' means to turn or return.

question: How does someone become a Christian?

scripture_says.text: Paul and Silas answered: 'Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved.'
Paul wrote: 'If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved.'
Peter preached: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ.'
Jesus invited: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.'
Jesus commanded: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them.'
Peter preached: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you.'
The thief on the cross was promised paradise without baptism.
Paul wrote: 'For by grace you have been saved through faith.'
Believers in Acts were baptized after believing.

history_records.text: The Didache (c. 70-100 AD) gives early instructions for baptism.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead... we too might walk in newness of life.'
Paul wrote: 'As many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ.'
Peter wrote: 'Baptism... now saves you, not as a removal of dirt from the body but as an appeal to God for a good conscience.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'baptizo' (βαπτίζω) means to dip, immerse, or wash.

scripture_says.text: Jesus taught: 'When you pray, go into your room and shut the door and pray to your Father who is in secret.'
Jesus gave the Lord's Prayer as a model: 'Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name...'
Paul instructed: 'Pray without ceasing.'
Paul wrote: 'In everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God.'
Jesus said: 'Where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them.'
Paul described the church as 'the body of Christ.'
Early believers 'devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers.'
Hebrews instructs: 'Not neglecting to meet together.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'ekklesia' (ἐκκλησία) means assembly or congregation, literally 'called out ones.'

scripture_says.text: Hebrews instructs: 'Not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another.'
Early believers met together regularly.
Paul gave instructions for when 'the whole church comes together.'
Jesus promised: 'Where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them.'

question: Why are there so many denominations?

scripture_says.text: Paul addressed divisions in Corinth: 'Each one of you says, "I follow Paul," or "I follow Apollos," or "I follow Cephas," or "I follow Christ."'
Paul asked: 'Is Christ divided?'
Jesus prayed 'that they may all be one.'
Paul urged: 'I appeal to you, brothers, that all of you agree, and that there be no divisions among you.'

history_records.text: Major historical divisions include East-West Schism (1054 AD) and Protestant Reformation (1517 AD).

question: Do all Christians believe the same things?

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote of 'one Lord, one faith, one baptism.'
Jude urged believers to 'contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints.'
Yet Paul acknowledged: 'One person esteems one day as better than another, while another esteems all days alike. Each one should be fully convinced in his own mind.'
Paul instructed: 'As for the one who is weak in faith, welcome him, but not to quarrel over opinions.'

history_records.text: The Apostles' Creed and Nicene Creed represent core beliefs shared across traditions.

scripture_says.text: Jesus promised: 'Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away.'
Peter wrote: 'The word of the Lord remains forever.'
Hebrews declares: 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever.'
Ecclesiastes observes: 'What has been is what will be... there is nothing new under the sun.'

history_records.text: Christianity has spread to every continent and remains the world's largest religion with over 2 billion adherents.

scripture_says.text: The psalmist wrote: 'The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.'
Paul wrote that God's attributes are 'clearly perceived... in the things that have been made.'
Solomon observed and studied nature: 'He spoke of trees... beasts... birds... reptiles... fish.'
Proverbs states: 'It is the glory of God to conceal things, but the glory of kings is to search things out.'

history_records.text: Many pioneers of modern science were Christians: Copernicus, Galileo, Newton, Faraday, Mendel, Pasteur.

scripture_says.text: Jesus warned of false prophets: 'Beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves.'
Jesus said: 'Not everyone who says to me, "Lord, Lord," will enter the kingdom of heaven.'
Peter warned: 'There will be false teachers among you.'
Jesus said: 'By their fruits you will recognize them.'
Jesus explained in the parable of the sower: 'Some fell on rocky ground... when tribulation or persecution arises... he falls away.'
John wrote: 'They went out from us, but they were not of us.'
Paul mentioned Demas, 'who loved this present world.'
Jesus warned: 'Because lawlessness will be increased, the love of many will grow cold.'

question: Why do people return to Christianity?

scripture_says.text: The prodigal son 'came to himself' and returned to his father.
Peter denied Jesus three times, then was restored.
James wrote: 'If anyone among you wanders from the truth and someone brings him back, let him know that whoever brings back a sinner from his wandering will save his soul.'
God declared through Jeremiah: 'Return, faithless Israel... I will not look on you in anger.'

question: How do I know if Christianity is true?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'If anyone's will is to do God's will, he will know whether the teaching is from God.'
The Bereans 'examined the Scriptures daily to see if these things were so.'
Paul wrote that the resurrection has 'many proofs.'
Peter urged: 'Be prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you.'

question: What if I believe some parts but not others?

scripture_says.text: A father cried: 'I believe; help my unbelief!'
Thomas doubted the resurrection until Jesus appeared to him.
Jesus said: 'Seek, and you will find.'
Paul acknowledged: 'Now I know in part; then I shall know fully.'
Jesus invited: 'Come and see.'
The psalmist invited: 'Oh, taste and see that the LORD is good!'
The Bereans examined the scriptures before believing.
Nicodemus came to Jesus at night with questions.
Jesus said: 'I came that they may have life and have it abundantly.'
Peter asked: 'Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life.'
Jesus promised: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.'
Paul described the hope of 'the glory of the immortal God.'
God blessed Adam and Eve, commanding them to be fruitful, multiply, fill the earth, and have dominion over every living thing.
God placed Adam in the garden to work it and keep it, commanding him not to eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
The consequence stated: 'In the day that you eat of it you shall surely die.'
After the fall, God pronounced consequences: pain in childbearing, toil in working the ground, and return to dust.
God also spoke of enmity between the serpent's seed and the woman's seed: 'He shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'berith' (בְּרִית) meaning covenant does not appear in Genesis 1-3, though the elements of covenant (parties, terms, consequences) are present.
Hosea 6:7 references Adam in covenant context: 'Like Adam they transgressed the covenant.'

scripture_says.text: God established his covenant with Noah before the flood: 'I will establish my covenant with you, and you shall come into the ark.'
After the flood, God blessed Noah and his sons, repeating the command to be fruitful and multiply.
God declared: 'I establish my covenant with you, that never again shall all flesh be cut off by the waters of the flood.'
The rainbow was given as the sign: 'This is the sign of the covenant that I make between me and you and every living creature.'
This covenant was made with Noah, his descendants, and every living creature.
God also instituted capital punishment for murder: 'Whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed.'

history_records.text: This is the first explicit use of 'berith' (בְּרִית) for covenant in scripture.

scripture_says.text: God called Abram to leave his country, promising: 'I will make of you a great nation, and I will bless you and make your name great.'
God promised the land of Canaan to Abram's offspring.
God made a formal covenant with Abram, passing between the cut pieces as a smoking fire pot and flaming torch.
God promised Abram offspring as numerous as the stars.
Circumcision was given as the sign of the covenant: 'Every male among you shall be circumcised... It shall be a sign of the covenant.'
God changed Abram's name to Abraham ('father of a multitude') and Sarai's to Sarah.
God declared this an 'everlasting covenant' to be God to Abraham and his offspring.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Abraham' (אַבְרָהָם) means 'father of a multitude.'
Ancient Near Eastern covenant ceremonies often involved cutting animals in half, with parties passing between the pieces.

scripture_says.text: God proposed the covenant at Sinai: 'If you will indeed obey my voice and keep my covenant, you shall be my treasured possession.'
The people responded: 'All that the LORD has spoken we will do.'
God spoke the Ten Commandments directly to the people.
Moses wrote down all the words of the LORD, built an altar, and threw blood on the people, saying: 'Behold the blood of the covenant.'
The covenant included detailed laws for worship, sacrifices, purity, civil matters, and social life.
Blessings for obedience and curses for disobedience were stated.
The Sabbath was given as the sign of this covenant.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'torah' (תּוֹרָה) meaning instruction or law, refers to the body of teaching given through Moses.
The covenant was written on stone tablets by God's finger.

scripture_says.text: God established an everlasting priesthood with Aaron and his sons: 'It shall be a statute forever throughout their generations.'

After Phinehas killed the Israelite and Midianite woman, God declared: 'Behold, I give to him my covenant of peace, and it shall be to him and to his descendants after him the covenant of a perpetual priesthood.'
The Levites were set apart for service to the LORD in place of the firstborn of Israel.
Malachi speaks of a 'covenant with Levi' characterized by life and peace, fear, instruction, and walking with God.
Jeremiah includes the Levitical priests in God's unbreakable covenant promises.

history_records.text: The name 'Phinehas' (פִּינְחָס) may derive from Egyptian meaning 'the Nubian' or 'bronze-colored.'
The Hebrew 'kohen' (כֹּהֵן) meaning priest appears over 700 times in the Old Testament.

scripture_says.text: Through Nathan, God promised David: 'I will raise up your offspring after you... and I will establish his kingdom.'
God declared: 'I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever.'
God promised: 'I will be to him a father, and he shall be to me a son.'
God pledged: 'Your house and your kingdom shall be made sure forever before me. Your throne shall be established forever.'
David later spoke of this as an 'everlasting covenant, ordered in all things and secure.'
The psalmist records: 'I have made a covenant with my chosen one; I have sworn to David my servant: I will establish your offspring forever.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'David' (דָּוִד) means 'beloved.'
The parallel account in Chronicles emphasizes the temple-building aspect of the promise.

scripture_says.text: Jeremiah prophesied: 'Behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah.'
This covenant would differ from Sinai: 'Not like the covenant that I made with their fathers... my covenant that they broke.'
God promised: 'I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts. And I will be their God, and they shall be my people.'
God declared: 'They shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest... For I will forgive their iniquity, and I will remember their sin no more.'
At the Last Supper, Jesus said: 'This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood.'
Hebrews quotes Jeremiah's prophecy extensively, declaring Jesus the mediator of a better covenant.

history_records.text: The Greek 'kaine diatheke' (καινὴ διαθήκη) means 'new covenant' or 'new testament.'
Ezekiel's parallel prophecy speaks of a new heart and new spirit.

scripture_says.text: The Abrahamic covenant is called everlasting: 'I will establish my covenant between me and you and your offspring after you... for an everlasting covenant.'
The Sabbath is tied to an everlasting covenant with Israel.
David spoke of God's promises as 'an everlasting covenant.'
Isaiah prophesied an everlasting covenant connected to David's sure mercies.
Ezekiel prophesied: 'I will make a covenant of peace with them. It shall be an everlasting covenant with them.'
Hebrews refers to 'the blood of the eternal covenant' in connection with Jesus' resurrection.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'olam' (עוֹלָם) meaning everlasting, perpetual, or ancient, describes the duration of these covenant promises.

scripture_says.text: The rainbow was the sign of the Noahic covenant: 'I have set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be a sign of the covenant.'
Circumcision was the sign of the Abrahamic covenant: 'You shall be circumcised in the flesh of your foreskins, and it shall be a sign of the covenant.'
The Sabbath was the sign of the Mosaic covenant: 'It is a sign forever between me and the people of Israel.'
Blood ratified the Mosaic covenant: Moses threw blood on the people saying, 'Behold the blood of the covenant.'
Jesus instituted the cup as a sign of the new covenant: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'oth' (אוֹת) meaning sign, token, or mark, is used for covenant signs throughout the Old Testament.
Paul calls circumcision 'a sign or seal' of the righteousness Abraham had by faith.

question: What were the covenant curses and blessings?

scripture_says.text: Obedience to the Mosaic covenant brought blessings: 'Blessed shall you be in the city, and blessed shall you be in the field.'
Blessings included fertility, victory over enemies, and prosperity.
Disobedience brought curses: 'Cursed shall you be in the city, and cursed shall you be in the field.'
Curses included disease, drought, defeat, exile, and oppression.
The curses and blessings were proclaimed at Mount Gerizim and Mount Ebal.
Leviticus also details covenant blessings and curses.
Paul wrote: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'qelalah' (קְלָלָה) meaning curse, and 'berakah' (בְּרָכָה) meaning blessing, are key covenant terms.
Ancient Near Eastern treaties (such as Hittite suzerainty treaties) similarly included blessing and curse sections.

question: What is the significance of the number 1?

scripture_says.text: The Shema declares: 'Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one.'
Paul wrote: 'There is one body and one Spirit... one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father of all.'
Jesus prayed for believers 'that they may all be one.'
Marriage makes two 'one flesh.'
Paul wrote: 'There is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'echad' (אֶחָד) meaning one appears over 900 times in the Old Testament.

question: What is the significance of the number 2?

scripture_says.text: The law required two or three witnesses to establish a matter: 'On the evidence of two witnesses or of three witnesses.'
Jesus sent the disciples out two by two.
Two tablets of stone contained the Ten Commandments.
Two witnesses appear in Revelation's prophecy.
Noah took animals by twos (and by sevens for clean animals).

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shenayim' (שְׁנַיִם) meaning two appears frequently in legal and ceremonial contexts.

question: What is the significance of the number 3?

scripture_says.text: Jesus rose on the third day.
Jonah was in the fish three days and three nights.
Peter denied Jesus three times.
Jesus asked Peter three times, 'Do you love me?'
The seraphim cried, 'Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts.'
Baptism is in the name of Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
Paul's benediction invokes 'The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shalosh' (שָׁלוֹשׁ) meaning three appears over 400 times in the Old Testament.

question: What is the significance of the number 4?

scripture_says.text: A river flowed out of Eden and divided into four rivers.
Ezekiel saw four living creatures, each with four faces and four wings.
Four horsemen appear in Revelation.
Four angels stand at the four corners of the earth, holding four winds.
Daniel saw four kingdoms in Nebuchadnezzar's dream.
Daniel saw four beasts rising from the sea.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'arba' (אַרְבַּע) meaning four appears over 300 times in the Old Testament.

question: What is the significance of the number 5?

scripture_says.text: The Torah consists of five books (Genesis through Deuteronomy).
David chose five smooth stones to face Goliath.
Jesus fed 5,000 men with five loaves and two fish.
Five wise and five foolish virgins appear in Jesus' parable.
The rich man had five brothers still living.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'chamesh' (חָמֵשׁ) meaning five is used throughout the Pentateuch.

question: What is the significance of the number 6?

scripture_says.text: God created the heavens and earth in six days.
Six days were given for work: 'Six days you shall labor and do all your work.'
The number of the beast is 666.
Goliath's height was six cubits and a span.
Six stone water jars were at the wedding in Cana.
The seraphim had six wings.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shesh' (שֵׁשׁ) meaning six is linked to work days in the creation narrative.

question: What is the significance of the number 7?

scripture_says.text: God rested on the seventh day and made it holy.
Israel marched around Jericho seven times on the seventh day with seven priests blowing seven trumpets.
Naaman dipped seven times in the Jordan.
The seven-branched lampstand (menorah) stood in the tabernacle.
Revelation contains seven churches, seven spirits, seven seals, seven trumpets, and seven bowls.
Peter asked if he should forgive seven times; Jesus replied, 'seventy-seven times.'
Clean animals entered the ark by sevens.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'sheba' (שֶׁבַע) meaning seven is related to 'shaba' (to swear, take an oath), as oaths were confirmed by seven.

question: What is the significance of the number 8?

scripture_says.text: Circumcision was performed on the eighth day.
Eight people were saved through the flood.
The Feast of Tabernacles had an eighth-day assembly.
David was the eighth son of Jesse.
Jesus rose on the first day of the week, one day after the seventh.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shemoneh' (שְׁמֹנֶה) meaning eight appears in purification and consecration contexts.

question: What is the significance of the number 10?

scripture_says.text: God gave Ten Commandments to Israel.
Ten plagues struck Egypt.
A tithe (tenth) was given to the LORD.
Ten virgins appear in Jesus' parable.
Ten lepers were cleansed; one returned.
The beast of Revelation has ten horns.
Daniel and his friends were tested for ten days.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'eser' (עֶשֶׂר) meaning ten appears in legal contexts and measurements.

question: What is the significance of the number 12?

scripture_says.text: Jacob had twelve sons, who became the twelve tribes of Israel.
Jesus chose twelve apostles.
Twelve baskets remained after feeding the 5,000.
Twelve stones were set up at the Jordan crossing.
The New Jerusalem has twelve gates with names of the twelve tribes and twelve foundations with names of the twelve apostles.
The woman in Revelation has a crown of twelve stars.
The tree of life bears twelve kinds of fruit.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'sheneim asar' (שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר) meaning twelve is consistently associated with Israel and governance.

question: What is the significance of the number 40?

scripture_says.text: It rained forty days and forty nights during the flood.
Moses was on the mountain forty days and forty nights receiving the law.
Israel wandered in the wilderness forty years.
The spies explored Canaan for forty days.
Elijah traveled forty days and forty nights to Horeb.
Jesus fasted forty days and forty nights in the wilderness.
Jesus appeared to his disciples over forty days after his resurrection.
Nineveh was given forty days to repent.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'arbaim' (אַרְבָּעִים) meaning forty appears in contexts of testing, probation, and transition.

question: What is the significance of the number 70?

scripture_says.text: Seventy of Israel's elders went up the mountain with Moses.
Seventy elders were appointed to help Moses.
Seventy souls of Jacob's household went to Egypt.
The Babylonian exile lasted seventy years.
Daniel's prophecy speaks of seventy weeks (seventy 'sevens').
Jesus appointed seventy (or seventy-two) and sent them out.
Jesus said to forgive seventy times seven (or seventy-seven times).

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shibim' (שִׁבְעִים) meaning seventy is seven times ten.
The Septuagint (LXX) is named for the tradition of seventy (or seventy-two) translators.

question: What is the significance of the number 666?

scripture_says.text: Revelation states: 'Let the one who has understanding calculate the number of the beast, for it is the number of a man, and his number is 666.'
The mark is required to buy or sell.
In the Old Testament, Solomon received 666 talents of gold in one year.
The descendants of Adonikam who returned from exile numbered 666.

history_records.text: Some early Greek manuscripts read 616 instead of 666 (Papyrus 115, Codex Ephraemi).
The Greek practice of gematria (assigning numerical values to letters) was known in the ancient world.

question: What is the significance of the number 144,000?

scripture_says.text: Revelation records: 'I heard the number of the sealed, 144,000, sealed from every tribe of the sons of Israel.'
Twelve thousand were sealed from each of the twelve tribes listed.
The 144,000 appear again standing with the Lamb on Mount Zion.
They are described as having the Father's name written on their foreheads.
They sing a new song that only they could learn.
They are described as 'firstfruits for God and the Lamb.'

history_records.text: The number 144,000 is 12 × 12 × 1,000 (twelve squared times one thousand).
The list of tribes in Revelation 7:5-8 differs from other biblical tribal lists, including Manasseh but omitting Dan.

question: How many animals did Noah take on the ark?

scripture_says.text: Genesis 6:19-20 records: 'Of every living thing of all flesh, you shall bring two of every sort into the ark.'
Genesis 7:2-3 records: 'Take with you seven pairs of all clean animals... and a pair of the animals that are not clean.'
Genesis 7:8-9 records: 'Of clean animals, and of animals that are not clean... two and two, male and female, went into the ark.'
After the flood, Noah sacrificed clean animals on an altar.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shib'ah shib'ah' (שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה) in Genesis 7:2 literally reads 'seven seven' and has been translated as 'seven pairs' or 'seven of each.'

question: Did Judas hang himself or fall headlong?

scripture_says.text: Matthew records: 'And throwing down the pieces of silver into the temple, he departed, and he went and hanged himself.'
Acts records Peter saying: 'This man acquired a field with the reward of his wickedness, and falling headlong he burst open in the middle and all his bowels gushed out.'
Matthew states the chief priests bought the field.
Acts states Judas acquired the field.

history_records.text: The Greek 'apēnxato' (ἀπήγξατο) in Matthew 27:5 means 'hanged himself.'
The Greek 'prēnēs genomenos' (πρηνὴς γενόμενος) in Acts 1:18 means 'falling headlong' or 'becoming prone.'

question: How many demoniacs at the Gadarenes?

scripture_says.text: Matthew records: 'Two demon-possessed men met him, coming out of the tombs.'
Mark records: 'There met him out of the tombs a man with an unclean spirit.'
Luke records: 'There met him a man from the city who had demons.'
Mark and Luke provide extended dialogue with one man; Matthew does not.

history_records.text: Matthew uses 'duo' (δύο) meaning two; Mark and Luke use singular forms.
The location is called 'Gadarenes' in some manuscripts and 'Gerasenes' or 'Gergesenes' in others.

sources.text: Textual variants in Matthew 8:28, Mark 5:1, Luke 8:26

question: How many angels at the tomb?

scripture_says.text: Matthew records: 'An angel of the Lord descended from heaven and came and rolled back the stone.'
Mark records: 'They saw a young man sitting on the right side, dressed in a white robe.'
Luke records: 'Two men stood by them in dazzling apparel.'
John records: 'She saw two angels in white, sitting where the body of Jesus had lain.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'angelos' (ἄγγελος) means messenger; 'neaniskos' (νεανίσκος) means young man.

question: When did Jesus cleanse the temple?

scripture_says.text: John places the temple cleansing at the beginning of Jesus' ministry, after the wedding at Cana.
Matthew, Mark, and Luke place the temple cleansing during the final week, after the triumphal entry.
John's account mentions oxen, sheep, and money changers; Jesus makes a whip of cords.
The Synoptics focus on the money changers and those selling doves.

history_records.text: John's Gospel is organized thematically around Jewish festivals; the Synoptics follow a more chronological narrative.

question: What were Jesus' last words on the cross?

scripture_says.text: Matthew records: 'And Jesus cried out again with a loud voice and yielded up his spirit.' The previous words were: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'
Mark records: 'And Jesus uttered a loud cry and breathed his last.' The previous words were: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'
Luke records: 'Then Jesus, calling out with a loud voice, said, "Father, into your hands I commit my spirit!" And having said this he breathed his last.'
John records: 'When Jesus had received the sour wine, he said, "It is finished," and he bowed his head and gave up his spirit.'

history_records.text: The Aramaic 'Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani' is quoted in Matthew 27:46; Mark 15:34 has 'Eloi, Eloi.'
The Greek 'tetelestai' (τετέλεσται) meaning 'it is finished' is in the perfect tense, indicating completed action.

scripture_says.text: Matthew records: 'So they took counsel and bought with them the potter's field as a burial place for strangers.'
Matthew identifies 'they' as the chief priests who received the silver from Judas.
Acts records Peter saying: 'Now this man acquired a field with the reward of his wickedness.'
Matthew calls it 'the Field of Blood' because it was purchased with blood money.
Acts explains the name 'Field of Blood' in connection with Judas's death there.

history_records.text: The Greek 'ektēsato' (ἐκτήσατο) in Acts 1:18 means 'acquired' or 'obtained.'
The Aramaic 'Akeldama' means 'Field of Blood.'

scripture_says.text: 1 Samuel records: 'Then Saul took his own sword and fell upon it.'
The text continues: 'Thus Saul died... on that day.'
An Amalekite told David: 'So I stood beside him and killed him, because I was sure that he could not live after he had fallen.'
David had the Amalekite executed for claiming to have killed the LORD's anointed.
Chronicles confirms: 'So Saul died for his breach of faith... Therefore the LORD put him to death.'

history_records.text: The Amalekite's account in 2 Samuel 1 is presented as his claim to David, not necessarily as narrative fact.

question: How old was Ahaziah when he became king?

scripture_says.text: 2 Kings records: 'Ahaziah was twenty-two years old when he began to reign.'
2 Chronicles records: 'Ahaziah was forty-two years old when he began to reign.'
His father Joram (Jehoram) died at age forty.

history_records.text: The Septuagint (LXX) and some manuscripts of Chronicles read 'twenty-two' rather than 'forty-two.'
Hebrew numerals (letters with numerical values) could be subject to copying errors.

question: Did God or Satan incite David to take the census?

scripture_says.text: 2 Samuel records: 'Again the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he incited David against them, saying, "Go, number Israel and Judah."'
1 Chronicles records: 'Then Satan stood against Israel and incited David to number Israel.'
Both accounts record David's confession: 'I have sinned greatly in that I have done this thing.'
Joab questioned the census: 'Why does my lord require this? Why should it be a cause of guilt for Israel?'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'satan' (שָׂטָן) means adversary or accuser. In 1 Chronicles 21:1 it appears without the definite article.
Job 1-2 portrays Satan acting within bounds set by God.

question: Where did Jesus give the Sermon on the Mount?

scripture_says.text: Matthew records: 'Seeing the crowds, he went up on the mountain, and when he sat down, his disciples came to him.'
Luke records: 'And he came down with them and stood on a level place.'
Matthew's sermon covers three chapters; Luke's parallel is shorter.
Both accounts include the Beatitudes, though with some differences.

history_records.text: The Greek 'oros' (ὄρος) in Matthew 5:1 means mountain or hill; 'topos pedinos' (τόπος πεδινός) in Luke 6:17 means level place or plain.

question: How long was the Egyptian sojourn?

scripture_says.text: Exodus records: 'The time that the people of Israel lived in Egypt was 430 years.'
Genesis records God telling Abraham: 'Your offspring will be sojourners in a land that is not theirs... and they will be afflicted for four hundred years.'
Stephen quoted: 'They would be enslaved and afflicted for four hundred years.'
Paul wrote: 'The law, which came 430 years afterward, does not annul a covenant previously ratified by God.'

history_records.text: The Septuagint (LXX) of Exodus 12:40 reads: 'The time that the people of Israel lived in Egypt and in the land of Canaan was 430 years.'
The Samaritan Pentateuch similarly includes Canaan in the 430 years.

question: How many years of famine did God offer David?

scripture_says.text: 2 Samuel records Gad offering: 'Shall three years of famine come to you in your land?'
1 Chronicles records: 'Either three years of famine, or three months of devastation by your foes.'
The Septuagint of 2 Samuel 24:13 reads 'three years' rather than 'seven years' found in some Hebrew manuscripts.

history_records.text: Some Hebrew manuscripts and translations of 2 Samuel 24:13 read 'seven years' while others read 'three years.'
The Septuagint, Syriac, and Arabic versions support 'three years' in 2 Samuel.

question: Did the centurion come to Jesus himself?

scripture_says.text: Matthew records: 'When he had entered Capernaum, a centurion came forward to him, appealing to him.'
Matthew continues with the centurion speaking directly to Jesus.
Luke records: 'He sent to him elders of the Jews, asking him to come and heal his servant.'
Luke adds: 'When he was not far from the house, the centurion sent friends, saying to him...'
Jesus commends the centurion's faith in both accounts.

history_records.text: Roman centurions commanded approximately 80-100 soldiers and held significant authority.
Ancient narratives sometimes attributed actions of agents to their senders.

scripture_says.text: Matthew, Luke, and John record Jesus predicting: 'Before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times.'
Mark records: 'Before the rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times.'
Mark records the rooster crowing after the first denial and again after the third.
Matthew records: 'Immediately the rooster crowed.'
Luke records: 'Immediately, while he was still speaking, the rooster crowed.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'alektorophōnia' (ἀλεκτοροφωνία) refers to the time of rooster crowing, a watch of the night (around 3 AM).
Some early manuscripts of Mark omit the first rooster crow in Mark 14:68.

question: What is Genesis 1 teaching about creation?
