scripture_says.text: Genesis 1:1 states: 'In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.'
The creation account spans six days, with God resting on the seventh day.
God spoke creation into being: 'And God said, "Let there be light," and there was light.'
Humanity was created in God's image: 'So God created man in his own image.'
God evaluated his creation as 'very good.'
The New Testament affirms: 'By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'bara' (בָּרָא) meaning 'create' is used exclusively of divine creation in scripture.
The Hebrew 'yom' (יוֹם) translated 'day' can refer to a 24-hour period, daylight hours, or an indefinite time.

question: Who are the 'sons of God' in Genesis 6?

scripture_says.text: Genesis records: 'The sons of God saw that the daughters of man were attractive. And they took as their wives any they chose.'
Their offspring were 'the mighty men who were of old, the men of renown.'
In Job, 'sons of God' appear before the LORD, with Satan among them.
Jude refers to 'angels who did not stay within their own position of authority.'
Peter mentions 'spirits in prison, because they formerly did not obey... in the days of Noah.'
Peter also mentions 'angels when they sinned' whom God cast into Tartarus.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'bene ha-elohim' (בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים) literally means 'sons of God' or 'sons of the gods.'
The Septuagint translates this as 'angels of God' in some manuscripts.
1 Enoch 6-7 (Second Temple Jewish text) interprets these as fallen angels called 'Watchers.'

question: What is the mark of Cain?

scripture_says.text: After Cain killed Abel, God said: 'The LORD put a mark on Cain, lest any who found him should attack him.'
The purpose was protective: 'If anyone kills Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold.'
Cain expressed fear: 'Whoever finds me will kill me.'
Scripture does not describe what the mark looked like.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'oth' (אוֹת) translated 'mark' means sign or token - the same word used for covenant signs.

question: Why did God harden Pharaoh's heart?

scripture_says.text: God told Moses beforehand: 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and though I multiply my signs and wonders in the land of Egypt, Pharaoh will not listen to you.'
Some texts say Pharaoh hardened his own heart: 'But Pharaoh hardened his heart this time also.'
Other texts say the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart: 'But the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh.'
Paul cites this: 'For the Scripture says to Pharaoh, "For this very purpose I have raised you up, that I might show my power in you."'
Paul continues: 'So then he has mercy on whomever he wills, and he hardens whomever he wills.'

history_records.text: Three Hebrew verbs are used: 'chazaq' (חָזַק) to strengthen, 'kabed' (כָּבֵד) to make heavy, and 'qashah' (קָשָׁה) to make hard.

question: What is the sin unto death?

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'If anyone sees his brother committing a sin not leading to death, he shall ask, and God will give him life... There is sin that leads to death; I do not say that one should pray for that.'
John continues: 'All wrongdoing is sin, but there is sin that does not lead to death.'
Earlier John wrote: 'No one born of God makes a practice of sinning.'
John also wrote: 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'hamartia pros thanaton' (ἁμαρτία πρὸς θάνατον) means 'sin toward death.'

question: What does 'baptism for the dead' mean?

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'Otherwise, what do people mean by being baptized on behalf of the dead? If the dead are not raised at all, why are people baptized on their behalf?'
This statement appears in Paul's argument for the resurrection of the dead.
Paul uses third person ('they') rather than first or second person when referring to this practice.
The broader context addresses: 'How can some of you say that there is no resurrection of the dead?'

history_records.text: The Greek 'baptizomenoi huper ton nekron' (βαπτιζόμενοι ὑπὲρ τῶν νεκρῶν) literally means 'being baptized on behalf of the dead.'
Over 40 different interpretations of this passage have been proposed throughout church history.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Every sin and blasphemy will be forgiven people, but the blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven.'
Jesus continued: 'Whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, either in this age or in the age to come.'
Mark records: 'Whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit never has forgiveness, but is guilty of an eternal sin.'
The context: Scribes were saying Jesus had 'an unclean spirit' and cast out demons by Beelzebul.

history_records.text: The Greek 'blasphemia' (βλασφημία) means slander, defamation, or irreverent speech.

question: What does 'born of water' mean in John 3?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said to Nicodemus: 'Unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God.'
Jesus continued: 'That which is born of the flesh is flesh, and that which is born of the Spirit is spirit.'
Jesus referenced the wind: 'The wind blows where it wishes... So it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit.'
Nicodemus had asked: 'How can a man be born when he is old? Can he enter a second time into his mother's womb?'
Jesus replied: 'Are you the teacher of Israel and yet you do not understand these things?'

history_records.text: Ezekiel 36:25-27 connects water cleansing with receiving a new spirit: 'I will sprinkle clean water on you... And I will put my Spirit within you.'
The Greek 'hudor' (ὕδωρ) means water; 'pneuma' (πνεῦμα) means spirit or wind.

question: What does 'keys of the kingdom' mean?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said to Peter: 'I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven, and whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven, and whatever you loose on earth shall be loosed in heaven.'
Jesus said this after Peter confessed: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.'
Similar binding and loosing authority was given to all disciples: 'Whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven.'
Jesus rebuked the lawyers: 'You have taken away the key of knowledge. You did not enter yourselves, and you hindered those who were entering.'
In Revelation, Jesus holds 'the keys of Death and Hades.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'kleis' (κλείς) means key; Isaiah 22:22 speaks of 'the key of the house of David' given to Eliakim.
Rabbinic literature uses 'binding and loosing' for authoritative teaching - forbidding and permitting.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said to Peter: 'And I tell you, you are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church.'
Peter's confession preceded this: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God.'
Jesus replied: 'Blessed are you, Simon Bar-Jonah! For flesh and blood has not revealed this to you, but my Father who is in heaven.'
Paul wrote: 'No one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ.'
Peter himself wrote of Christ: 'A living stone... the cornerstone.'
Paul describes the church 'built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'Petros' (Πέτρος) means stone or rock; 'petra' (πέτρα) means rock or bedrock. Both appear in Matthew 16:18.
The Aramaic 'Kepha' (כֵּיפָא), Peter's name, means rock and has no masculine/feminine distinction.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Truly, I say to you, this generation will not pass away until all these things take place.'
The preceding context describes the temple's destruction, signs, and the coming of the Son of Man.
Jesus also said: 'But concerning that day and hour no one knows, not even the angels of heaven, nor the Son, but the Father only.'
Jesus spoke of 'this evil and adulterous generation' seeking signs.
Jesus said judgment would come on 'this generation' for the blood of the prophets.

history_records.text: The Greek 'genea' (γενεά) can mean generation (people living at the same time), race, or kind.
The temple was destroyed by Rome in AD 70, approximately 40 years after Jesus' ministry.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'The sons of the kingdom will be thrown into the outer darkness. In that place there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.'
The king commanded: 'Bind him hand and foot and cast him into the outer darkness.'
The master said: 'Cast the worthless servant into the outer darkness.'
All three references include the phrase 'weeping and gnashing of teeth.'
Jude describes false teachers as 'wandering stars, for whom the gloom of utter darkness has been reserved forever.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'skotos to exoteron' (σκότος τὸ ἐξώτερον) means 'the outer darkness' or 'the darkness outside.'

question: What does 'the spirits in prison' mean?

scripture_says.text: Peter wrote: 'He went and proclaimed to the spirits in prison, because they formerly did not obey, when God's patience waited in the days of Noah.'
This follows the statement that Christ 'was put to death in the flesh but made alive in the spirit.'
Peter also wrote: 'God did not spare angels when they sinned, but cast them into hell and committed them to chains of gloomy darkness.'
Peter connects this to Noah: 'The Spirit of Christ in them was... predicting the sufferings of Christ.'
Peter later wrote: 'This is why the gospel was preached even to those who are dead.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'pneumasin en phulake' (πνεύμασιν ἐν φυλακῇ) means 'spirits in prison' or 'spirits in custody.'
1 Enoch describes imprisoned angels (Watchers) who sinned before the flood.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'You know what is restraining him now so that he may be revealed in his time.'
Paul continued: 'For the mystery of lawlessness is already at work. Only he who now restrains it will do so until he is out of the way.'
After the restrainer is removed: 'The lawless one will be revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will kill with the breath of his mouth.'
Paul had told them before: 'Do you not remember that when I was still with you I told you these things?'
The lawless one comes 'by the activity of Satan with all power and false signs and wonders.'

history_records.text: The Greek uses neuter 'to katechon' (τὸ κατέχον - 'what restrains') in v.6 and masculine 'ho katechon' (ὁ κατέχων - 'he who restrains') in v.7.

scripture_says.text: The book identifies itself: 'The revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave him to show to his servants the things that must soon take place.'
John was told: 'Write what you see in a book and send it to the seven churches.'
The book pronounces: 'Blessed is the one who reads aloud the words of this prophecy.'
Jesus declared: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end.'
The book concludes with the new heaven, new earth, and new Jerusalem.
A warning is given against adding to or taking from the words of this prophecy.

history_records.text: The Greek 'apokalypsis' (ἀποκάλυψις) means revelation, disclosure, or unveiling.
The book uses extensive imagery drawn from Old Testament prophets, particularly Daniel, Ezekiel, and Zechariah.
Early church tradition attributed the book to the apostle John, writing from Patmos during Domitian's reign (c. AD 95).

question: What was Abraham's prayer for Sodom?

scripture_says.text: Abraham interceded: 'Will you indeed sweep away the righteous with the wicked?'
Abraham negotiated from fifty righteous down to ten: 'Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak again but this once. Suppose ten are found there.'
The LORD agreed: 'For the sake of ten I will not destroy it.'
Abraham appealed to God's justice: 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just?'
Ten righteous were not found; Sodom was destroyed, but Lot was rescued.

question: What was Jacob's prayer at Peniel?

scripture_says.text: Jacob prayed before meeting Esau: 'O God of my father Abraham and God of my father Isaac... Deliver me, please, from the hand of my brother.'
Jacob confessed: 'I am not worthy of the least of all the deeds of steadfast love and all the faithfulness that you have shown to your servant.'
Jacob wrestled with a man until daybreak and said: 'I will not let you go unless you bless me.'
Jacob's name was changed to Israel: 'For you have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed.'
Jacob named the place Peniel, saying: 'For I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been delivered.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Peniel' (פְּנִיאֵל) means 'face of God.'

question: What was Moses' prayer at the Red Sea?

scripture_says.text: Moses told the people: 'Fear not, stand firm, and see the salvation of the LORD... The LORD will fight for you, and you have only to be silent.'
The LORD said to Moses: 'Why do you cry to me? Tell the people of Israel to go forward.'
Moses was instructed: 'Lift up your staff, and stretch out your hand over the sea and divide it.'
Moses stretched out his hand, and the LORD drove the sea back.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'yeshua' (יְשׁוּעָה) meaning salvation or deliverance appears in Moses' declaration.

question: What was Moses' prayer after the golden calf?

scripture_says.text: Moses pleaded: 'O LORD, why does your wrath burn hot against your people, whom you have brought out of the land of Egypt?'
Moses appealed: 'Remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, your servants, to whom you swore by your own self.'
The LORD relented: 'And the LORD relented from the disaster that he had spoken of bringing on his people.'
Moses offered himself: 'But now, if you will forgive their sin—but if not, please blot me out of your book that you have written.'
Moses asked to see God's glory; God proclaimed his name and character.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'nacham' (נָחַם) translated 'relented' means to be sorry, comfort, or change one's mind.

scripture_says.text: Hannah prayed in bitterness of soul, weeping: 'O LORD of hosts, if you will indeed look on the affliction of your servant and remember me.'
Hannah vowed: 'If you will give to your servant a son, then I will give him to the LORD all the days of his life.'
Eli the priest initially thought she was drunk because she was praying in her heart.
After Samuel's birth, Hannah praised: 'My heart exults in the LORD; my horn is exalted in the LORD.'
Hannah's song declares: 'The LORD kills and brings to life; he brings down to Sheol and raises up.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Channah' (חַנָּה) means grace or favor.
Mary's Magnificat in Luke 1:46-55 echoes Hannah's prayer.

question: What was David's prayer of confession?

scripture_says.text: David pleaded: 'Have mercy on me, O God, according to your steadfast love; according to your abundant mercy blot out my transgressions.'
David confessed: 'Against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight.'
David asked: 'Create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me.'
David pleaded: 'Cast me not away from your presence, and take not your Holy Spirit from me.'
David declared: 'The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise.'

history_records.text: The psalm heading states: 'A Psalm of David, when Nathan the prophet went to him, after he had gone in to Bathsheba.'

question: What was Solomon's prayer at the temple dedication?

scripture_says.text: Solomon acknowledged: 'But will God indeed dwell on the earth? Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you; how much less this house that I have built!'
Solomon asked: 'Hear the plea of your servant and of your people Israel, when they pray toward this place.'
Solomon prayed for various situations: when someone sins, in defeat, in drought, in famine, in battle, and when foreigners come to pray.
Solomon prayed for exiles: 'If they repent with all their heart... then hear in heaven... and forgive your people.'
Fire came down from heaven and consumed the offerings; the glory of the LORD filled the temple.

history_records.text: Solomon knelt with his hands spread toward heaven during this prayer.

question: What was Elijah's prayer on Carmel?

scripture_says.text: Elijah prayed: 'O LORD, God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, let it be known this day that you are God in Israel, and that I am your servant.'
Elijah continued: 'Answer me, O LORD, answer me, that this people may know that you, O LORD, are God, and that you have turned their hearts back.'
Fire from the LORD fell and consumed the burnt offering, the wood, the stones, the dust, and the water.
The people fell on their faces and said: 'The LORD, he is God; the LORD, he is God.'
Elijah then prayed for rain, bowing with his face between his knees.

history_records.text: James wrote: 'Elijah was a man with a nature like ours, and he prayed fervently that it might not rain, and for three years and six months it did not rain.'

scripture_says.text: When threatened by Assyria, Hezekiah prayed: 'O LORD, the God of Israel, enthroned above the cherubim, you are the God, you alone.'
Hezekiah asked: 'So now, O LORD our God, save us, please, from his hand, that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you, O LORD, are God alone.'
Isaiah prophesied that the LORD would defend Jerusalem; the angel of the LORD struck 185,000 Assyrians.
When told he would die, Hezekiah prayed: 'O LORD, please remember how I have walked before you in faithfulness.'
The LORD added fifteen years to Hezekiah's life.

history_records.text: The Assyrian king Sennacherib's campaign against Judah is documented in Assyrian records (the Taylor Prism).

question: What was Jonah's prayer from the fish?

scripture_says.text: Jonah prayed: 'I called out to the LORD, out of my distress, and he answered me; out of the belly of Sheol I cried, and you heard my voice.'
Jonah described his experience: 'The waters closed in over me to take my life; the deep surrounded me; weeds were wrapped about my head.'
Jonah confessed: 'Those who pay regard to vain idols forsake their hope of steadfast love.'
Jonah declared: 'But I with the voice of thanksgiving will sacrifice to you; what I have vowed I will pay. Salvation belongs to the LORD!'
The LORD spoke to the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.

history_records.text: Jesus referenced Jonah: 'Just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.'

question: What was Daniel's prayer of confession?

scripture_says.text: Daniel prayed: 'O Lord, the great and awesome God, who keeps covenant and steadfast love with those who love him and keep his commandments.'
Daniel confessed: 'We have sinned and done wrong and acted wickedly and rebelled, turning aside from your commandments.'
Daniel acknowledged: 'To us, O Lord, belongs open shame... because we have sinned against you.'
Daniel pleaded: 'O Lord, hear; O Lord, forgive. O Lord, pay attention and act. Delay not, for your own sake.'
While Daniel was praying, Gabriel appeared with the prophecy of seventy weeks.

history_records.text: Daniel prayed after reading Jeremiah's prophecy of seventy years for Jerusalem's desolation.

scripture_says.text: Nehemiah prayed: 'O LORD God of heaven, the great and awesome God who keeps covenant and steadfast love with those who love him.'
Nehemiah confessed: 'We have acted very corruptly against you and have not kept the commandments.'
Nehemiah reminded God of his promise: 'If you return to me and keep my commandments... I will gather them.'
Nehemiah asked: 'Give success to your servant today, and grant him mercy in the sight of this man.'
Nehemiah was cupbearer to King Artaxerxes, who granted him permission to rebuild Jerusalem's walls.

history_records.text: Nehemiah fasted and prayed 'for days' before approaching the king.

scripture_says.text: Jesus taught: 'Pray then like this: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name.'
'Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven.'
'Give us this day our daily bread.'
'And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors.'
'And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil.'
Luke's version is shorter and was given in response to: 'Lord, teach us to pray.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'epiousios' (ἐπιούσιος) translated 'daily' appears only here in Greek literature; its meaning is debated.
The doxology 'For yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory forever. Amen' appears in later manuscripts.

question: What was Jesus' high priestly prayer?

scripture_says.text: Jesus prayed: 'Father, the hour has come; glorify your Son that the Son may glorify you.'
Jesus defined eternal life: 'That they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent.'
Jesus prayed for his disciples: 'I am not praying for the world but for those whom you have given me.'
Jesus prayed: 'Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth.'
Jesus prayed for future believers: 'I do not ask for these only, but also for those who will believe in me through their word, that they may all be one.'

question: What was Jesus' prayer in Gethsemane?

scripture_says.text: Jesus prayed: 'My Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will.'
Jesus prayed a second time: 'My Father, if this cannot pass unless I drink it, your will be done.'
Jesus was 'sorrowful and troubled' and told his disciples: 'My soul is very sorrowful, even to death.'
Luke records: 'And being in agony he prayed more earnestly; and his sweat became like great drops of blood falling down to the ground.'
Mark records Jesus saying: 'Abba, Father, all things are possible for you. Remove this cup from me. Yet not what I will, but what you will.'

history_records.text: The Aramaic 'Abba' (אַבָּא) is an intimate address for father.
Hebrews references Jesus' prayers with tears: 'In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears.'

question: What was Jesus' prayer on the cross?

scripture_says.text: Jesus prayed: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do.'
Jesus cried out: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'
Jesus said: 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit!'
Jesus said to the thief: 'Truly, I say to you, today you will be with me in paradise.'
Jesus said: 'It is finished.'

history_records.text: The Aramaic 'Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani' quotes Psalm 22:1.
'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit' quotes Psalm 31:5.

question: What was Stephen's prayer at his death?

scripture_says.text: Stephen 'gazed into heaven and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing at the right hand of God.'
Stephen declared: 'Behold, I see the heavens opened, and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God.'
As they stoned him, Stephen called out: 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit.'
Stephen knelt and cried: 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them.'
Then Stephen 'fell asleep.'

history_records.text: Stephen was described as 'full of the Holy Spirit' when he saw this vision.
Saul (later Paul) was present, approving of Stephen's execution.

question: What was Paul's prayer for the Ephesians?

scripture_says.text: Paul prayed 'that the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory, may give you the Spirit of wisdom and of revelation in the knowledge of him.'
Paul prayed that 'the eyes of your hearts' be enlightened to know 'the hope to which he has called you.'
Paul prayed that they would know 'the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe.'
Paul later prayed 'that according to the riches of his glory he may grant you to be strengthened with power through his Spirit in your inner being.'
Paul prayed that they would 'know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge, that you may be filled with all the fullness of God.'
Daniel describes Michael as 'one of the chief princes' who came to help against the prince of Persia.
Daniel calls Michael 'your prince' and 'the great prince who has charge of your people.'
Jude refers to Michael as 'the archangel' who disputed with the devil about the body of Moses.
Revelation describes: 'Now war arose in heaven, Michael and his angels fighting against the dragon.'
Michael and his angels prevailed, and the dragon was thrown down to earth.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Mikha'el' (מִיכָאֵל) means 'Who is like God?'
Michael is the only angel called 'archangel' in Protestant canonical scripture.

scripture_says.text: Gabriel appeared to Daniel to explain a vision: 'Gabriel, make this man understand the vision.'
Gabriel came to Daniel 'in swift flight' during his prayer to give him insight.
Gabriel appeared to Zechariah announcing John the Baptist's birth: 'I am Gabriel. I stand in the presence of God.'
Gabriel was sent to Mary in Nazareth to announce Jesus' birth.
Gabriel told Mary: 'You will conceive in your womb and bear a son, and you shall call his name Jesus.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Gavri'el' (גַּבְרִיאֵל) means 'Man of God' or 'God is my strength.'

scripture_says.text: God placed cherubim east of Eden 'to guard the way to the tree of life.'
Golden cherubim were made for the mercy seat: 'The cherubim shall spread out their wings above, overshadowing the mercy seat.'
God is described as 'enthroned above the cherubim.'
Ezekiel's vision describes cherubim with four faces (man, lion, ox, eagle) and four wings.
Ezekiel saw 'the likeness of a throne' above the cherubim.
Solomon's temple had large cherubim overlaid with gold.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'keruv' (כְּרוּב), plural 'keruvim' (כְּרוּבִים), appears over 90 times in the Old Testament.

scripture_says.text: Isaiah saw 'the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up... Above him stood the seraphim.'
Each seraph 'had six wings: with two he covered his face, and with two he covered his feet, and with two he flew.'
The seraphim called to one another: 'Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory!'
A seraph flew to Isaiah with a burning coal from the altar and touched his lips, saying: 'Your guilt is taken away, and your sin atoned for.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'saraph' (שָׂרָף), plural 'seraphim,' comes from a root meaning 'to burn.'
The same Hebrew word is used for the 'fiery serpents' in Numbers 21:6 and the 'bronze serpent' in Numbers 21:8.

question: What is the 'angel of the LORD'?

scripture_says.text: The angel of the LORD found Hagar and spoke to her, and she 'called the name of the LORD who spoke to her, "You are a God of seeing."'
The angel of the LORD called to Abraham and said: 'By myself I have sworn, declares the LORD.'
The angel of the LORD appeared to Moses in a flame of fire; then 'the LORD saw that he turned aside to see.'
The angel of the LORD appeared to Gideon and 'the LORD turned to him and said...'
The angel of the LORD appeared to Manoah's wife; Manoah said: 'We shall surely die, for we have seen God.'
Jacob said of the angel who wrestled with him: 'I have seen God face to face.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'malakh YHWH' (מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה) means 'messenger of the LORD' or 'angel of the LORD.'

question: What are the 'sons of God' in Job?

scripture_says.text: Job records: 'Now there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and Satan also came among them.'
This scene is repeated: 'Again there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and Satan also came.'
God asked Job: 'Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth... when the morning stars sang together and all the sons of God shouted for joy?'
The sons of God appear in a heavenly assembly where the LORD presides.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'bene elohim' (בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים) appears in Job 1:6, 2:1, and 38:7, translated 'sons of God.'
Similar heavenly council imagery appears in 1 Kings 22:19-22 and Psalm 82:1.

scripture_says.text: At Jesus' birth: 'Suddenly there was with the angel a multitude of the heavenly host praising God.'
Micaiah said: 'I saw the LORD sitting on his throne, and all the host of heaven standing beside him.'
The psalmist wrote: 'Praise him, all his angels; praise him, all his hosts!'
God is called 'LORD of hosts' (YHWH Sabaoth) over 250 times in scripture.
Nehemiah declared: 'You are the LORD, you alone. You have made heaven, the heaven of heavens, with all their host.'
Israel was warned not to worship 'the host of heaven.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'tsava' (צָבָא) means army or host; 'tsava hashamayim' means 'host of heaven.'

question: What are thrones, dominions, principalities, powers?

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'By him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities.'
Paul wrote that Christ is 'far above all rule and authority and power and dominion.'
Paul wrote: 'We do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness.'
Paul wrote that God 'disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them' in Christ.
Paul declared that neither 'angels nor rulers' nor 'powers' can separate us from the love of God.

history_records.text: The Greek terms are: 'thronoi' (θρόνοι) thrones, 'kyriotetes' (κυριότητες) dominions, 'archai' (ἀρχαί) rulers/principalities, 'exousiai' (ἐξουσίαι) authorities/powers.

scripture_says.text: Satan appeared among the sons of God and accused Job: 'Does Job fear God for no reason?'
Satan stood against Israel and incited David to number the people.
Zechariah saw 'Joshua the high priest standing before the angel of the LORD, and Satan standing at his right hand to accuse him.'
Satan tempted Jesus in the wilderness for forty days.
Jesus said: 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven.'
Revelation identifies 'that ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, the deceiver of the whole world.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'satan' (שָׂטָן) means adversary, accuser, or opponent.
The Greek 'satanas' (σατανᾶς) is a transliteration of the Hebrew.

scripture_says.text: Jesus was 'led up by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil.'
Jesus spoke of 'the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels.'
Jesus said the devil 'was a murderer from the beginning, and does not stand in the truth, because there is no truth in him... he is a liar and the father of lies.'
Peter wrote: 'Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour.'
John wrote: 'The devil has been sinning from the beginning. The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the works of the devil.'
The devil will be 'thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'diabolos' (διάβολος) means slanderer, false accuser, or one who throws [accusations] across.

scripture_says.text: Jesus cast out demons throughout his ministry: 'He cast out the spirits with a word.'
Demons recognized Jesus: 'What have you to do with us, O Son of God? Have you come here to torment us before the time?'
Jesus gave his disciples 'authority over unclean spirits, to cast them out.'
Paul wrote: 'What pagans sacrifice they offer to demons and not to God.'
James wrote: 'Even the demons believe—and shudder!'
Demons are associated with the devil: 'the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'daimonion' (δαιμόνιον) refers to an evil spirit or demon.
The Septuagint uses 'daimonia' to translate references to pagan gods in Deuteronomy 32:17 and Psalm 106:37.

scripture_says.text: Jesus healed 'all who were oppressed by the devil.'
Mark records: 'There was in their synagogue a man with an unclean spirit. And he cried out...'
Jesus rebuked unclean spirits: 'Be silent, and come out of him!'
Jesus described an unclean spirit that leaves a person, wanders, and returns with seven other spirits more evil.
Unclean spirits and demons are used interchangeably: 'Even the unclean spirits... obey him.'
Revelation describes 'three unclean spirits like frogs' coming from the dragon, beast, and false prophet.

history_records.text: The Greek 'pneuma akatharton' (πνεῦμα ἀκάθαρτον) means 'unclean spirit.'

question: What is the serpent in Genesis?

scripture_says.text: Genesis introduces: 'Now the serpent was more crafty than any other beast of the field that the LORD God had made.'
The serpent questioned God's word: 'Did God actually say...?'
The serpent contradicted God: 'You will not surely die.'
God cursed the serpent: 'On your belly you shall go, and dust you shall eat all the days of your life.'
God declared enmity between the serpent's seed and the woman's seed: 'He shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel.'
Revelation identifies: 'That ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, the deceiver of the whole world.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'nachash' (נָחָשׁ) means serpent or snake.

scripture_says.text: Job 41 devotes an entire chapter to describing Leviathan as a powerful creature that cannot be tamed.
Leviathan is described: 'His sneezings flash forth light... Out of his mouth go flaming torches; sparks of fire leap forth.'
God asks: 'Can you draw out Leviathan with a fishhook?'
The psalmist wrote: 'There go the ships, and Leviathan, which you formed to play in it.'
Isaiah prophesied: 'In that day the LORD with his hard and great and strong sword will punish Leviathan the fleeing serpent.'
The psalmist recalled: 'You crushed the heads of Leviathan; you gave him as food for the creatures of the wilderness.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'livyathan' (לִוְיָתָן) appears 6 times in the Old Testament.
Ugaritic texts from ancient Canaan mention a similar sea creature called 'Lotan.'

scripture_says.text: God described Behemoth to Job: 'Behold, Behemoth, which I made as I made you; he eats grass like an ox.'
Behemoth's strength is described: 'His bones are tubes of bronze, his limbs like bars of iron.'
Behemoth dwells in marsh and river: 'Under the lotus plants he lies, in the shelter of the reeds and in the marsh.'
God declared: 'He is the first of the works of God; let him who made him bring near his sword!'
Its tail is described: 'He makes his tail stiff like a cedar.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'behemoth' (בְּהֵמוֹת) is the plural of 'behemah' (beast), possibly used as an intensive to mean 'great beast.'

question: What are the living creatures in Ezekiel?

scripture_says.text: Ezekiel saw 'the likeness of four living creatures. And this was their appearance: they had a human likeness.'
Each had four faces: 'The face of a man... the face of a lion... the face of an ox... the face of an eagle.'
Each had four wings: 'Two of each covered their bodies.'
Their appearance was 'like burning coals of fire, like the appearance of torches moving to and fro.'
Ezekiel later identified them: 'These were the living creatures that I saw underneath the God of Israel by the Chebar canal; and I knew that they were cherubim.'
Wheels within wheels accompanied the creatures, 'full of eyes all around.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'chayyot' (חַיּוֹת) means 'living ones' or 'living creatures.'

question: What are the living creatures in Revelation?

scripture_says.text: John saw 'around the throne, on each side of the throne... four living creatures, full of eyes in front and behind.'
'The first living creature like a lion, the second living creature like an ox, the third living creature with the face of a man, and the fourth living creature like an eagle in flight.'
Each had six wings, 'full of eyes all around and within.'
They cry day and night: 'Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord God Almighty, who was and is and is to come!'
The living creatures gave the seven bowls of wrath to the angels.
They participate in heavenly worship with the twenty-four elders.

history_records.text: The Greek 'zoa' (ζῷα) means 'living beings' or 'living creatures.'
The imagery combines elements from Isaiah 6 (seraphim with six wings) and Ezekiel 1 (four faces).

question: What is the angel of death?

scripture_says.text: The LORD struck down the firstborn of Egypt: 'At midnight the LORD struck down all the firstborn in the land of Egypt.'
The LORD said: 'I will pass through the land of Egypt that night, and I will strike all the firstborn.'
Hebrews mentions 'the Destroyer' in connection with the Passover.
The 'destroyer' appears in Exodus: 'The LORD will pass over the door and will not allow the destroyer to enter.'
An angel struck 185,000 Assyrians: 'The angel of the LORD went out and struck down 185,000 in the camp of the Assyrians.'
David saw 'the angel of the LORD standing between earth and heaven, and in his hand a drawn sword stretched out over Jerusalem.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'mashchith' (מַשְׁחִית) means 'destroyer' and appears in Exodus 12:23.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said of children: 'See that you do not despise one of these little ones. For I tell you that in heaven their angels always see the face of my Father who is in heaven.'
The psalmist wrote: 'He will command his angels concerning you to guard you in all your ways.'
Hebrews asks: 'Are they not all ministering spirits sent out to serve for the sake of those who are to inherit salvation?'
When Peter was released from prison, others said: 'It is his angel!'
An angel came to Daniel: 'O Daniel, man greatly loved, understand the words that I speak to you.'
An angel appeared to Paul during the shipwreck: 'This very night there stood before me an angel of the God to whom I belong.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'leitourgika pneumata' (λειτουργικὰ πνεύματα) in Hebrews 1:14 means 'ministering spirits.'

scripture_says.text: Genesis opens: 'In the beginning, God (Elohim) created the heavens and the earth.'
Elohim is used with singular verbs when referring to the God of Israel: 'God (Elohim) said...'
Elohim is used with plural verbs when referring to other gods: 'You shall have no other gods (elohim) before me.'
The term is also used for judges or rulers: 'You shall not revile God (elohim), nor curse a ruler of your people.'
Moses was made 'as God (elohim) to Pharaoh.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Elohim' (אֱלֹהִים) is grammatically plural but takes singular verbs when referring to Israel's God. It appears over 2,500 times.
Related to 'El' (אֵל), a generic word for god/deity, and 'Eloah' (אֱלוֹהַּ), the singular form.

scripture_says.text: God revealed his name to Moses: 'God said to Moses, "I AM WHO I AM." And he said, "Say this to the people of Israel: I AM has sent me to you."'
God declared: 'I am the LORD (YHWH). I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, as God Almighty, but by my name the LORD I did not make myself fully known to them.'
The third commandment states: 'You shall not take the name of the LORD (YHWH) your God in vain.'
YHWH is used in the compound phrase 'LORD of hosts' (YHWH Sabaoth) over 250 times.

history_records.text: The Hebrew YHWH (יהוה), the Tetragrammaton, is related to the verb 'hayah' (הָיָה) meaning 'to be.' It appears nearly 7,000 times.
Jewish tradition avoids pronouncing the name, substituting 'Adonai' (Lord) or 'HaShem' (the Name).
English Bibles typically render YHWH as 'LORD' (small capitals).

scripture_says.text: Abraham addressed God: 'O Lord GOD (Adonai YHWH), what will you give me?'
Isaiah saw 'the Lord (Adonai) sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up.'
The psalmist wrote: 'The LORD (YHWH) says to my Lord (Adonai): Sit at my right hand.'
Ezekiel repeatedly records: 'Thus says the Lord GOD (Adonai YHWH).'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Adonai' (אֲדֹנָי) means 'my Lord' or 'my Master.' It is the plural of 'adon' (lord, master) with a first-person possessive suffix.
'Adonai' is traditionally substituted when reading the Tetragrammaton (YHWH) aloud.

scripture_says.text: God appeared to Abram: 'I am God Almighty (El Shaddai); walk before me, and be blameless.'
Isaac blessed Jacob: 'God Almighty (El Shaddai) bless you and make you fruitful and multiply you.'
God said: 'I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, as God Almighty (El Shaddai).'
Job uses 'Shaddai' (Almighty) 31 times, more than any other book.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'El Shaddai' (אֵל שַׁדַּי) is traditionally translated 'God Almighty.' The etymology of 'Shaddai' is debated.
Possible derivations include 'shad' (breast), 'shadad' (to overpower), or an Akkadian word for mountain.

scripture_says.text: God proclaimed his character: 'The LORD, the LORD, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love (chesed) and faithfulness.'
The psalmist repeatedly declared: 'His steadfast love (chesed) endures forever.'
Micah asked: 'What does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness (chesed), and to walk humbly with your God?'
David praised: 'Surely goodness and mercy (chesed) shall follow me all the days of my life.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'chesed' (חֶסֶד) appears approximately 250 times, variously translated as lovingkindness, steadfast love, mercy, kindness, or loyalty.
Chesed often appears in covenant contexts, describing God's faithful love toward his people.

scripture_says.text: God is described as 'abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness (emet).'
The psalmist wrote: 'The sum of your word is truth (emet), and every one of your righteous rules endures forever.'
Zechariah commanded: 'These are the things that you shall do: Speak the truth (emet) to one another.'
The psalmist prayed: 'Lead me in your truth (emet) and teach me.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'emet' (אֱמֶת) means truth, faithfulness, or reliability. It shares the same root as 'amen' (אָמֵן).
Emet often pairs with chesed (lovingkindness) to describe God's covenant character.

scripture_says.text: The Aaronic blessing includes: 'The LORD lift up his countenance upon you and give you peace (shalom).'
Isaiah prophesied of the Messiah: 'Of the increase of his government and of peace (shalom) there will be no end.'
Isaiah wrote: 'You keep him in perfect peace (shalom shalom) whose mind is stayed on you.'
The greeting 'Go in peace (shalom)' appears throughout scripture.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shalom' (שָׁלוֹם) means peace, completeness, wholeness, welfare, or well-being. It appears over 230 times.
Shalom encompasses more than absence of conflict; it includes prosperity, health, and harmonious relationships.

scripture_says.text: Abraham 'believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness (tsedaqah).'
The psalmist wrote: 'Righteousness (tsedaqah) and justice are the foundation of your throne.'
Isaiah prophesied: 'In the LORD all the offspring of Israel shall be justified and shall glory.'
Amos commanded: 'Let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness (tsedaqah) like an ever-flowing stream.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'tsedaqah' (צְדָקָה) means righteousness, justice, or righteous acts. Related to 'tsaddiq' (righteous person).
In later Hebrew, tsedaqah came to mean charitable giving or almsgiving.

scripture_says.text: Abraham asked: 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do what is just (mishpat)?'
God declared: 'I am the LORD who practices steadfast love, justice (mishpat), and righteousness in the earth.'
Isaiah prophesied that the Servant would 'bring forth justice (mishpat) to the nations.'
Micah asked: 'What does the LORD require of you but to do justice (mishpat)?'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'mishpat' (מִשְׁפָּט) means justice, judgment, or ordinance. It appears over 400 times.
Mishpat can refer to the act of judging, a legal decision, a custom, or what is right and fair.

scripture_says.text: Moses commanded: 'And these words that I command you today shall be on your heart.'
The psalmist declared: 'Blessed is the man... his delight is in the law (torah) of the LORD, and on his law he meditates day and night.'
Psalm 119 extensively praises the torah: 'Oh how I love your law (torah)! It is my meditation all the day.'
Isaiah prophesied: 'Out of Zion shall go forth the law (torah), and the word of the LORD from Jerusalem.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'torah' (תּוֹרָה) means instruction, teaching, or law. It comes from 'yarah' (to throw, cast, or direct).
Torah refers to the five books of Moses (Pentateuch) and more broadly to God's instruction.

scripture_says.text: God told Noah: 'I will establish my covenant (berith) with you.'
God made a covenant with Abraham: 'I will establish my covenant (berith) between me and you and your offspring.'
At Sinai, Moses 'took the Book of the Covenant (berith) and read it in the hearing of the people.'
Jeremiah prophesied: 'I will make a new covenant (berith) with the house of Israel.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'berith' (בְּרִית) means covenant, treaty, or agreement. It appears nearly 290 times.
The phrase 'to cut a covenant' (karat berith) reflects ancient covenant-making ceremonies involving animal sacrifice.

scripture_says.text: God 'breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and the man became a living creature (nephesh).'
The psalmist wrote: 'Bless the LORD, O my soul (nephesh), and all that is within me.'
The Shema commands: 'You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul (nephesh).'
Leviticus states: 'For the life (nephesh) of the flesh is in the blood.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'nephesh' (נֶפֶשׁ) means soul, life, being, person, or self. It appears over 750 times.
Nephesh is used for both humans and animals (Genesis 1:20, 21, 24) as living beings.

scripture_says.text: At creation: 'The Spirit (ruach) of God was hovering over the face of the waters.'
God declared: 'My Spirit (ruach) shall not abide in man forever, for he is flesh.'
The psalmist wrote: 'When you send forth your Spirit (ruach), they are created.'
Ezekiel prophesied: 'I will put my Spirit (ruach) within you, and cause you to walk in my statutes.'
Ruach also means wind: 'God made a wind (ruach) blow over the earth.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'ruach' (רוּחַ) means spirit, wind, or breath. It appears nearly 400 times.
Context determines whether ruach refers to God's Spirit, human spirit, wind, or breath.

scripture_says.text: God saw 'that every intention of the thoughts of his heart (leb) was only evil continually.'
The Shema commands: 'You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart (leb).'
Samuel was told: 'Man looks on the outward appearance, but the LORD looks on the heart (leb).'
Solomon asked God for 'an understanding heart (leb) to judge your people.'
David prayed: 'Create in me a clean heart (leb), O God.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'leb' (לֵב) or 'lebab' (לֵבָב) means heart. It appears over 850 times.
In Hebrew thought, the heart is the center of the entire inner person: intellect, will, and emotions.

scripture_says.text: The seraphim cried: 'Holy (qadosh), holy (qadosh), holy (qadosh) is the LORD of hosts.'
God commanded: 'You shall be holy (qadosh), for I the LORD your God am holy (qadosh).'
God is called 'the Holy One (qadosh) of Israel' repeatedly in Isaiah.
The Sabbath was made holy (qadosh): 'God blessed the seventh day and made it holy.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'qadosh' (קָדוֹשׁ) means holy, sacred, or set apart. The root 'qadash' means to be set apart or consecrated.
Holiness implies separation from the common or profane and dedication to God.

scripture_says.text: Moses asked: 'Please show me your glory (kabod).'
The glory (kabod) of the LORD settled on Mount Sinai, appearing 'like a devouring fire.'
The glory (kabod) of the LORD filled the tabernacle.
The psalmist declared: 'The heavens declare the glory (kabod) of God.'
Ezekiel saw the glory (kabod) of the LORD depart from the temple.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'kabod' (כָּבוֹד) means glory, honor, or weight. From 'kabed' meaning heavy or weighty.
God's glory often appears as visible brightness, cloud, or fire.

scripture_says.text: Moses declared: 'Fear not, stand firm, and see the salvation (yeshuah) of the LORD.'
Hannah praised: 'My heart exults in the LORD... I rejoice in your salvation (yeshuah).'
The psalmist wrote: 'The LORD is my light and my salvation (yesha); whom shall I fear?'
Isaiah prophesied: 'Behold, God is my salvation (yeshuah); I will trust, and will not be afraid.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew verb 'yasha' (יָשַׁע) means to save, deliver, or rescue. Related nouns include 'yeshuah' and 'yesha' (salvation).
The name 'Joshua' (Yehoshua) and 'Jesus' (Greek form) mean 'YHWH saves.'

scripture_says.text: God declared: 'I will redeem (ga'al) you with an outstretched arm.'
Job declared: 'I know that my Redeemer (go'el) lives.'
Boaz acted as Ruth's kinsman-redeemer (go'el).
Isaiah called God: 'The Holy One of Israel is your Redeemer (go'el).'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'ga'al' (גָּאַל) means to redeem, act as kinsman, or avenge. The 'go'el' is the kinsman-redeemer.
The kinsman-redeemer had duties: redeem property (Lev. 25:25), marry a widow (Ruth 4), and avenge blood (Num. 35:19).

scripture_says.text: On the Day of Atonement: 'He shall make atonement (kaphar) for the holy place.'
The priest 'shall make atonement (kaphar) for him for his sin.'
Moses said: 'Perhaps I can make atonement (kaphar) for your sin.'
The mercy seat (kapporet) covered the ark of the covenant.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'kaphar' (כָּפַר) means to cover, make atonement, or appease. Related noun 'kapporet' is the mercy seat.
Yom Kippur (Day of Atonement) comes from 'kippur,' related to kaphar.

scripture_says.text: The people responded to curses: 'And all the people shall say, "Amen."'
Nehemiah records: 'All the assembly said "Amen" and praised the LORD.'
Jesus often said: 'Truly (amen), truly (amen), I say to you.'
Paul wrote: 'All the promises of God find their Yes in him. That is why it is through him that we utter our Amen to God.'
Jesus is called 'the Amen, the faithful and true witness.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'amen' (אָמֵן) means truly, surely, or so be it. From 'aman,' to be firm, reliable, or faithful.
Amen affirms agreement with what has been said, especially in prayer and blessing.

scripture_says.text: Many psalms begin or end with 'Praise the LORD' (hallelujah).
The Hallel psalms (113-118) are songs of praise sung at Passover.
Psalm 150 repeatedly calls all creation to praise: 'Praise the LORD!'
In Revelation, a great multitude cries: 'Hallelujah! Salvation and glory and power belong to our God.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'hallelujah' combines 'hallelu' (praise!, plural imperative of 'halal') + 'Yah' (shortened form of YHWH).
Hallelujah appears 24 times in Psalms and 4 times in Revelation (in Greek as 'allelouia').

scripture_says.text: Selah appears 71 times in the Psalms, often between sections.
Selah appears 3 times in Habakkuk's prayer-psalm.
The term appears in various contexts: after declarations, questions, and praises.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'selah' (סֶלָה) appears 74 times total. Its meaning is uncertain.
Theories include: a musical notation (pause, interlude), a call to lift up voices, or a signal to prostrate.
The Septuagint translates selah as 'diapsalma,' suggesting a musical interlude.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: We also rejoice in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received reconciliation (katallage).
Paul declared: God reconciled us to himself through Christ and gave us the ministry of reconciliation.
Paul explained: In Christ God was reconciling the world to himself.
Paul pleaded: Be reconciled to God.

history_records.text: The Greek katallage means reconciliation, exchange, or restoration of relationship.
The verb katallasso means to reconcile or restore to favor.

scripture_says.text: John opens: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God (theos), and the Word was God (theos).'
Jesus quoted Psalm 82: 'I said, you are gods (theoi).'
Paul wrote of 'the one God (theos), the Father, from whom are all things.'
Thomas confessed to Jesus: 'My Lord and my God (theos)!'

history_records.text: The Greek 'theos' (θεός) means god or deity. Used for the true God and for false gods depending on context.
Theos appears over 1,300 times in the New Testament.

scripture_says.text: Peter proclaimed: 'God has made him both Lord (kyrios) and Christ.'
Paul wrote: 'If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord (kyrios)... you will be saved.'
Every tongue will confess 'that Jesus Christ is Lord (kyrios), to the glory of God the Father.'
The Septuagint uses 'kyrios' to translate YHWH: 'The Lord (kyrios) said to my Lord (kyrios).'

history_records.text: The Greek 'kyrios' (κύριος) means lord, master, or owner. It appears over 700 times in the NT.
The Septuagint uses kyrios to translate both YHWH and Adonai.

scripture_says.text: Peter confessed: 'You are the Christ (christos), the Son of the living God.'
Andrew told Simon: 'We have found the Messiah (which means Christ).'
The woman at the well said: 'I know that Messiah is coming (he who is called Christ).'
Paul preached 'that the Christ (christos) must suffer and... rise from the dead.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'christos' (χριστός) means anointed one, equivalent to Hebrew 'mashiach' (messiah).
Kings, priests, and prophets were anointed in the Old Testament (1 Sam. 16:13, Exo. 29:7, 1 Ki. 19:16).

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'In the beginning was the Word (logos), and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.'
'And the Word (logos) became flesh and dwelt among us.'
Jesus said: 'The words (logoi) that I have spoken to you are spirit and life.'
Peter called scripture 'the word (logos) of God... which lives and abides forever.'
The rider on the white horse is called 'The Word (logos) of God.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'logos' (λόγος) means word, reason, account, or discourse. It appears over 300 times in the NT.
In Greek philosophy, logos referred to the rational principle governing the universe.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'God is spirit (pneuma), and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth.'
At Pentecost: 'They were all filled with the Holy Spirit (pneuma).'
Paul wrote: 'The fruit of the Spirit (pneuma) is love, joy, peace...'
Jesus 'breathed on them and said to them, "Receive the Holy Spirit (pneuma)."'
Pneuma also refers to the human spirit: 'The spirit (pneuma) is willing, but the flesh is weak.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'pneuma' (πνεῦμα) means spirit, wind, or breath. It appears nearly 400 times in the NT.
Context determines whether pneuma refers to God's Spirit, human spirit, angels, or wind.

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'For God so loved (agapaō) the world, that he gave his only Son.'
John declared: 'God is love (agapē).'
Paul described love: 'Love (agapē) is patient and kind; love does not envy or boast.'
Jesus commanded: 'Love (agapaō) one another as I have loved (agapaō) you.'
The greatest commandment: 'You shall love (agapaō) the Lord your God with all your heart.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'agapē' (ἀγάπη) and verb 'agapaō' (ἀγαπάω) denote self-giving, unconditional love.
Agape is rare in classical Greek but prominent in the NT, appearing over 250 times (noun and verb).

scripture_says.text: Jesus asked Peter: 'Simon, son of John, do you love (phileō) me?'
John writes of 'the disciple whom Jesus loved (phileō).'
Jesus wept at Lazarus's tomb; the Jews said: 'See how he loved (phileō) him!'
Paul wrote: 'If anyone has no love (phileō) for the Lord, let him be accursed.'
Jesus said: 'The Father himself loves (phileō) you, because you have loved me.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'phileō' (φιλέω) means to love, have affection for, or be fond of. It often implies friendship or emotional attachment.
Related words include 'philos' (friend), 'philadelphia' (brotherly love), and 'philosophia' (love of wisdom).

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'From his fullness we have all received, grace (charis) upon grace (charis).'
Paul wrote: 'By grace (charis) you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God.'
Paul described grace: 'Where sin increased, grace (charis) abounded all the more.'
Paul's letters typically begin and end with grace: 'Grace (charis) to you and peace from God our Father.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'charis' (χάρις) means grace, favor, kindness, or gift. It appears over 150 times in the NT.
Related word 'charisma' (χάρισμα) means gift of grace, used for spiritual gifts.

scripture_says.text: Hebrews defines: 'Now faith (pistis) is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.'
Paul wrote: 'We hold that one is justified by faith (pistis) apart from works of the law.'
Jesus said: 'Your faith (pistis) has saved you; go in peace.'
Paul listed: 'The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace... faithfulness (pistis).'

history_records.text: The Greek 'pistis' (πίστις) means faith, trust, belief, or faithfulness. It appears nearly 250 times in the NT.
Related verb 'pisteuō' (πιστεύω) means to believe or trust, appearing over 240 times.

scripture_says.text: Paul wrote: 'Now hope (elpis) that is seen is not hope. For who hopes for what he sees?'
Peter urged: 'Always being prepared to make a defense... for the hope (elpis) that is in you.'
Paul linked faith, hope, and love: 'So now faith, hope (elpis), and love abide, these three.'
Paul described Christ as 'our hope (elpis).'
Hebrews describes hope as 'a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'elpis' (ἐλπίς) means hope or expectation. It appears over 50 times in the NT.
Biblical hope is confident expectation based on God's promises, not wishful thinking.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness (dikaiosyne).'
Paul wrote: 'The righteousness (dikaiosyne) of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe.'
Christ is 'our righteousness (dikaiosyne) and sanctification and redemption.'
Paul sought to 'be found in him, not having a righteousness (dikaiosyne) of my own... but that which comes through faith.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'dikaiosyne' (δικαιοσύνη) means righteousness or justice. It appears over 90 times in the NT.
Related words: 'dikaios' (righteous), 'dikaioō' (to justify), 'dikaiōsis' (justification).

scripture_says.text: The angel announced: 'For unto you is born this day in the city of David a Savior (sōtēr).'
Peter declared: 'There is salvation (soteria) in no one else.'
Paul wrote: 'The gospel... is the power of God for salvation (soteria) to everyone who believes.'
Paul urged: 'Work out your own salvation (soteria) with fear and trembling.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'soteria' (σωτηρία) means salvation, deliverance, or preservation. It appears over 45 times in the NT.
Related words: 'sōtēr' (savior), 'sōzō' (to save). The name 'Jesus' (Iēsous) means 'YHWH saves.'

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'In him was life (zōē), and the life was the light of men.'
Jesus said: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life (zōē).'
Jesus promised: 'I came that they may have life (zōē) and have it abundantly.'
John defined eternal life: 'This is eternal life (zōē), that they know you the only true God.'
Paul wrote: 'The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life (zōē).'

history_records.text: The Greek 'zōē' (ζωή) means life, particularly in the sense of vitality or the living essence. It appears over 130 times in the NT.
Greek distinguishes 'zōē' (life, living) from 'bios' (manner of life, livelihood).

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'I am the way, and the truth (aletheia), and the life.'
Jesus promised: 'You will know the truth (aletheia), and the truth will set you free.'
Jesus said to Pilate: 'For this purpose I was born... to bear witness to the truth (aletheia).'
Paul urged: 'Speaking the truth (aletheia) in love, we are to grow up in every way into him.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'aletheia' (ἀλήθεια) means truth or reality. It appears over 100 times in the NT.
Etymologically, aletheia may mean 'unhiddenness' (a-letheia), that which is revealed or disclosed.

scripture_says.text: The angels sang: 'Glory (doxa) to God in the highest, and on earth peace.'
John wrote: 'We have seen his glory (doxa), glory as of the only Son from the Father.'
Jesus prayed: 'Father, glorify me in your own presence with the glory (doxa) that I had with you before the world existed.'
Paul wrote: 'All have sinned and fall short of the glory (doxa) of God.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'doxa' (δόξα) means glory, honor, praise, or splendor. It appears over 165 times in the NT.
Doxa translates the Hebrew 'kabod' (glory, weight) in the Septuagint.

scripture_says.text: John the Baptist proclaimed: 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin (hamartia) of the world!'
Paul wrote: 'All have sinned (hamartanō) and fall short of the glory of God.'
John wrote: 'If we confess our sins (hamartias), he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins.'
Paul declared: 'The wages of sin (hamartia) is death.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'hamartia' (ἁμαρτία) means sin, missing the mark, or failure. It appears over 170 times in the NT.
The root meaning is 'to miss the mark,' as in archery. Related verb 'hamartanō' means to sin.

scripture_says.text: John the Baptist preached: 'Repent (metanoeō), for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.'
Jesus began preaching: 'Repent (metanoeō) and believe in the gospel.'
Peter urged: 'Repent (metanoeō) and be baptized every one of you.'
Paul declared: 'God... commands all people everywhere to repent (metanoeō).'
Paul wrote of 'godly grief' that 'produces a repentance (metanoia) that leads to salvation.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'metanoia' (μετάνοια) means repentance, change of mind, or turning. From 'meta' (after, change) + 'nous' (mind).
The verb 'metanoeō' (μετανοέω) means to repent or change one's mind.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said: 'On this rock I will build my church (ekklesia).'
The believers in Jerusalem: 'Great fear came upon the whole church (ekklesia).'
Paul addressed 'the church (ekklesia) of God that is in Corinth.'
Paul wrote of Christ as 'the head of the church (ekklesia), his body.'
The term also refers to local assemblies: 'the churches (ekklesiai) of Galatia.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'ekklesia' (ἐκκλησία) means assembly, congregation, or church. From 'ek' (out) + 'kaleō' (to call).
In secular Greek, ekklesia referred to a citizens' assembly called together for public business.

scripture_says.text: Jesus preached: 'Repent, for the kingdom (basileia) of heaven is at hand.'
Jesus taught: 'Seek first the kingdom (basileia) of God and his righteousness.'
Jesus told Pilate: 'My kingdom (basileia) is not of this world.'
Paul wrote: 'The kingdom (basileia) of God is not a matter of eating and drinking but of righteousness and peace.'
Jesus gave many parables about the kingdom: 'The kingdom of heaven is like...'

history_records.text: The Greek 'basileia' (βασιλεία) means kingdom, reign, or royal power. It appears over 160 times in the NT.
'Kingdom of heaven' (Matthew) and 'kingdom of God' (Mark, Luke) refer to the same reality.

scripture_says.text: Mark begins: 'The beginning of the gospel (euangelion) of Jesus Christ, the Son of God.'
Jesus proclaimed: 'The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe in the gospel (euangelion).'
Paul wrote: 'The gospel... is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes.'
Paul defined the gospel: 'That Christ died for our sins... was buried... was raised on the third day.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'euangelion' (εὐαγγέλιον) means good news or gospel. From 'eu' (good) + 'angelos' (messenger).
The related verb 'euangelizō' (εὐαγγελίζω) means to proclaim good news or preach the gospel.

scripture_says.text: Jesus 'called his disciples and chose from them twelve, whom he named apostles (apostoloi).'
Jesus said: 'An apostle (apostolos) is not greater than the one who sent him.'
Paul defended his apostleship: 'Am I not an apostle (apostolos)? Have I not seen Jesus our Lord?'
The church is 'built on the foundation of the apostles (apostoloi) and prophets.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'apostolos' (ἀπόστολος) means one sent, messenger, or apostle. From 'apostello' (to send forth).
Beyond the Twelve, others are called apostles: Paul, Barnabas, James the Lord's brother, and others.

scripture_says.text: Jesus called: 'Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.' They became his disciples (mathētai).
Jesus said: 'A disciple (mathētēs) is not above his teacher.'
Jesus commissioned: 'Go therefore and make disciples (mathēteusate) of all nations.'
In Antioch 'the disciples (mathētai) were first called Christians.'
Jesus said: 'By this all people will know that you are my disciples (mathētai), if you have love for one another.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'mathētēs' (μαθητής) means learner, pupil, or disciple. From 'manthanō' (to learn).
Mathētēs appears over 260 times in the Gospels and Acts, never in the Epistles (which use other terms).

scripture_says.text: Jesus promised: 'I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper (paraklētos), to be with you forever.'
Jesus said: 'The Helper (paraklētos), the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, he will teach you all things.'
Jesus explained: 'When the Helper (paraklētos) comes, whom I will send to you from the Father... he will bear witness about me.'
John wrote: 'If anyone does sin, we have an advocate (paraklētos) with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'paraklētos' (παράκλητος) means helper, advocate, counselor, or comforter. From 'para' (alongside) + 'kaleō' (to call).
In legal contexts, paraklētos referred to one called alongside to help, especially in court.

scripture_says.text: John wrote: 'He is the propitiation (hilasmos) for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world.'
John wrote: 'In this is love... that he... sent his Son to be the propitiation (hilasmos) for our sins.'
Paul wrote that God put Christ forward 'as a propitiation (hilastērion) by his blood.'
Hebrews describes Christ making 'propitiation (hilaskesthai) for the sins of the people.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'hilasmos' (ἱλασμός) means propitiation, atoning sacrifice, or means of forgiveness.
Related term 'hilastērion' (ἱλαστήριον) is used for the mercy seat in Hebrews 9:5.

question: What is the parable of the sower?

scripture_says.text: Jesus told a parable of a sower who went out to sow seed. Some fell on the path and birds ate it; some on rocky ground where it sprang up quickly but withered; some among thorns which choked it; and some on good soil, yielding thirty, sixty, and a hundredfold.

Jesus explained the parable: the seed is the word of God. The path represents those from whom Satan takes it; rocky ground those who receive with joy but fall away; thorns those choked by cares and riches; good soil those who hear and bear fruit.

history_records.text: This parable appears in all three Synoptic Gospels with nearly identical wording, suggesting it was widely remembered in the early church.

question: What is the parable of the wheat and tares?

scripture_says.text: Jesus told of a man who sowed good seed in his field, but while men slept, an enemy sowed tares among the wheat. When both grew together, servants asked whether to pull the tares, but the master said to let both grow until harvest, when tares would be gathered and burned, and wheat gathered into the barn.

Jesus explained: the sower of good seed is the Son of Man, the field is the world, good seeds are sons of the kingdom, tares are sons of the evil one, the enemy is the devil, harvest is the end of the age, and reapers are angels.

history_records.text: The Greek word 'zizania' (tares) refers to darnel, a weed that closely resembles wheat until the heads appear, making early separation difficult.

question: What is the parable of the mustard seed?

scripture_says.text: Jesus compared the kingdom of heaven to a mustard seed, which is the smallest of all seeds, but when it grows, it becomes the largest of garden plants and becomes a tree, so that birds of the air come and nest in its branches.

history_records.text: The black mustard plant (Sinapis nigra) commonly grows in Israel and can reach heights of 8-12 feet, large enough for birds to perch in its branches.

question: What is the parable of the leaven?

scripture_says.text: Jesus compared the kingdom of heaven to leaven that a woman took and hid in three measures of flour until the whole batch was leavened.

history_records.text: Three measures (Greek 'sata tria') equals approximately one ephah or about 40 liters of flour - enough bread for about 100 people, suggesting the expansive nature of the kingdom.

question: What is the parable of the hidden treasure?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said the kingdom of heaven is like treasure hidden in a field. When a man found it, he hid it again and in his joy went and sold all he had and bought that field.

history_records.text: Burying treasure was common in ancient Palestine due to lack of banks and frequent invasions. Roman law held that treasure belonged to the landowner.

question: What is the parable of the pearl of great price?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said the kingdom of heaven is like a merchant seeking beautiful pearls. When he found one pearl of great value, he went and sold all that he had and bought it.

history_records.text: Pearls were the most valued gems in the ancient world. The Greek word 'margarites' specifically denotes a pearl. Pearl merchants traveled extensively to the Persian Gulf and Red Sea.

question: What is the parable of the dragnet?

scripture_says.text: Jesus compared the kingdom of heaven to a dragnet cast into the sea that gathered fish of every kind. When full, fishermen drew it ashore, sat down, and sorted the good into vessels but threw the bad away. So it will be at the end of the age: angels will separate the wicked from the righteous.

history_records.text: The Greek 'sagene' refers to a large seine net, sometimes hundreds of feet long, which was dragged through the water by boats or pulled from shore.

question: What is the parable of the unmerciful servant?

scripture_says.text: A king wished to settle accounts. A servant owing ten thousand talents could not pay, so the master ordered him sold. The servant begged for patience and the master forgave the debt. But that servant found a fellow servant owing him a hundred denarii, seized him, and demanded payment. When the fellow servant begged for patience, he refused and had him imprisoned.

When the master heard, he summoned the wicked servant and said, 'Should you not have had mercy on your fellow servant as I had mercy on you?' In anger he delivered him to the jailers until he should pay all his debt.

history_records.text: Ten thousand talents was an astronomical sum - the annual tax revenue of all Judea, Samaria, and Idumea was only 600 talents. A hundred denarii was about 100 days' wages for a laborer.

question: What is the parable of the laborers in the vineyard?

scripture_says.text: A landowner hired laborers early in the morning for a denarius a day. He hired more at the third, sixth, ninth, and eleventh hours. At evening, he paid all workers the same - one denarius. Those hired first complained, but the master said he did them no wrong since they agreed to a denarius. The last will be first, and first last.

history_records.text: A denarius was the standard daily wage for a laborer in first-century Palestine. The workday was reckoned from sunrise (about 6 AM) to sunset.

question: What is the parable of the two sons?

scripture_says.text: A man had two sons. He told the first to go work in the vineyard. The son said 'I will not' but afterward regretted it and went. The father told the second son, who said 'I go, sir' but did not go. Jesus asked which did the father's will. They answered, 'The first.' Jesus said tax collectors and prostitutes enter the kingdom before the chief priests and elders.

history_records.text: The Greek 'metameletheis' (regretted/changed his mind) indicates a change of heart leading to changed action, distinct from 'metanoia' (repentance).

question: What is the parable of the wicked tenants?

scripture_says.text: A landowner planted a vineyard, set a hedge around it, dug a winepress, built a tower, and leased it to tenants. At harvest, he sent servants to collect his fruit, but the tenants beat, killed, and stoned them. He sent more servants with the same result. Finally he sent his son, saying 'They will respect my son.' But the tenants said 'This is the heir; let us kill him and seize his inheritance.' They threw him out of the vineyard and killed him.

Jesus asked what the owner will do to those tenants. They said he will destroy them and give the vineyard to other tenants. Jesus quoted, 'The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone.' The kingdom of God will be taken from you and given to a nation producing its fruits.

history_records.text: This parable echoes Isaiah's Song of the Vineyard (Isaiah 5:1-7), where Israel is depicted as God's vineyard that produced wild grapes.

question: What is the parable of the wedding feast?

scripture_says.text: A king prepared a wedding feast for his son and sent servants to call those invited, but they would not come. He sent other servants saying the feast was ready, but they made light of it, went to their farm or business, and some seized the servants and killed them. The king was angry, destroyed those murderers and burned their city.

The king told servants to go to the highways and invite anyone they found. They gathered all they found, both bad and good, and the wedding hall was filled. But when the king saw a man without a wedding garment, he said, 'Friend, how did you get in without a wedding garment?' The man was speechless. The king had him bound and cast into outer darkness.

history_records.text: Ancient Near Eastern custom often provided wedding garments to guests. The man's lack of a garment indicated refusal of the host's provision, not poverty.

question: What is the parable of the ten virgins?

scripture_says.text: Ten virgins took their lamps to meet the bridegroom. Five were foolish and took no oil; five were wise and took oil in vessels with their lamps. While the bridegroom delayed, they all slumbered and slept. At midnight a cry came: 'The bridegroom is coming!' All arose and trimmed their lamps, but the foolish had no oil and went to buy more.

While they went to buy, the bridegroom came. Those who were ready went in with him to the marriage feast, and the door was shut. Later the other virgins came saying, 'Lord, lord, open to us.' But he answered, 'Truly, I say to you, I do not know you.' Watch therefore, for you know neither the day nor the hour.

history_records.text: Jewish wedding customs involved a torch-lit procession from the bride's home to the groom's home, often at night. Lamps (lampades) were small oil lamps requiring frequent refueling.

question: What is the parable of the talents?

scripture_says.text: A man going on a journey called his servants and entrusted them with his property: to one he gave five talents, to another two, to another one, each according to his ability. The one with five talents traded and made five more. The one with two made two more. But the one with one talent dug in the ground and hid his master's money.

When the master returned, he commended those who doubled their talents: 'Well done, good and faithful servant. You have been faithful over a little; I will set you over much. Enter into the joy of your master.' But the servant who hid his talent was called wicked and lazy. His talent was taken and given to the one with ten.

history_records.text: A talent was about 75 pounds of silver, worth approximately 6,000 denarii or about 20 years' wages for a laborer. Five talents was an enormous sum.

question: What is the parable of the sheep and goats?

scripture_says.text: When the Son of Man comes in his glory with all the angels, he will sit on his glorious throne. All nations will be gathered before him, and he will separate people as a shepherd separates sheep from goats, putting sheep on his right and goats on his left.

The King will say to those on his right, 'Come, you who are blessed by my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world. For I was hungry and you gave me food, thirsty and you gave me drink, a stranger and you welcomed me, naked and you clothed me, sick and you visited me, in prison and you came to me.'

The righteous will ask when they did these things. The King will answer, 'As you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me.' To those on the left he will say, 'Depart from me, you cursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels.'

history_records.text: In Palestine, sheep and goats often grazed together during the day but were separated at night because goats need warmth. Sheep were more valuable than goats.

question: What is the parable of the good Samaritan?

scripture_says.text: A man going from Jerusalem to Jericho fell among robbers who stripped him, beat him, and left him half dead. A priest came down that road and passed by on the other side. Likewise a Levite came, looked at him, and passed by on the other side.

A Samaritan came where he was, saw him, and had compassion. He bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine, set him on his own animal, brought him to an inn, and took care of him. The next day he gave two denarii to the innkeeper, saying, 'Take care of him, and whatever more you spend, I will repay when I come back.'
Jesus asked, 'Which of these three proved to be a neighbor to him who fell among the robbers?' The lawyer said, 'The one who showed him mercy.' Jesus said, 'Go and do likewise.'

history_records.text: The road from Jerusalem to Jericho descended about 3,400 feet over 17 miles through desolate terrain, notorious for robbers. Jews and Samaritans had long-standing hostility (John 4:9).

question: What is the parable of the rich fool?

scripture_says.text: The land of a rich man produced plentifully. He thought, 'What shall I do, for I have nowhere to store my crops?' He said, 'I will tear down my barns and build larger ones, and there I will store all my grain and my goods. And I will say to my soul, Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years; relax, eat, drink, be merry.'
But God said to him, 'Fool! This night your soul is required of you, and the things you have prepared, whose will they be?' So is the one who lays up treasure for himself and is not rich toward God.

history_records.text: The Greek 'aphron' (fool) indicates one who is senseless or without understanding of divine truth, not merely lacking intelligence.

question: What is the parable of the barren fig tree?

scripture_says.text: A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard. He came seeking fruit on it and found none. He said to the vinedresser, 'Look, for three years now I have come seeking fruit on this fig tree, and I find none. Cut it down. Why should it use up the ground?'
The vinedresser answered, 'Sir, let it alone this year also, until I dig around it and put on manure. Then if it should bear fruit next year, well and good; but if not, you can cut it down.'

history_records.text: Fig trees in Palestine take about three years to mature enough to bear fruit. The mention of 'three years' suggests the tree had ample time to produce.
