question: What is the parable of the great banquet?

scripture_says.text: A man gave a great banquet and invited many. At the hour for the banquet he sent his servant to say, 'Come, for everything is now ready.' But they all alike began to make excuses. The first said, 'I have bought a field, and I must go see it.' Another said, 'I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I must examine them.' Another said, 'I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.'

The master was angry and told his servant to go quickly into the streets and lanes and bring in the poor, crippled, blind, and lame. When there was still room, he said, 'Go out to the highways and hedges and compel people to come in, that my house may be filled. For I tell you, none of those who were invited shall taste my banquet.'

history_records.text: Ancient banquet etiquette involved two invitations: an initial invitation and a second call when the feast was ready. Refusing the second call was a serious insult.

question: What is the parable of the lost sheep?

scripture_says.text: Jesus asked, 'What man of you, having a hundred sheep, if he has lost one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the open country, and go after the one that is lost, until he finds it? And when he has found it, he lays it on his shoulders, rejoicing.'

When he comes home, he calls together his friends and neighbors, saying, 'Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep that was lost.' Just so, there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance.

history_records.text: Shepherds in ancient Israel were personally responsible for every sheep. A lost sheep meant financial loss and potential danger to the flock.

question: What is the parable of the lost coin?

scripture_says.text: What woman, having ten silver coins, if she loses one coin, does not light a lamp and sweep the house and seek diligently until she finds it? And when she has found it, she calls together her friends and neighbors, saying, 'Rejoice with me, for I have found the coin that I had lost.'
Just so, I tell you, there is joy before the angels of God over one sinner who repents.

history_records.text: The Greek 'drachma' was a silver coin worth about a day's wage. Ten coins may have been a woman's dowry or savings, often worn as a headdress ornament.

question: What is the parable of the prodigal son?

scripture_says.text: A man had two sons. The younger asked for his share of the property, and the father divided his property between them. The younger son gathered all he had, traveled to a far country, and squandered his property in reckless living. When he had spent everything, a severe famine arose, and he began to be in need. He went and hired himself out to feed pigs, longing to eat the pods the pigs ate.

He came to himself and said, 'How many of my father's hired servants have more than enough bread, but I perish here with hunger! I will arise and go to my father, and I will say to him, Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Treat me as one of your hired servants.'

While he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, ran and embraced him and kissed him. The son began to say, 'Father, I have sinned...' But the father said to his servants, 'Bring quickly the best robe, put a ring on his hand, shoes on his feet, bring the fattened calf and kill it. Let us eat and celebrate. For this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'asotos' (prodigal/reckless) means wastefully extravagant, living without saving. For a Jewish son to feed pigs (unclean animals) represented utter degradation.

question: What is the parable of the dishonest manager?

scripture_says.text: A rich man's manager was accused of wasting his possessions. The master called him and said, 'What is this I hear about you? Turn in the account of your management, for you can no longer be manager.' The manager said to himself, 'What shall I do? I am not strong enough to dig, and I am ashamed to beg.'

He summoned his master's debtors. He asked the first, 'How much do you owe?' He said, 'A hundred measures of oil.' He said, 'Take your bill, sit down quickly, and write fifty.' He asked another, 'How much do you owe?' He said, 'A hundred measures of wheat.' He said, 'Take your bill and write eighty.'

The master commended the dishonest manager for his shrewdness. For the sons of this world are more shrewd in dealing with their own generation than the sons of light. Make friends for yourselves by means of unrighteous wealth, so that when it fails they may receive you into the eternal dwellings.

history_records.text: The Greek 'oikonomos' (manager/steward) was a household administrator, often a trusted slave who had full authority over his master's affairs.

question: What is the parable of the rich man and Lazarus?

scripture_says.text: There was a rich man clothed in purple and fine linen who feasted sumptuously every day. At his gate was laid a poor man named Lazarus, covered with sores, who desired to be fed with what fell from the rich man's table. Even the dogs came and licked his sores.

The poor man died and was carried by angels to Abraham's side. The rich man also died and was buried. In Hades, being in torment, he lifted up his eyes and saw Abraham far off and Lazarus at his side. He called out, 'Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus to dip the end of his finger in water and cool my tongue, for I am in anguish in this flame.'

Abraham said, 'Child, remember that you in your lifetime received your good things, and Lazarus bad things; but now he is comforted here, and you are in anguish. Besides, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, so that those who would pass from here to you cannot, and none may cross from there to us.'

history_records.text: Lazarus (Greek: Lazaros, from Hebrew Eleazar, meaning 'God has helped') is the only named character in any of Jesus' parables.

question: What is the parable of the persistent widow?

scripture_says.text: Jesus told them a parable to the effect that they ought always to pray and not lose heart. In a certain city there was a judge who neither feared God nor respected man. And there was a widow in that city who kept coming to him and saying, 'Give me justice against my adversary.'

For a while he refused, but afterward he said to himself, 'Though I neither fear God nor respect man, yet because this widow keeps bothering me, I will give her justice, so that she will not beat me down by her continual coming.'

The Lord said, 'Hear what the unrighteous judge says. And will not God give justice to his elect, who cry to him day and night? Will he delay long over them? I tell you, he will give justice to them speedily.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'ekdikesis' (justice/vindication) refers to legal protection or deliverance from an oppressor. Widows were especially vulnerable in ancient society without a male advocate.

question: What is the parable of the Pharisee and tax collector?

scripture_says.text: Jesus told this parable to some who trusted in themselves that they were righteous and treated others with contempt. Two men went up into the temple to pray, one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector.

The Pharisee, standing by himself, prayed thus: 'God, I thank you that I am not like other men, extortioners, unjust, adulterers, or even like this tax collector. I fast twice a week; I give tithes of all that I get.'

But the tax collector, standing far off, would not even lift up his eyes to heaven, but beat his breast, saying, 'God, be merciful to me, a sinner!' I tell you, this man went down to his house justified, rather than the other. For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.

history_records.text: The Greek 'hilaskomai' (be merciful/propitiated) is related to 'hilasterion' (mercy seat). The tax collector's prayer echoes the Day of Atonement ritual.

question: What is the parable of the minas?

scripture_says.text: A nobleman went into a far country to receive a kingdom and then return. Calling ten of his servants, he gave them ten minas and said, 'Engage in business until I come.' But his citizens hated him and sent a delegation after him, saying, 'We do not want this man to reign over us.'

When he returned, having received the kingdom, he ordered the servants called to whom he had given the money. The first came saying, 'Lord, your mina has made ten minas more.' He said, 'Well done, good servant! Because you have been faithful in a very little, you shall have authority over ten cities.' The second came saying, 'Lord, your mina has made five minas.' He said, 'You are to be over five cities.'

Another came saying, 'Lord, here is your mina, which I kept laid away in a handkerchief; for I was afraid of you, because you are a severe man.' He said, 'I will condemn you with your own words, you wicked servant! Why did you not put my money in the bank, and at my coming I might have collected it with interest?' Take the mina from him and give it to the one who has ten minas.

history_records.text: A mina (Greek 'mna') was about 100 drachmas or about 100 days' wages. The historical context may reflect Archelaus' journey to Rome in 4 BC to receive his kingdom.

question: What was the Ark of the Covenant?

scripture_says.text: God instructed Moses to make an ark of acacia wood, two and a half cubits long, a cubit and a half wide, and a cubit and a half high, overlaid with pure gold inside and out, with a gold molding around it. Four gold rings were attached for carrying poles.
The ark contained the tablets of the covenant. God said He would meet with Moses there and speak with him from above the mercy seat, between the two cherubim.
The ark was placed in the Most Holy Place of the tabernacle, behind the veil.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'aron' (ark/chest) refers to a box or chest. The ark was carried by Levites using poles and accompanied Israel through the wilderness and into Canaan.

question: What were the tablets of stone?

scripture_says.text: When God finished speaking with Moses on Mount Sinai, He gave him two tablets of the testimony, tablets of stone, written with the finger of God.
The tablets were the work of God, and the writing was the writing of God, engraved on the tablets. They were written on both sides, on the front and on the back.
Moses broke the first tablets when he saw the golden calf. God told him to cut two new tablets like the first, and God wrote the Ten Commandments on them again.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'luchot' (tablets) comes from a root meaning to glisten or be polished. Ancient Near Eastern treaties were often inscribed on stone tablets.

scripture_says.text: The LORD told Moses to take rods from the leaders of each tribe, with each man's name on his rod, and Aaron's name on the rod of Levi. He was to place them in the tent of meeting before the testimony.

The next day Moses entered the tent, and behold, the rod of Aaron for the house of Levi had sprouted, put forth buds, produced blossoms, and bore ripe almonds. Moses brought out all the rods, and each man took his rod.
The LORD said to Moses, 'Put back the rod of Aaron before the testimony, to be kept as a sign for the rebels, that you may put an end to their grumbling against me.'

history_records.text: Aaron's rod was kept in the ark according to Hebrews 9:4, along with the tablets and a pot of manna, as a testimony of God's choice of the Aaronic priesthood.

question: What was the pot of manna?

scripture_says.text: Moses said, 'This is what the LORD has commanded: Let an omer of manna be kept throughout your generations, so that they may see the bread with which I fed you in the wilderness.'
Moses said to Aaron, 'Take a jar and put an omer of manna in it and place it before the LORD to be kept throughout your generations.' Aaron placed it before the testimony to be kept.

history_records.text: The writer of Hebrews mentions 'a golden urn holding the manna' kept in the ark, indicating the pot was made of gold.

scripture_says.text: When fiery serpents bit the people and many died, the LORD said to Moses, 'Make a fiery serpent and set it on a pole, and everyone who is bitten, when he sees it, shall live.' So Moses made a bronze serpent and set it on a pole. If a serpent bit anyone, he would look at the bronze serpent and live.
King Hezekiah broke in pieces the bronze serpent that Moses had made, for until those days the people of Israel had made offerings to it; it was called Nehushtan.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'nechushtan' (Nehushtan) is a wordplay combining 'nachash' (serpent) and 'nechoshet' (bronze). Jesus compared His crucifixion to this event (John 3:14).

scripture_says.text: The LORD said to Moses, 'Let them make me a sanctuary, that I may dwell in their midst. Exactly as I show you concerning the pattern of the tabernacle, and of all its furniture, so you shall make it.'
The tabernacle consisted of curtains of fine twisted linen with cherubim woven into them, boards of acacia wood overlaid with gold, and a veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place.
The cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle. When the cloud lifted, Israel would journey; when it remained, they stayed in place.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'mishkan' (tabernacle/dwelling) comes from 'shakan' (to dwell). The tabernacle served as God's dwelling place among His people from Sinai until Solomon's temple.

question: What was the ark of bulrushes?

scripture_says.text: When Moses' mother could hide him no longer, she took a basket made of bulrushes, daubed it with bitumen and pitch, put the child in it, and placed it among the reeds by the river bank.

His sister stood at a distance to know what would be done to him. Pharaoh's daughter came to bathe at the river, saw the basket among the reeds, and sent her servant to bring it. When she opened it, she saw the child, a boy crying, and had compassion on him.

history_records.text: The Hebrew word 'tevah' (basket/ark) is the same word used for Noah's ark (Gen 6:14). Papyrus boats were common in Egypt.

scripture_says.text: The LORD said to Moses, 'What is that in your hand?' He said, 'A staff.' God said, 'Throw it on the ground.' He threw it, and it became a serpent. The LORD said, 'Put out your hand and catch it by the tail.' He did so, and it became a staff in his hand.
God said to take the staff in his hand, with which he would do the signs. Moses took the staff of God in his hand.
Moses stretched out his staff over the Red Sea, and the LORD drove the sea back by a strong east wind all night and made the sea dry land, and the waters were divided.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'matteh' (staff/rod) was a shepherd's implement that became the instrument of God's power. It was used in the plagues, parting the sea, and bringing water from the rock.

question: What were the Urim and Thummim?

scripture_says.text: You shall put in the breastpiece of judgment the Urim and the Thummim, and they shall be on Aaron's heart when he goes in before the LORD. Thus Aaron shall bear the judgment of the people of Israel on his heart before the LORD regularly.
Saul inquired of the LORD, but the LORD did not answer him, either by dreams, or by Urim, or by prophets.
The governor told them they were not to eat of the most holy food until a priest stood to minister with Urim and Thummim.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Urim' likely means 'lights' and 'Thummim' means 'perfections.' Their exact nature is unknown, but they were used for divine guidance.

scripture_says.text: They shall make the ephod of gold, of blue and purple and scarlet yarns, and of fine twined linen, skillfully worked. It shall have two shoulder pieces attached to its two edges.

You shall take two onyx stones and engrave on them the names of the sons of Israel, six names on each stone. You shall set the stones on the shoulder pieces of the ephod, as stones of remembrance for the sons of Israel.
David was wearing a linen ephod when he danced before the LORD as they brought up the ark.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'ephod' was a priestly vestment worn over the robe. The high priest's ephod was elaborate, while ordinary priests wore a simple linen ephod.

question: What was the breastplate of judgment?

scripture_says.text: You shall make a breastpiece of judgment, in skilled work, like the work of the ephod. It shall be square and doubled, a span its length and a span its breadth.

You shall set in it four rows of stones: sardius, topaz, and carbuncle in the first row; emerald, sapphire, and diamond in the second; jacinth, agate, and amethyst in the third; beryl, onyx, and jasper in the fourth. Each stone shall be engraved with the name of one of the twelve tribes.
So Aaron shall bear the names of the sons of Israel in the breastpiece of judgment on his heart when he goes into the Holy Place, for a remembrance before the LORD regularly.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'choshen mishpat' (breastpiece of judgment) contained the Urim and Thummim and was worn over the heart when seeking God's decisions.

scripture_says.text: You shall make a mercy seat of pure gold. Two cubits and a half shall be its length, and a cubit and a half its breadth.

You shall make two cherubim of gold; of hammered work shall you make them, on the two ends of the mercy seat. The cherubim shall spread out their wings above, overshadowing the mercy seat with their wings, their faces toward one another.
There I will meet with you, and from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubim that are on the ark of the testimony, I will speak with you.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'kapporet' (mercy seat/atonement cover) comes from 'kaphar' (to cover, atone). On the Day of Atonement, the high priest sprinkled blood on it.

question: What were the cherubim on the ark?

scripture_says.text: You shall make two cherubim of gold; of hammered work shall you make them, on the two ends of the mercy seat. Make one cherub on one end, and one cherub on the other end. Of one piece with the mercy seat shall you make the cherubim on its two ends.
The cherubim shall spread out their wings above, overshadowing the mercy seat with their wings, their faces toward one another; toward the mercy seat shall the faces of the cherubim be.
The LORD said He would meet with Moses and speak from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubim on the ark of the testimony.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'keruvim' (cherubim) were heavenly beings associated with God's presence. They first appear guarding Eden (Gen 3:24) and are prominent in Ezekiel's visions.

scripture_says.text: You shall make a lampstand of pure gold. The lampstand shall be made of hammered work: its base, its stem, its cups, its calyxes, and its flowers shall be of one piece with it. Six branches shall go out of its sides, three from one side and three from the other.
Three cups made like almond blossoms with calyx and flower on each branch, and four cups on the stem of the lampstand. You shall make seven lamps for it, and they shall give light in front of it.
It shall be made, with all these utensils, out of a talent of pure gold. See that you make them after the pattern shown you on the mountain.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'menorah' (lampstand) was placed in the Holy Place opposite the table of showbread. It was fueled with pure olive oil and tended morning and evening.

question: What was the table of showbread?

scripture_says.text: You shall make a table of acacia wood. Two cubits shall be its length, a cubit its breadth, and a cubit and a half its height. You shall overlay it with pure gold and make a molding of gold around it.
You shall set the bread of the Presence on the table before me regularly.
Every Sabbath he shall arrange it before the LORD regularly. It shall be for Aaron and his sons, and they shall eat it in a holy place, for it is most holy to him.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'lechem panim' (bread of the Presence/showbread) literally means 'bread of the face,' signifying bread set before God's face. Twelve loaves represented the twelve tribes.

question: What was the altar of incense?

scripture_says.text: You shall make an altar on which to burn incense; you shall make it of acacia wood. A cubit shall be its length, and a cubit its breadth. It shall be square, and two cubits shall be its height. You shall overlay it with pure gold.

Aaron shall burn fragrant incense on it. Every morning when he dresses the lamps he shall burn it, and when Aaron sets up the lamps at twilight, he shall burn it, a regular incense offering before the LORD.
You shall not offer unauthorized incense on it, or a burnt offering, or a grain offering, and you shall not pour a drink offering on it.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'mizbach miqtar' (altar of incense) stood in the Holy Place before the veil. Incense represented the prayers of God's people rising to heaven.

question: What was the altar of burnt offering?

scripture_says.text: You shall make the altar of acacia wood, five cubits long and five cubits broad. The altar shall be square, and its height shall be three cubits. You shall make horns for it on its four corners.
You shall make pots for removing its ashes, shovels, basins, forks, and fire pans. All its utensils you shall make of bronze.
You shall make for it a grating, a network of bronze, and on the net you shall make four bronze rings at its four corners.

history_records.text: The bronze altar stood in the tabernacle courtyard. It was the place where animal sacrifices were offered. Solomon's temple had a larger bronze altar (2 Chronicles 4:1).

scripture_says.text: You shall make a basin of bronze, with its stand of bronze, for washing. You shall put it between the tent of meeting and the altar, and you shall put water in it.

Aaron and his sons shall wash their hands and their feet from it. When they go into the tent of meeting, or when they come near the altar to minister, they shall wash with water, so that they may not die.
He made the basin of bronze and its stand of bronze, from the mirrors of the ministering women who ministered in the entrance of the tent of meeting.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'kiyor' (laver/basin) was made from the bronze mirrors donated by Israelite women. Solomon's temple had a 'molten sea' and ten smaller lavers on wheeled stands.

question: What were the curtains of the tabernacle?

scripture_says.text: You shall make the tabernacle with ten curtains of fine twined linen and blue and purple and scarlet yarns; you shall make them with cherubim skillfully worked into them.
The length of each curtain shall be twenty-eight cubits, and the breadth of each curtain four cubits. All the curtains shall be the same size.
You shall make curtains of goats' hair for a tent over the tabernacle; eleven curtains shall you make. You shall also make a covering of tanned rams' skins and a covering of goatskins on top.

history_records.text: The tabernacle had four layers of covering: fine linen with cherubim, goats' hair, rams' skins dyed red, and an outer covering of fine leather (possibly dugong or porpoise skin).

question: What was the veil of the temple?

scripture_says.text: You shall make a veil of blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen. It shall be made with cherubim skillfully worked into it. The veil shall separate for you the Holy Place from the Most Holy.
At the moment of Jesus' death, the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom.
We have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'paroketh' (veil/curtain) separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy. Josephus records that the temple veil was four inches thick and could not be torn by horses pulling in opposite directions.

scripture_says.text: The king made a great ivory throne and overlaid it with the finest gold. The throne had six steps, and a round top at the back of the throne. On each side of the seat were armrests, and two lions stood beside the armrests.
Twelve lions stood there, one on each end of a step on the six steps. Nothing like it had ever been made in any kingdom.

history_records.text: Solomon's throne was renowned throughout the ancient world. The lions likely symbolized royal power and the tribe of Judah. Ivory was a precious import, often from Africa.

scripture_says.text: David ran and stood over the Philistine, took his sword, drew it out of its sheath, and killed him and cut off his head with it. When the Philistines saw that their champion was dead, they fled.

David said to Ahimelech the priest, 'Is there not here a spear or a sword? For I have brought neither my sword nor my weapons with me.' The priest said, 'The sword of Goliath the Philistine, whom you struck down in the Valley of Elah, is here wrapped in a cloth behind the ephod. If you will take that, take it.' David said, 'There is none like it; give it to me.'

history_records.text: The sword was kept at the tabernacle at Nob as a trophy of God's victory. David later retrieved it when fleeing from Saul, recognizing its unique value.

question: What was the sling of David?

scripture_says.text: David took his staff in his hand and chose five smooth stones from the brook and put them in his shepherd's pouch. His sling was in his hand, and he approached the Philistine.
David put his hand in his bag and took out a stone and slung it and struck the Philistine on his forehead. The stone sank into his forehead, and he fell on his face to the ground.
So David prevailed over the Philistine with a sling and with a stone, and struck the Philistine and killed him. There was no sword in the hand of David.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'qela' (sling) was a shepherd's tool and weapon. Ancient slingers were formidable warriors; Judges mentions 700 left-handed Benjamite slingers who could hit a hair.

question: What were the pieces of silver?

scripture_says.text: Then one of the twelve, whose name was Judas Iscariot, went to the chief priests and said, 'What will you give me if I deliver him over to you?' And they paid him thirty pieces of silver.

When Judas, his betrayer, saw that Jesus was condemned, he changed his mind and brought back the thirty pieces of silver to the chief priests and elders, saying, 'I have sinned by betraying innocent blood.'

The chief priests took the pieces of silver and said, 'It is not lawful to put them into the treasury, since it is blood money.' They used them to buy the potter's field as a burial place for strangers.

history_records.text: Thirty pieces of silver was the compensation for a slave gored by an ox (Exodus 21:32). Matthew sees fulfillment in Zechariah 11:12-13, where the shepherd is valued at thirty shekels.

question: What was the crown of thorns?

scripture_says.text: The soldiers of the governor took Jesus into the governor's headquarters and gathered the whole battalion before him. They stripped him and put a scarlet robe on him, and twisting together a crown of thorns, they put it on his head.
The soldiers wove a crown of thorns and put it on his head and arrayed him in a purple robe. They came up to him, saying, 'Hail, King of the Jews!' and struck him with their hands.
Jesus came out, wearing the crown of thorns and the purple robe. Pilate said to them, 'Behold the man!'

history_records.text: The Greek 'akanthinos stephanos' (crown of thorns) was a mockery of royal crowns. The thorns may have been from the Ziziphus spina-christi (Christ-thorn jujube) or similar plants common in Jerusalem.

scripture_says.text: When the soldiers had crucified Jesus, they took his garments and divided them into four parts, one part for each soldier. But the tunic was seamless, woven in one piece from top to bottom.

So they said to one another, 'Let us not tear it, but cast lots for it to see whose it shall be.' This was to fulfill the Scripture which says, 'They divided my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'chiton arraphos' (seamless tunic) was woven on a vertical loom. The high priest's robe was also seamless according to Jewish tradition (Josephus, Antiquities 3.7.4).

scripture_says.text: As they went out, they found a man of Cyrene, Simon by name. They compelled this man to carry his cross. And when they came to a place called Golgotha, which means Place of a Skull, they crucified him.

Pilate also wrote an inscription and put it on the cross. It read, 'Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews.' Many of the Jews read this inscription, for the place where Jesus was crucified was near the city, and it was written in Aramaic, in Latin, and in Greek.
Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us - for it is written, 'Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'stauros' (cross) was the Roman instrument of execution reserved for slaves, criminals, and non-citizens. Crucifixion was designed for maximum pain and public shame.

question: What was the tomb of Jesus?

scripture_says.text: When it was evening, there came a rich man from Arimathea, named Joseph, who also was a disciple of Jesus. He went to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus. Then Pilate ordered it to be given to him.
Joseph took the body and wrapped it in a clean linen shroud and laid it in his own new tomb, which he had cut in the rock. And he rolled a great stone to the entrance of the tomb and went away.
Now in the place where he was crucified there was a garden, and in the garden a new tomb in which no one had yet been laid.

history_records.text: Rock-cut tombs were expensive and typically owned by wealthy families. Using a new tomb ensured Jesus' body would not be confused with another, fulfilling Isaiah 53:9 about being with the rich in death.

question: What was the stone rolled away?

scripture_says.text: And behold, there was a great earthquake, for an angel of the Lord descended from heaven and came and rolled back the stone and sat on it. His appearance was like lightning, and his clothing white as snow.
They were saying to one another, 'Who will roll away the stone for us from the entrance of the tomb?' And looking up, they saw that the stone had been rolled back - it was very large.
They found the stone rolled away from the tomb, but when they went in they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus.

history_records.text: The Greek 'lithos' (stone) refers to large circular stones rolled in grooves to seal tomb entrances. The guards and seal mentioned in Matthew 27:66 indicate the stone's security.

scripture_says.text: The LORD God said, 'It is not good that the man should be alone; I will make him a helper fit for him.' So the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon the man, and while he slept took one of his ribs and made it into a woman and brought her to the man.
The man called his wife's name Eve, because she was the mother of all living.
The serpent deceived Eve by his cunning, and she ate the forbidden fruit and gave some to her husband.

history_records.text: The Hebrew name 'Chavvah' (Eve) is related to 'chay' (living), signifying her role as mother of all humanity.

scripture_says.text: God said to Abraham, 'As for Sarai your wife, you shall not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall be her name. I will bless her, and I will give you a son by her.'
Sarah conceived and bore Abraham a son in his old age at the time of which God had spoken to him. Abraham called his son Isaac.
Sarah lived 127 years and died at Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron) in the land of Canaan. Abraham buried her in the cave of Machpelah.

history_records.text: The Hebrew name 'Sarah' means 'princess.' She was originally called 'Sarai.' She is cited in Hebrews 11:11 as an example of faith.

scripture_says.text: Sarai, Abram's wife, had borne him no children. She had a female Egyptian servant whose name was Hagar. Sarai said to Abram, 'Go in to my servant; it may be that I shall obtain children by her.'
Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram called the name of his son Ishmael.
After Isaac was weaned, Sarah saw Ishmael laughing and told Abraham to cast out the slave woman and her son. God told Hagar He would make Ishmael into a nation.

history_records.text: Paul uses Hagar allegorically in Galatians 4:24-25 to represent Mount Sinai and the covenant of law, contrasting with Sarah who represents the covenant of promise.

scripture_says.text: Abraham's servant went to Nahor's city and met Rebekah at the well. She was the daughter of Bethuel, Abraham's nephew. She watered his camels and showed hospitality.
Isaac brought Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent and took her, and she became his wife, and he loved her.
Rebekah conceived twins. The LORD said to her, 'Two nations are in your womb, and two peoples from within you shall be divided; the older shall serve the younger.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Rivqah' (Rebekah) may mean 'to tie' or 'to snare.' She helped Jacob receive Isaac's blessing in Genesis 27.

scripture_says.text: Jacob came to the land of the people of the east and saw Rachel, daughter of Laban, coming with her father's sheep. Jacob loved Rachel and served Laban seven years for her.
Rachel was barren. When God remembered her and opened her womb, she bore Joseph and later died giving birth to Benjamin.
Jacob buried Rachel on the way to Ephrath (that is, Bethlehem) and set up a pillar over her tomb.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Rachel' means 'ewe.' Jeremiah 31:15 refers to Rachel weeping for her children, quoted in Matthew 2:18 regarding the slaughter of infants.

scripture_says.text: Laban had two daughters. The name of the older was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel. Leah's eyes were weak, but Rachel was beautiful in form and appearance.
Laban gave Leah to Jacob instead of Rachel on the wedding night. Jacob discovered the deception in the morning.
When the LORD saw that Leah was unloved, he opened her womb. She bore Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun, and a daughter Dinah.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Leah' may mean 'weary' or 'cow.' She became the ancestress of the priestly tribe (Levi) and the royal tribe (Judah), making her an ancestor of Jesus.

question: Who was Tamar daughter-in-law of Judah?

scripture_says.text: Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, and her name was Tamar. Er died, and Judah told his second son Onan to perform the duty of a brother-in-law to her, but Onan also died.
When Judah did not give Tamar his third son Shelah, she disguised herself and Judah unknowingly slept with her. She conceived twins, Perez and Zerah.
Judah acknowledged, 'She is more righteous than I, since I did not give her to my son Shelah.'

history_records.text: Tamar is listed in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus. Through Perez, she became an ancestor of King David and ultimately of Christ.

scripture_says.text: His sister stood at a distance to know what would be done to him (Moses in the basket). She arranged for Moses' mother to nurse him for Pharaoh's daughter.
Then Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, took a tambourine in her hand, and all the women went out after her with tambourines and dancing. And Miriam sang to them.
Miriam and Aaron spoke against Moses because of the Cushite woman he had married. Miriam became leprous, white as snow. Moses cried to the LORD, and she was healed after seven days outside the camp.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Miryam' is the same name as Mary (Greek Mariam). God said through Micah, 'I sent before you Moses, Aaron, and Miriam' (Micah 6:4).

scripture_says.text: Joshua sent two men secretly from Shittim as spies. They went and came into the house of a prostitute whose name was Rahab and lodged there. She hid them on the roof.
Rahab said to the spies, 'I know that the LORD has given you the land... for the LORD your God, he is God in the heavens above and on the earth beneath.'
Joshua spared Rahab the prostitute and her father's household and all who belonged to her, because she hid the messengers.

history_records.text: Rahab appears in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus as the mother of Boaz. She is commended for faith in Hebrews 11:31 and James 2:25.

scripture_says.text: Now Deborah, a prophetess, the wife of Lappidoth, was judging Israel at that time. She used to sit under the palm of Deborah between Ramah and Bethel, and the people of Israel came up to her for judgment.
She sent and summoned Barak and said to him, 'The LORD, the God of Israel, commands you: Go, gather your men at Mount Tabor... and I will draw out Sisera, the general of Jabin's army, to meet you.'
Deborah and Barak sang on that day, saying, 'That the leaders took the lead in Israel, that the people offered themselves willingly, bless the LORD!'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Devorah' (Deborah) means 'bee.' She is the only female judge of Israel mentioned in Scripture and led Israel during 40 years of peace.

scripture_says.text: Sisera fled away on foot to the tent of Jael, the wife of Heber the Kenite. Jael came out to meet Sisera and said to him, 'Turn aside, my lord; do not be afraid.' She covered him with a rug.

Jael took a tent peg, and took a hammer in her hand. Then she went softly to him and drove the peg into his temple until it went down into the ground while he was lying fast asleep from weariness. So he died.
Deborah sang, 'Most blessed of women be Jael, the wife of Heber the Kenite, of tent-dwelling women most blessed.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yael' means 'mountain goat.' The Kenites were descendants of Moses' father-in-law and were metalworkers, explaining Jael's skill with a hammer.

scripture_says.text: Ruth said to Naomi, 'Do not urge me to leave you or to return from following you. For where you go I will go, and where you lodge I will lodge. Your people shall be my people, and your God my God.'
Ruth went and gleaned in the field after the reapers, and she happened to come to the part of the field belonging to Boaz, who was of the clan of Elimelech.
Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. She bore a son, and they named him Obed. He was the father of Jesse, the father of David.

history_records.text: Ruth was a Moabite who became an ancestor of King David and appears in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus. The Hebrew 'Ruth' may mean 'friend' or 'companion.'

scripture_says.text: Elimelech, his wife Naomi, and their two sons went to sojourn in Moab. Elimelech died, and the sons married Moabite women. After about ten years, both sons also died, leaving Naomi with her two daughters-in-law.
Naomi said, 'Do not call me Naomi; call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me. I went away full, and the LORD has brought me back empty.'
Naomi took the child (Obed) and laid him on her lap and became his nurse. The women of the neighborhood said, 'A son has been born to Naomi.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Naomi' means 'pleasant' or 'my delight.' 'Mara' means 'bitter.' Through Ruth's son Obed, Naomi became the great-great-grandmother of King David.

scripture_says.text: Elkanah had two wives. The name of one was Hannah. She had no children, for the LORD had closed her womb. Her rival provoked her grievously to irritate her.
Hannah prayed and wept bitterly. She vowed, 'O LORD of hosts, if you will give your servant a son, I will give him to the LORD all the days of his life.'

The LORD remembered her. Hannah conceived and bore a son and called his name Samuel, for she said, 'I have asked for him from the LORD.' When she had weaned him, she brought him to Eli at the house of the LORD.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Channah' means 'grace' or 'favor.' Hannah's song of praise (1 Samuel 2:1-10) has parallels with Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55).

scripture_says.text: The name of the man was Nabal, and the name of his wife Abigail. The woman was discerning and beautiful, but the man was harsh and badly behaved.
When Abigail saw David, she fell before him and said, 'On me alone, my lord, be the guilt. Let your servant speak.' She brought provisions and averted David's wrath against Nabal's house.
When the LORD had struck Nabal and he died, David sent and spoke to Abigail, to take her as his wife.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Avigayil' (Abigail) means 'my father is joy' or 'source of joy.' She bore David a son named Chileab (2 Samuel 3:3).

scripture_says.text: David sent messengers and took her, and she came to him, and he lay with her. The woman conceived and sent word to David, 'I am pregnant.'
David had Uriah the Hittite, her husband, placed in the front line of battle where he was killed. Then David brought Bathsheba to his house, and she became his wife.
Bathsheba bore Solomon. The LORD loved him and sent word by Nathan the prophet to call him Jedidiah.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Bat-Sheva' means 'daughter of the oath' or 'seventh daughter.' She advocated for Solomon's succession (1 Kings 1:11-31) and appears in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus.

question: Who was the Queen of Sheba?

scripture_says.text: When the queen of Sheba heard of the fame of Solomon concerning the name of the LORD, she came to test him with hard questions. She came to Jerusalem with a very great retinue, with camels bearing spices and very much gold and precious stones.

When she had seen all the wisdom of Solomon, the house that he had built, the food of his table, and the attendance of his servants, there was no more breath in her. She said, 'The report was true, but I did not believe it until I came.'

Jesus said, 'The queen of the South will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.'

history_records.text: Sheba was located in modern-day Yemen. The queen brought 120 talents of gold (about 4.5 tons). Ethiopian tradition identifies her as Makeda and claims the Ethiopian royal line descends from her and Solomon.

scripture_says.text: Ahab took for his wife Jezebel the daughter of Ethbaal king of the Sidonians, and went and served Baal and worshiped him.
Jezebel cut off the prophets of the LORD. She used Ahab's seal to arrange the murder of Naboth to seize his vineyard.
Elijah prophesied concerning Jezebel, 'The dogs shall eat Jezebel within the walls of Jezreel.' When Jehu came, she was thrown from a window, trampled, and eaten by dogs.

history_records.text: The name 'Jezebel' became proverbial for a wicked, idolatrous woman. In Revelation 2:20, the church at Thyatira is rebuked for tolerating 'that woman Jezebel.'

scripture_says.text: When Athaliah the mother of Ahaziah saw that her son was dead, she arose and destroyed all the royal family. But Jehosheba took Joash and stole him away from among the king's sons who were being put to death.
Athaliah reigned over the land for six years while Joash was hidden in the house of the LORD.
In the seventh year, Jehoiada the priest crowned Joash king. When Athaliah heard the noise, she came to the people in the temple. Jehoiada commanded that she be brought out and put to death.

history_records.text: Athaliah was daughter of Ahab and Jezebel, and the only queen regnant of Judah. She attempted to destroy the Davidic line but failed due to Jehosheba's intervention.

scripture_says.text: Hilkiah the priest found the Book of the Law in the house of the LORD. When King Josiah heard the words of the Law, he tore his clothes and sent messengers to inquire of the LORD.
So Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam, Achbor, Shaphan, and Asaiah went to Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the keeper of the wardrobe. She lived in Jerusalem in the Second Quarter.

She said, 'Thus says the LORD: Behold, I will bring disaster upon this place... Because they have forsaken me. But as for the king of Judah, because your heart was penitent, you shall be gathered to your grave in peace.'

history_records.text: Huldah ministered during the same period as Jeremiah and Zephaniah. The court sought her word, suggesting she had a recognized prophetic ministry in Jerusalem.

scripture_says.text: He (Mordecai) was bringing up Hadassah, that is Esther, the daughter of his uncle, for she had neither father nor mother. The young woman had a beautiful figure and was lovely to look at.
The king loved Esther more than all the women, and she won grace and favor in his sight. He set the royal crown on her head and made her queen instead of Vashti.

Mordecai told Esther, 'Who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this?' Esther said, 'Go, gather all the Jews... and fast for me... Then I will go to the king, though it is against the law. And if I perish, I perish.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Hadassah' means 'myrtle.' 'Esther' may derive from Persian 'stara' (star) or be related to the goddess Ishtar. Esther was queen to Ahasuerus (Xerxes I) of Persia.

scripture_says.text: An excellent wife who can find? She is far more precious than jewels. The heart of her husband trusts in her, and he will have no lack of gain.

She seeks wool and flax, and works with willing hands. She rises while it is yet night and provides food for her household. She considers a field and buys it; with the fruit of her hands she plants a vineyard.

Strength and dignity are her clothing, and she laughs at the time to come. She opens her mouth with wisdom, and the teaching of kindness is on her tongue. Her children rise up and call her blessed; her husband also, and he praises her.

history_records.text: Proverbs 31:10-31 is an acrostic poem, each verse beginning with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet. It is attributed to the words King Lemuel's mother taught him.

question: Who was Mary mother of Jesus?

scripture_says.text: The angel Gabriel was sent from God to a virgin betrothed to a man whose name was Joseph. The virgin's name was Mary. The angel said, 'You will conceive in your womb and bear a son, and you shall call his name Jesus.'

Mary said, 'How will this be, since I am a virgin?' The angel answered, 'The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you; therefore the child to be born will be called holy - the Son of God.'
Mary said, 'My soul magnifies the Lord, and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior, for he has looked on the humble estate of his servant.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'Maria' or 'Mariam' comes from Hebrew 'Miryam' (Miriam). Mary was present at the cross and with the disciples after the ascension (Acts 1:14).

scripture_says.text: In the days of Herod, king of Judea, there was a priest named Zechariah, and he had a wife from the daughters of Aaron, and her name was Elizabeth. They were both righteous before God, walking blamelessly. But they had no child, because Elizabeth was barren.

After these days his wife Elizabeth conceived, and for five months she kept herself hidden, saying, 'Thus the Lord has done for me in the days when he looked on me, to take away my reproach among people.'
When Elizabeth heard Mary's greeting, the baby leaped in her womb. Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit and exclaimed, 'Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb!'

history_records.text: The name 'Elizabeth' (Greek Elisabet) comes from Hebrew 'Elisheba' meaning 'my God is an oath' or 'God is my abundance.' She was a relative of Mary (Luke 1:36).

scripture_says.text: There was a prophetess, Anna, the daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Asher. She was advanced in years, having lived with her husband seven years from when she was a virgin, and then as a widow until she was eighty-four.
She did not depart from the temple, worshiping with fasting and prayer night and day.
Coming up at that very hour she began to give thanks to God and to speak of him to all who were waiting for the redemption of Jerusalem.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Hanna' (Anna) is the same as Hebrew 'Channah' (Hannah). She is one of only three named prophetesses in the New Testament (along with the four daughters of Philip in Acts 21:9).

scripture_says.text: The twelve were with him, and also some women who had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities: Mary, called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out.

Mary Magdalene and the other Mary were sitting opposite the tomb. On the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene came to the tomb early, while it was still dark, and saw that the stone had been taken away.
Jesus appeared first to Mary Magdalene, from whom he had cast out seven demons. Jesus said to her, 'Mary.' She turned and said to him in Aramaic, 'Rabboni!' (which means Teacher).

history_records.text: 'Magdalene' indicates she was from Magdala, a town on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee. She was among the women who financially supported Jesus' ministry (Luke 8:3).

scripture_says.text: A woman named Martha received him into her house. And she had a sister called Mary, who sat at the Lord's feet and listened to his teaching.
Jesus said to Martha, 'Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many things, but one thing is necessary. Mary has chosen the good portion, which will not be taken away from her.'
Mary took a pound of expensive ointment made from pure nard, and anointed the feet of Jesus and wiped his feet with her hair. The house was filled with the fragrance of the perfume.

history_records.text: Mary of Bethany was the sister of Martha and Lazarus. Her home in Bethany, about two miles from Jerusalem, was a place of rest for Jesus.

scripture_says.text: As they went on their way, Jesus entered a village. And a woman named Martha welcomed him into her house.
Martha was distracted with much serving. And she went up to him and said, 'Lord, do you not care that my sister has left me to serve alone? Tell her then to help me.'

Jesus said to Martha, 'I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live.' Martha said, 'Yes, Lord; I believe that you are the Christ, the Son of God, who is coming into the world.'

history_records.text: The Aramaic 'Martha' means 'lady' or 'mistress.' She is portrayed as a capable hostess and her confession in John 11:27 is one of the clearest in the Gospels.

scripture_says.text: A woman from Samaria came to draw water. Jesus said to her, 'Give me a drink.' The Samaritan woman said, 'How is it that you, a Jew, ask for a drink from me, a woman of Samaria?' (For Jews have no dealings with Samaritans.)
Jesus said to her, 'Go, call your husband.' The woman answered, 'I have no husband.' Jesus said, 'You have had five husbands, and the one you now have is not your husband.'

The woman left her water jar and went away into town and said to the people, 'Come, see a man who told me all that I ever did. Can this be the Christ?' Many Samaritans from that town believed in him because of the woman's testimony.

history_records.text: The woman came at noon ('the sixth hour'), an unusual time suggesting she avoided other women. Jacob's well in Sychar was an important historical site.

scripture_says.text: Paul found a Jew named Aquila, a native of Pontus, recently come from Italy with his wife Priscilla, because Claudius had commanded all the Jews to leave Rome. He stayed with them and worked, for they were tentmakers by trade.
When Priscilla and Aquila heard Apollos speak boldly in the synagogue, they took him aside and explained to him the way of God more accurately.
Greet Prisca and Aquila, my fellow workers in Christ Jesus, who risked their necks for my life, to whom not only I give thanks but all the churches of the Gentiles give thanks as well.

history_records.text: 'Priscilla' is the diminutive of 'Prisca.' She is mentioned six times in the New Testament, four times before her husband, suggesting her prominence in ministry.

scripture_says.text: On the Sabbath day we went outside the gate to the riverside, where we supposed there was a place of prayer, and we sat down and spoke to the women who had come together. One who heard us was a woman named Lydia, from the city of Thyatira, a seller of purple goods, who was a worshiper of God.

The Lord opened her heart to pay attention to what was said by Paul. And after she was baptized, and her household as well, she urged us, saying, 'If you have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come to my house and stay.'

history_records.text: Thyatira was famous for its purple dye industry. Lydia was likely a wealthy businesswoman. Her home became the meeting place for the church in Philippi (Acts 16:40).

scripture_says.text: I commend to you our sister Phoebe, a servant of the church at Cenchreae, that you may welcome her in the Lord in a way worthy of the saints, and help her in whatever she may need from you, for she has been a patron of many and of myself as well.

history_records.text: The Greek 'diakonos' (servant/deacon) used for Phoebe is the same word used for male deacons. 'Prostatis' (patron/benefactor) indicates she was a woman of means who supported the ministry.

scripture_says.text: Greet Andronicus and Junia, my kinsmen and my fellow prisoners. They are well known to the apostles, and they were in Christ before me.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Iounian' can be read as female (Junia) or male (Junias). Early church fathers like Chrysostom identified Junia as a woman. The phrase 'well known to the apostles' indicates prominence in the early church.

question: What did people eat in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: God said, 'Behold, I have given you every plant yielding seed that is on the face of all the earth, and every tree with seed in its fruit. You shall have them for food.'
The Israelites ate bread, lentils, beans, grain, grapes, figs, pomegranates, olives, and honey. After leaving Egypt, they longed for the fish, cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic of Egypt.
John the Baptist ate locusts and wild honey. Jesus ate fish, bread, and wine.

history_records.text: The staple diet included bread, olive oil, and wine. Meat was eaten mainly at feasts. Fish was common near water bodies. Dairy included milk, curds, and cheese.

question: What did people wear in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: The LORD God made for Adam and his wife garments of skins and clothed them.
A woman shall not wear a man's garment, nor shall a man put on a woman's cloak.
John wore a garment of camel's hair and a leather belt around his waist.

history_records.text: Basic clothing included an inner tunic (ketonet/chiton), outer cloak (simlah/himation), belt, and sandals. Wool and linen were the primary fabrics. Purple indicated wealth.

question: What were houses like in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: When you build a new house, you shall make a parapet for your roof, that you may not bring the guilt of blood upon your house, if anyone should fall from it.
Four men carried a paralytic and, when they could not get near Jesus because of the crowd, they removed the roof above him and let down the bed.
Rahab let the spies down by a rope through the window, for her house was built into the city wall.

history_records.text: Typical houses had stone or mud-brick walls, flat roofs used for sleeping and storage, and small windows. Floors were often packed earth. The roof was accessed by external stairs.

question: What was family life like in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Honor your father and your mother, that your days may be long in the land that the LORD your God is giving you.
Train up a child in the way he should go; even when he is old he will not depart from it.
Children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right. Fathers, do not provoke your children to anger, but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord.

history_records.text: The family (Hebrew 'mishpachah') was the basic social unit, often including extended relatives. The father was the head of household with authority over children's marriages and property.

question: What was education like in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: These words that I command you today shall be on your heart. You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house, and when you walk by the way.
Jesus went up into the temple and began teaching. The Jews marveled, saying, 'How is it that this man has learning, when he has never studied?'
Paul said, 'I am a Jew, brought up in this city, educated at the feet of Gamaliel according to the strict manner of the law of our fathers.'

history_records.text: Primary education was the parents' responsibility, especially teaching Torah. Synagogue schools (bet sefer) developed after the exile. Advanced study was with a rabbi (bet midrash).

question: What were occupations in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Abel was a keeper of sheep, and Cain was a worker of the ground.
Jesus was known as the carpenter's son, and Joseph was a carpenter.
Paul worked with Aquila and Priscilla because they were tentmakers by trade.

history_records.text: Common occupations included shepherds, farmers, fishermen, carpenters, potters, weavers, tanners, metalworkers, merchants, and tax collectors. Many trades were hereditary.

question: What was travel like in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Abraham rose early in the morning, saddled his donkey, and took two of his young men with him, and his son Isaac.
A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and he fell among robbers, who stripped him and beat him.
Paul experienced dangers from rivers, dangers from robbers, dangers in the city, dangers in the wilderness, dangers at sea.

history_records.text: Travel was typically by foot, donkey, or camel. Roman roads improved travel in NT times. Sea travel was seasonal, avoiding winter months. Inns and caravanserai provided lodging.

question: What was agriculture like in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Isaac sowed in that land and reaped in the same year a hundredfold. The LORD blessed him.
A sower went out to sow. And as he sowed, some seeds fell along the path... some on rocky ground... some among thorns... some on good soil.
The land produces by itself, first the blade, then the ear, then the full grain in the ear. When the grain is ripe, at once he puts in the sickle, because the harvest has come.

history_records.text: The agricultural calendar followed the early rains (October-November), growing season, and late rains (March-April), then harvest. Key crops were wheat, barley, grapes, olives, and figs.

question: What were trades and crafts in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Tubal-cain was the forger of all instruments of bronze and iron.

Bezalel was filled with the Spirit of God, with skill, intelligence, and knowledge in every kind of craft, to devise artistic designs, to work in gold, silver, and bronze, in cutting stones, and in carving wood.
Demetrius, a silversmith who made silver shrines of Artemis, brought no little business to the craftsmen.

history_records.text: Specialized crafts included metalworking, pottery, weaving, dyeing, tanning, carpentry, and stonemasonry. Guilds developed for craftsmen, often located in specific city quarters.

question: What were marriage customs in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Abraham said to his servant, 'Go to my country and to my kindred, and take a wife for my son Isaac.'
Jacob loved Rachel. He said to Laban, 'I will serve you seven years for your younger daughter Rachel.'
At the wedding in Cana, when the wine ran out, Jesus turned water into wine. The master of the feast said, 'You have kept the good wine until now.'

history_records.text: Marriage involved betrothal (binding legal agreement), payment of bride price (mohar), and a wedding feast lasting up to seven days. Marriages were typically arranged by families.

question: What were burial customs in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah east of Mamre in the land of Canaan.
Joseph took the body of Jesus, wrapped it in a clean linen shroud and laid it in his own new tomb, which he had cut in the rock. He rolled a great stone to the entrance of the tomb.
Lazarus had already been in the tomb four days. Martha said, 'Lord, by this time there will be an odor, for he has been dead four days.'

history_records.text: Burial occurred on the same day due to rapid decomposition in the hot climate. Bodies were washed, wrapped in linen with spices, and placed in tombs or caves. Professional mourners were hired.

question: What were hospitality customs in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Abraham ran to meet the three visitors and bowed himself to the earth. He said, 'Let a little water be brought, and wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree.' He hastened to prepare a meal for them.
Jesus said to Simon, 'You gave me no water for my feet, but she has wet my feet with her tears. You gave me no kiss, but from the time I came in she has not ceased to kiss my feet.'
Do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares.

history_records.text: Hospitality was a sacred duty in the ancient Near East. It included providing water for washing feet, food, lodging, and protection for travelers. Refusing hospitality was shameful.

question: What were greeting customs in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Esau ran to meet Jacob and embraced him and fell on his neck and kissed him, and they wept.
Greet one another with a holy kiss.
The Pharisees love greetings in the marketplaces and to be called rabbi by others.

history_records.text: Common greetings included 'Shalom' (peace), bowing, embracing, and kissing on the cheek. The depth of the bow indicated relative social status.

question: What were gift-giving customs in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Jacob sent a present to Esau his brother: two hundred female goats and twenty male goats, two hundred ewes and twenty rams, thirty milking camels and their calves, forty cows and ten bulls.
The wise men opened their treasures and offered Jesus gifts: gold, frankincense, and myrrh.
Naaman brought ten talents of silver, six thousand shekels of gold, and ten changes of clothes as a gift.

history_records.text: Gifts established or maintained relationships, showed respect, sealed agreements, and were expected when visiting dignitaries. Refusing a gift was insulting.

question: What were mourning customs in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Jacob tore his garments and put sackcloth on his loins and mourned for his son many days.
Job arose and tore his robe and shaved his head and fell on the ground and worshiped.
When Jesus arrived at Jairus's house, he saw the flute players and the crowd making a commotion. He said, 'Go away, for the girl is not dead but sleeping.'

history_records.text: Mourning customs included tearing garments, wearing sackcloth, putting ashes or dust on the head, fasting, weeping, and hiring professional mourners with flutes.

question: What were birth customs in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: The midwives said to Pharaoh, 'The Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women, for they are vigorous and give birth before the midwife comes to them.'
When the child was born, Mary wrapped him in swaddling cloths and laid him in a manger.
On the eighth day they came to circumcise the child. They would have called him Zechariah after his father, but his mother said, 'No; he shall be called John.'

history_records.text: After birth, the baby was washed, rubbed with salt (thought to firm the skin), and wrapped tightly in cloth bands (swaddling). Circumcision and naming occurred on the eighth day.

question: What were name-giving customs in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Adam called his wife's name Eve, because she was the mother of all living.
Rachel named her son Ben-oni (son of my sorrow), but his father called him Benjamin (son of the right hand).
An angel said to Mary, 'You shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins.'

history_records.text: Hebrew names often expressed circumstances of birth, parental hopes, or theological meaning. Names could be changed to mark significant events (Abram to Abraham, Jacob to Israel).

question: What were covenant-making customs in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: The LORD said to Abram, 'Bring me a heifer three years old, a female goat three years old, a ram three years old, a turtledove, and a young pigeon.' He cut them in half and laid each half over against the other.
Laban said to Jacob, 'Come now, let us make a covenant, you and I.' So Jacob took a stone and set it up as a pillar. They gathered stones, made a heap, and ate there by the heap.
Moses took the blood and threw it on the people and said, 'Behold the blood of the covenant that the LORD has made with you.'

history_records.text: Covenant ceremonies often involved sacrifice, a shared meal, exchange of gifts, calling witnesses, setting up memorial stones, and invoking divine sanctions for breaking the covenant.

question: What was the role of kings in Israel?

scripture_says.text: The elders of Israel said to Samuel, 'Appoint for us a king to judge us like all the nations.' Samuel warned them what a king would do: take their sons, daughters, fields, and a tenth of their grain and flocks.
The king was to write a copy of the law, read it all the days of his life, keep all its statutes, and not consider himself above his brothers.
David was king over all Israel, and he administered justice and equity to all his people.

history_records.text: The king was anointed (Hebrew 'mashiach') and served as military leader, chief judge, and covenant representative. Unlike pagan kings, Israel's king was under God's law.

question: What was the role of judges?

scripture_says.text: The LORD raised up judges, who saved them out of the hand of those who plundered them.
Deborah, a prophetess, the wife of Lappidoth, was judging Israel at that time. She used to sit under the palm of Deborah, and the people of Israel came up to her for judgment.
Samuel judged Israel all the days of his life. He went on a circuit year by year to Bethel, Gilgal, and Mizpah, and judged Israel in all these places.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'shophetim' (judges) were military deliverers and civil leaders raised up during the period between Joshua and the monarchy. They administered justice and led in battle.

question: What was the role of elders?

scripture_says.text: Moses chose able men out of all Israel and made them heads over the people, chiefs of thousands, of hundreds, of fifties, and of tens. They judged the people at all times.
The elders of Israel assembled and came to Samuel at Ramah and said, 'Now appoint for us a king.'
Paul and Barnabas appointed elders for them in every church, with prayer and fasting.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'zaqen' (elder) referred to mature men who served as community leaders, advisors, and judges at city gates. The 70 elders assisted Moses in governing Israel.

scripture_says.text: When day came, the council of the elders of the people gathered together, both chief priests and scribes, and they led Jesus away to their council.

The high priest said, 'I adjure you by the living God, tell us if you are the Christ, the Son of God.' Jesus said, 'You have said so.' Then the high priest tore his robes and said, 'He has uttered blasphemy.'

Gamaliel, a Pharisee in the council, a teacher of the law held in honor by all the people, said, 'If this plan or this undertaking is of man, it will fail; but if it is of God, you will not be able to overthrow them.'

history_records.text: The Sanhedrin (Greek 'synedrion') was the supreme Jewish court of 71 members, led by the high priest. It handled religious and civil matters within limits set by Rome.

scripture_says.text: In those days a decree went out from Caesar Augustus that all the world should be registered.
Jesus said to them, 'Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's.'
Paul said, 'I appeal to Caesar.' Then Festus answered, 'To Caesar you have appealed; to Caesar you shall go.'

history_records.text: Rome governed through the emperor (Caesar), the Senate, governors (proconsuls, procurators, or prefects), and local client kings like Herod. Roman citizenship provided legal protections.

question: What was the role of governors?

scripture_says.text: In the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate being governor of Judea...

Pilate said to Jesus, 'Do you not know that I have authority to release you and authority to crucify you?' Jesus answered, 'You would have no authority over me at all unless it had been given you from above.'
Felix the governor sent for Paul and heard him speak about faith in Christ Jesus.

history_records.text: Roman governors (Greek 'hegemon') administered provinces, collected taxes, commanded troops, and rendered judicial decisions. Judea had procurators/prefects after Archelaus was deposed in 6 AD.

question: What was the role of tax collectors?

scripture_says.text: As Jesus passed on, he saw a man called Matthew sitting at the tax booth, and he said to him, 'Follow me.' And he rose and followed him.
Zacchaeus was a chief tax collector and was rich. He said to the Lord, 'Behold, Lord, the half of my goods I give to the poor. And if I have defrauded anyone of anything, I restore it fourfold.'
The Pharisees said to his disciples, 'Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?'

history_records.text: The Greek 'telones' (tax collector) worked for the Roman system, collecting customs, tolls, and taxes. They often overcharged for profit and were despised as collaborators and extortioners.

question: What was the role of soldiers?

scripture_says.text: Soldiers asked John, 'What shall we do?' He said to them, 'Do not extort money from anyone by threats or false accusation, and be content with your wages.'
A centurion came forward, appealing to Jesus, 'Lord, my servant is lying paralyzed at home, suffering terribly.' Jesus marveled and said, 'With no one in Israel have I found such faith.'
The soldiers plaited a crown of thorns and put it on his head. They struck him with their hands and mocked him.

history_records.text: Roman soldiers served in legions (about 6,000 men) and auxiliary units. A centurion commanded 100 soldiers. Soldiers maintained order, guarded prisoners, and executed sentences.

scripture_says.text: The LORD said to Moses, 'Let them make me a sanctuary, that I may dwell in their midst. Exactly as I show you concerning the pattern of the tabernacle, and all its furniture, so you shall make it.'
The cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle. Moses was not able to enter the tent of meeting because the cloud settled on it.
Stephen said, 'Our fathers had the tent of witness in the wilderness, as he who spoke to Moses directed him to make it, according to the pattern that he had seen.'

history_records.text: The tabernacle (Hebrew 'mishkan,' dwelling) was a portable sanctuary used from Sinai until Solomon built the temple. It symbolized God's presence among His people.

scripture_says.text: Solomon began to build the house of the LORD in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah. The house that I am to build will be great, for our God is greater than all gods.
Jesus entered the temple and drove out all who sold and bought in the temple. He said, 'My house shall be called a house of prayer, but you make it a den of robbers.'
Jesus said, 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.' He was speaking about the temple of his body.

history_records.text: Three temples stood in Jerusalem: Solomon's temple (destroyed 586 BC), the Second Temple rebuilt by Zerubbabel (516 BC) and greatly expanded by Herod (destroyed 70 AD).

scripture_says.text: Jesus went throughout all Galilee, teaching in their synagogues and proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom.
Jesus came to Nazareth, where he had been brought up. As was his custom, he went to the synagogue on the Sabbath day, and he stood up to read.
Paul went into the synagogue and for three months spoke boldly, reasoning and persuading them about the kingdom of God.

history_records.text: The Greek 'synagoge' (gathering place) developed during the Babylonian exile when there was no temple. Synagogues required 10 adult men and served for Scripture reading, prayer, and instruction.

question: What was the role of priests?

scripture_says.text: You shall bring Aaron and his sons to the entrance of the tent of meeting and wash them with water and put on Aaron the holy garments. You shall anoint him and consecrate him, that he may serve me as priest.
The priests' lips should guard knowledge, and people should seek instruction from his mouth, for he is the messenger of the LORD of hosts.
Zechariah, a priest of the division of Abijah, was chosen by lot to enter the temple of the Lord and burn incense.

history_records.text: Priests (Hebrew 'kohanim') were descendants of Aaron who offered sacrifices, maintained the sanctuary, taught the law, and pronounced blessings. They served in courses (1 Chronicles 24).

question: What was the role of Levites?

scripture_says.text: The LORD said to Aaron, 'I have taken your brothers the Levites from among the people of Israel. They are a gift to you, given to the LORD, to do the service of the tent of meeting.'
The Levites are to perform the service of the tent of meeting. They shall be in charge of the tent of meeting and all its furnishings, and minister to the priests.
David told the leaders of the Levites to appoint their brothers as singers who should play loudly on musical instruments, on harps, lyres, and cymbals, to raise sounds of joy.

history_records.text: The Levites were descendants of Levi who served as assistants to the priests. They transported the tabernacle, served as musicians, gatekeepers, teachers, and scribes.

question: What was the role of prophets?

scripture_says.text: The LORD said to Moses, 'I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brothers. I will put my words in his mouth, and he shall speak to them all that I command him.'
The word of the LORD came to Jeremiah: 'Before I formed you in the womb I knew you, and before you were born I consecrated you; I appointed you a prophet to the nations.'
Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'navi' (prophet) was God's spokesman, receiving divine messages through visions, dreams, or direct speech. Prophets called Israel to repentance and foretold future events.

scripture_says.text: Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you tithe mint and dill and cumin, and have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness.
The Pharisees and Sadducees came, and to test Jesus they asked him to show them a sign from heaven.
Paul said, 'I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees. It is with respect to the hope and the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial.'

history_records.text: The Pharisees (Hebrew 'perushim,' separated ones) were a Jewish sect that emphasized strict observance of the written and oral Torah. Josephus estimates there were about 6,000 Pharisees.

scripture_says.text: The same day Sadducees came to him, who say that there is no resurrection, and they asked him a question.
The Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, nor angel, nor spirit, but the Pharisees acknowledge them all.
The high priest rose up, and all who were with him (that is, the party of the Sadducees), and filled with jealousy they arrested the apostles.

history_records.text: The Sadducees were a Jewish aristocratic party, largely priests and wealthy families. They accepted only the written Torah, denied resurrection, and cooperated with Rome.

scripture_says.text: Scripture does not directly name the Essenes. They are known from historical sources.

history_records.text: The Essenes were a Jewish sect known for communal living, strict purity, and copying Scripture. They are likely connected to the Dead Sea Scrolls community at Qumran. Josephus, Philo, and Pliny describe them.
sources: Philo, Every Good Man is Free 75-91

scripture_says.text: One of the twelve apostles was Simon who was called the Zealot.
Gamaliel mentioned Judas the Galilean, who rose up in the days of the census and drew away some of the people after him. He too perished, and all who followed him were scattered.

history_records.text: The Zealots (Greek 'zelotes') were Jewish nationalists who advocated armed resistance against Rome. Founded by Judas of Galilee in 6 AD, they were key instigators of the Jewish revolt (66-70 AD).

scripture_says.text: When Jesus finished these sayings, the crowds were astonished at his teaching, for he was teaching them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes.
Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you shut the kingdom of heaven in people's faces.
Ezra the scribe stood on a wooden platform and opened the book in the sight of all the people. When he opened it, all the people stood.

history_records.text: Scribes (Greek 'grammateis,' Hebrew 'sopherim') were professional interpreters and copyists of the law. They developed after the exile and were experts in legal questions.

scripture_says.text: Jesus said to them, 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst.'
Man does not live by bread alone, but man lives by every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD.
Jesus took bread, and after blessing it broke it and gave it to the disciples, and said, 'Take, eat; this is my body.'

history_records.text: Bread was the staple food of the ancient Near East. The Hebrew 'lechem' often refers to food in general. Jesus calls himself 'the bread of life' (Joh 6:35).

scripture_says.text: He took a cup, and when he had given thanks he gave it to them, saying, 'Drink of it, all of you, for this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.'
Wine gladdens the heart of man, oil to make his face shine, and bread to strengthen man's heart.
On this mountain the LORD of hosts will make for all peoples a feast of rich food, a feast of well-aged wine.

history_records.text: Wine represented joy, abundance, and blessing in Scripture. The Hebrew 'yayin' and Greek 'oinos' can refer to both fermented and fresh grape products.

scripture_says.text: Jesus answered, 'Everyone who drinks of this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again. The water I give will become a spring of water welling up to eternal life.'
I will pour water on the thirsty land, and streams on the dry ground; I will pour my Spirit upon your offspring.
He saved us through the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit.

history_records.text: Water appears in purification contexts (Eze 36:25), as 'living water' (Joh 7:38), and connected to the Spirit (Joh 7:39). Ritual washing was central to Jewish purification rites.

scripture_says.text: The life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life.
