Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.
The blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin.

history_records.text: Blood (Hebrew 'dam') represents life itself. God forbade eating blood because life belongs to Him. The sacrificial blood covered sin and established covenant.

scripture_says.text: Samuel took the horn of oil and anointed him in the midst of his brothers. And the Spirit of the LORD rushed upon David from that day forward.
You anoint my head with oil; my cup overflows.
God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power.

history_records.text: Oil (Hebrew 'shemen') symbolized the Holy Spirit, consecration, and blessing. Kings, priests, and prophets were anointed with oil. 'Messiah' means 'anointed one.'

scripture_says.text: The angel of the LORD appeared to Moses in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush. The bush was burning, yet it was not consumed.
Our God is a consuming fire.
Divided tongues as of fire appeared to them and rested on each one of them. And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'esh' appears over 370 times. Fire appears at the burning bush (Exo 3), on Sinai (Exo 19:18), consuming sacrifices (Lev 9:24), and at Pentecost (Acts 2:3).

scripture_says.text: God said, 'Let there be light,' and there was light. And God saw that the light was good. And God separated the light from the darkness.
Jesus spoke to them, saying, 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.'
God is light, and in him is no darkness at all.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'or' appears in creation (Gen 1:3), in 'the LORD is my light' (Psa 27:1), and Jesus' 'I am the light of the world' (Joh 8:12). Light/darkness contrast appears throughout scripture.

scripture_says.text: Darkness was over the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters.
This is the judgment: the light has come into the world, and people loved the darkness rather than the light because their works were evil.
We do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'choshek' appears at creation (Gen 1:2), in the plague on Egypt (Exo 10:21-22), and at the crucifixion (Mat 27:45).

scripture_says.text: Abraham said, 'God will provide for himself the lamb for a burnt offering, my son.'
The next day John saw Jesus coming toward him, and said, 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!'
You were ransomed with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot.

history_records.text: The lamb was the primary sacrificial animal, especially for Passover. The Greek 'amnos' (lamb) is used of Christ as the ultimate sacrifice that fulfills all lamb offerings.

scripture_says.text: Judah is a lion's cub; from the prey, my son, you have gone up. He stooped down; he crouched as a lion and as a lioness; who dares rouse him?
One of the elders said to me, 'Weep no more; behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered.'
Be sober-minded; be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour.

history_records.text: Rev 5:5 calls Jesus 'the Lion of the tribe of Judah.' 1 Pet 5:8 describes Satan as 'a roaring lion.' Lions were common in ancient Israel but are now extinct there.

scripture_says.text: Now the serpent was more crafty than any other beast of the field that the LORD God had made. He said to the woman, 'Did God actually say...?'
That ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan, the deceiver of the whole world - he was thrown down to the earth.
As Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'nachash' appears in Eden (Gen 3), as the bronze serpent (Num 21:8-9), and in wisdom sayings (Mat 10:16). Revelation 12:9 identifies the serpent with Satan.

scripture_says.text: Noah sent forth a dove from him, to see if the waters had subsided. The dove came back to him in the evening, and behold, in her mouth was a freshly plucked olive leaf.
When Jesus was baptized, immediately he went up from the water, and behold, the heavens were opened, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and coming to rest on him.
Be wise as serpents and innocent as doves.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'yonah' appears with Noah (Gen 8:8-12), at Jesus' baptism (Mat 3:16), and in 'innocent as doves' (Mat 10:16). Doves were acceptable sacrifices for the poor (Lev 5:7).

scripture_says.text: You yourselves have seen what I did to the Egyptians, and how I bore you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself.
They who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings like eagles.
The first living creature was like a lion, the second like an ox, the third had the face of a man, and the fourth was like a flying eagle.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'nesher' appears in 'wings like eagles' (Isa 40:31), God carrying Israel 'on eagles' wings' (Exo 19:4), and among unclean birds (Lev 11:13).

scripture_says.text: The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want. He makes me lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside still waters.
Jesus said, 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.'
When the chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'roeh' is applied to God (Psa 23:1), to kings (2 Sam 5:2), to religious leaders (Jer 23:1-4), and Jesus calls himself 'the good shepherd' (Joh 10:11).

scripture_says.text: You brought a vine out of Egypt; you drove out the nations and planted it.
Jesus said, 'I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser. I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit.'
Israel is a luxuriant vine that yields its fruit. The more his fruit increased, the more altars he built.

history_records.text: The vine (Hebrew 'gephen') symbolized Israel and later Christ. Viticulture was central to Israelite agriculture. The vine and vineyard appear frequently as metaphors for God's people.

scripture_says.text: Out of the ground the LORD God made to spring up every tree that is pleasant to the sight and good for food. The tree of life was in the midst of the garden, and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
He is like a tree planted by streams of water that yields its fruit in its season, and its leaf does not wither.
On either side of the river, the tree of life with its twelve kinds of fruit, yielding its fruit each month. The leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations.

history_records.text: Trees symbolize life, prosperity, and rootedness. The tree of life represents eternal life. Various trees (olive, fig, cedar) carry specific symbolic meanings throughout Scripture.

scripture_says.text: The LORD is my rock and my fortress and my deliverer, my God, my rock, in whom I take refuge.
They all drank the same spiritual drink. For they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ.
Everyone who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'tsur' and 'sela' are applied to God (Deu 32:4, Psa 18:2). Moses struck the rock (Exo 17:6, Num 20:11). Paul writes 'that Rock was Christ' (1 Cor 10:4).

scripture_says.text: Moses went up to God. The LORD called to him out of the mountain, saying, 'Thus you shall say to the house of Israel.'

It shall come to pass in the latter days that the mountain of the house of the LORD shall be established as the highest of the mountains, and shall be lifted up above the hills; and all the nations shall flow to it.
Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a high mountain by themselves. And he was transfigured before them.

history_records.text: Mountains symbolize God's presence, revelation, and power. Key events occurred on mountains: Sinai (law), Carmel (Elijah), the Mount of Transfiguration, and the Mount of Olives.

scripture_says.text: The earth was without form and void, and darkness was over the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters.
The wicked are like the tossing sea; for it cannot be quiet, and its waters toss up mire and dirt.
Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away, and the sea was no more.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'yam' appears in creation (Gen 1:10), parting of the Red Sea (Exo 14), Jesus calming the storm (Mar 4:39), and 'the sea was no more' (Rev 21:1).

scripture_says.text: The LORD your God has blessed you in all the work of your hands. He knows your going through this great wilderness. These forty years the LORD your God has been with you.
A voice cries: 'In the wilderness prepare the way of the LORD; make straight in the desert a highway for our God.'
Jesus was led up by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil.

history_records.text: The wilderness (Hebrew 'midbar') represents testing, dependence on God, and preparation for service. Israel's 40 years, Elijah's journey, and Jesus' temptation all occur in the wilderness.

question: What is the typology of Adam?

scripture_says.text: Adam was a type of the one who was to come. But the free gift is not like the trespass. For if many died through one man's trespass, much more have the grace of God and the free gift by the grace of that one man Jesus Christ abounded for many.
As in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive. The first man Adam became a living being; the last Adam became a life-giving spirit.

history_records.text: Paul explicitly calls Adam a 'type' (Greek 'typos') of Christ. Both are heads of humanity - Adam brought death through disobedience, Christ brings life through obedience.

question: What is the typology of Noah?

scripture_says.text: By faith Noah, being warned by God concerning events as yet unseen, in reverent fear constructed an ark for the saving of his household.

God's patience waited in the days of Noah, while the ark was being prepared, in which a few, that is, eight persons, were brought safely through water. Baptism, which corresponds to this, now saves you.

history_records.text: 1 Peter 3:20-21 connects Noah's flood with baptism. Jesus references 'the days of Noah' in Matthew 24:37-39.

question: What is the typology of Abraham?

scripture_says.text: By faith Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac, and he who had received the promises was in the act of offering up his only son. He considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead, from which, figuratively speaking, he did receive him back.
Know then that it is those of faith who are the sons of Abraham. In Christ Jesus the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles.

history_records.text: Hebrews 11:17-19 describes Abraham receiving Isaac back 'figuratively.' Romans 4:16-17 calls Abraham 'the father of us all.'

question: What is the typology of Isaac?

scripture_says.text: Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac, his only son. He considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead, from which, figuratively speaking, he did receive him back.
Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son. Abraham said, 'God will provide for himself the lamb for a burnt offering, my son.'

history_records.text: Genesis 22 records Isaac carrying wood up Mount Moriah as his father's 'only son' (22:2). Hebrews 11:19 says Abraham received Isaac back 'figuratively speaking.'

question: What is the typology of Joseph?

scripture_says.text: Joseph's brothers were jealous of him. They sold Joseph into Egypt, but God was with him and rescued him out of all his afflictions.
Joseph said to his brothers, 'As for you, you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring it about that many people should be kept alive.'

history_records.text: Stephen references Joseph's story in Acts 7:9-14. Joseph was sold by his brothers (Gen 37:28), falsely accused (Gen 39:20), exalted to rule (Gen 41:41), and forgave his brothers (Gen 45:5).

question: What is the typology of Moses?

scripture_says.text: The LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you, from your brothers - it is to him you shall listen.
Moses was faithful in all God's house as a servant, to testify to the things that were to be spoken later, but Christ is faithful over God's house as a son.
By faith Moses left Egypt, not being afraid of the anger of the king. He considered the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt.

history_records.text: Moses prophesied 'a prophet like me' (Deu 18:15). Acts 3:22-23 and 7:37 apply this to Jesus. Both were threatened in infancy, came out of Egypt, and fasted 40 days.

question: What is the typology of Joshua?

scripture_says.text: Moses the servant of the LORD died there in the land of Moab. Joshua the son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom, for Moses had laid his hands on him. The people of Israel obeyed him.
For if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on. So then, there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God.

history_records.text: Joshua (Hebrew 'Yehoshua') shares the same name as Jesus (Greek 'Iesous'). He led Israel into the Promised Land as Jesus leads believers into eternal rest. Joshua succeeded Moses as Jesus fulfills the Law.

question: What is the typology of David?

scripture_says.text: I have found in David the son of Jesse a man after my heart, who will do all my will. Of this man's offspring God has brought to Israel a Savior, Jesus.
He raised up David to be their king, of whom he testified, 'I have found David the son of Jesse, a man after my heart.'
The book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham.

history_records.text: 2 Samuel 7:12-16 records God's covenant with David promising an eternal throne. The NT identifies Jesus as 'Son of David' (Mat 1:1, Rom 1:3).

question: What is the typology of Solomon?

scripture_says.text: The queen of the South came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.
I will raise up your offspring after you, who shall come from your body, and I will establish his kingdom. He shall build a house for my name, and I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever.

history_records.text: Solomon's name means 'peace' (Hebrew 'Shelomo'). Jesus said 'something greater than Solomon is here' (Mat 12:42). Isaiah 9:6 prophesied a 'Prince of Peace.'

question: What is the typology of Jonah?

scripture_says.text: Jesus answered them, 'An evil and adulterous generation seeks for a sign, but no sign will be given to it except the sign of the prophet Jonah. For just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.'
The men of Nineveh will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and behold, something greater than Jonah is here.

history_records.text: Jesus explicitly identifies Jonah's experience as a sign pointing to His death, burial, and resurrection. Jonah's preaching brought Gentile repentance, prefiguring the gospel going to all nations.

question: What is the typology of the tabernacle?

scripture_says.text: They serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things. For when Moses was about to erect the tent, he was instructed by God, saying, 'See that you make everything according to the pattern that was shown you on the mountain.'
Christ has entered, not into holy places made with hands, which are copies of the true things, but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf.
The Word became flesh and dwelt (tabernacled) among us, and we have seen his glory.

history_records.text: Every element of the tabernacle - the courtyard, altar, laver, lampstand, table, incense altar, veil, and ark - points to aspects of Christ's person and work.

question: What is the typology of the temple?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said, 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.' He was speaking about the temple of his body.
Do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you?

You are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God, built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone, in whom the whole structure grows into a holy temple in the Lord.

history_records.text: Jesus speaks of 'the temple of his body' (Joh 2:21). Paul calls believers 'a temple of the Holy Spirit' (1 Cor 6:19). The veil was torn at Christ's death (Mat 27:51).

question: What is the typology of the sacrifices?

scripture_says.text: For since the law has but a shadow of the good things to come instead of the true form of these realities, it can never, by the same sacrifices that are continually offered every year, make perfect those who draw near.
We have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.
Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.

history_records.text: The five Levitical offerings (burnt, grain, peace, sin, guilt) all point to aspects of Christ's sacrifice. The burnt offering represents total dedication; the sin offering represents substitutionary atonement.

question: What is the typology of the priesthood?

scripture_says.text: We have such a high priest, one who is seated at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven, a minister in the holy places, in the true tent that the Lord set up, not man.

He holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever. Consequently, he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them.
You are a priest forever, after the order of Melchizedek.

history_records.text: The Aaronic priesthood's limitations (death, sinfulness, repeated offerings) point to Christ's superior priesthood after the order of Melchizedek - eternal, sinless, offering once for all.

question: What is the typology of the Passover?

scripture_says.text: The blood shall be a sign for you, on the houses where you are. And when I see the blood, I will pass over you, and no plague will befall you to destroy you.
Cleanse out the old leaven that you may be a new lump, as you really are unleavened. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.
You were ransomed with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot.

history_records.text: The Passover lamb's blood protected from judgment; Christ's blood delivers from God's wrath. Jesus was crucified at Passover. John records Jesus' death coinciding with the slaughter of Passover lambs.

question: What is the typology of the manna?

scripture_says.text: Jesus said to them, 'Truly, truly, I say to you, it was not Moses who gave you the bread from heaven, but my Father gives you the true bread from heaven. For the bread of God is he who comes down from heaven and gives life to the world.'
Your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, and they died. I am the living bread that came down from heaven. If anyone eats of this bread, he will live forever.

history_records.text: The manna (Hebrew 'man hu?' - 'What is it?') sustained physical life in the wilderness. Jesus identifies Himself as the true bread from heaven that sustains eternal life.

question: What is the typology of the bronze serpent?

scripture_says.text: As Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life.
The LORD said to Moses, 'Make a fiery serpent and set it on a pole, and everyone who is bitten, when he sees it, shall live.' So Moses made a bronze serpent.

history_records.text: Jesus explicitly identifies the bronze serpent as a type of His crucifixion. Just as looking at the serpent brought physical healing, looking to Christ in faith brings spiritual life.

question: What is the typology of the cities of refuge?

scripture_says.text: The LORD said to Moses, 'Speak to the people of Israel and say to them, When you cross the Jordan into the land of Canaan, then you shall select cities to be cities of refuge for you, that the manslayer who kills any person without intent may flee there.'
We who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us. We have this as a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul.

history_records.text: Six cities of refuge provided protection for those who unintentionally caused death. Christ is the believer's refuge from the avenger (divine judgment). The cities were accessible from anywhere in Israel.

scripture_says.text: Adam was the first man, formed from the dust of the ground.
God breathed into his nostrils the breath of life.
He was placed in the Garden of Eden to work it and keep it.
Eve was made from his rib as a helper fit for him.
After eating the forbidden fruit, he was expelled from Eden.
He lived 930 years and had sons and daughters.
Paul calls him 'the first man Adam' and contrasts him with Christ.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'adam' (אָדָם) means 'man' or 'mankind,' related to 'adamah' (ground).

scripture_says.text: Cain was the firstborn son of Adam and Eve.
He was a worker of the ground.
His offering was not accepted by the LORD, while Abel's was.
He killed his brother Abel and was cursed to be a fugitive.
God set a mark on him so no one would kill him.
He built a city and named it after his son Enoch.
John says Cain 'was of the evil one' and killed Abel because his deeds were evil.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Qayin' (קַיִן) is related to 'qanah' meaning 'to acquire' or 'to get.'

scripture_says.text: Abel was the second son of Adam and Eve.
He was a keeper of sheep.
His offering of the firstborn of his flock was accepted by the LORD.
He was killed by his brother Cain.
His blood cried out from the ground.
Hebrews says 'by faith Abel offered... a more acceptable sacrifice.'
Jesus calls him 'righteous Abel.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Hevel' (הֶבֶל) means 'breath' or 'vapor.'

scripture_says.text: Seth was born to Adam and Eve after Abel's death.
Eve said God 'appointed' him as another offspring in place of Abel.
Adam was 130 years old when Seth was born.
Seth lived 912 years.
He fathered Enosh, and in that time 'people began to call upon the name of the LORD.'
He is in the genealogy of Jesus through Luke.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Sheth' (שֵׁת) means 'appointed' or 'set.'

scripture_says.text: Enoch was the son of Jared, seventh from Adam.
He 'walked with God' for 300 years after fathering Methuselah.
He lived 365 years, then 'he was not, for God took him.'
Hebrews says 'by faith Enoch was taken up so that he should not see death.'
Jude quotes a prophecy attributed to Enoch about the Lord's coming.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Chanokh' (חֲנוֹךְ) means 'dedicated' or 'initiated.'

scripture_says.text: Methuselah was the son of Enoch.
He lived 969 years, the longest lifespan recorded in scripture.
He fathered Lamech when he was 187 years old.
He is in the genealogy of Jesus through Luke.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Methushelach' (מְתוּשֶׁלַח) may mean 'man of the dart' or 'when he dies, it shall be sent.'

scripture_says.text: Noah was the son of Lamech, tenth from Adam.
He 'found favor in the eyes of the LORD' and was 'a righteous man, blameless in his generation.'
He 'walked with God.'
God commanded him to build an ark to survive the flood.
He was 600 years old when the flood came.
After the flood, God made a covenant with him, setting the rainbow as a sign.
He planted a vineyard and became drunk.
He lived 950 years.
Peter calls him 'a herald of righteousness.'
Hebrews says 'by faith Noah... constructed an ark.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Noach' (נֹחַ) means 'rest' or 'comfort.'
Flood narratives appear in many ancient Near Eastern texts, including the Epic of Gilgamesh (Utnapishtim) and the Atrahasis Epic.

scripture_says.text: Shem was one of Noah's three sons.
He entered the ark with his wife.
He and Japheth covered their father's nakedness.
Noah blessed him: 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem.'
He lived 600 years.
He is the ancestor of the Semitic peoples and is in the genealogy of Jesus.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Shem' (שֵׁם) means 'name' or 'renown.'
The term 'Semitic' (referring to languages and peoples) derives from Shem.

scripture_says.text: Ham was one of Noah's three sons.
He entered the ark with his wife.
He saw his father's nakedness and told his brothers.
Noah cursed Canaan (Ham's son): 'a servant of servants shall he be.'
His sons were Cush, Egypt (Mizraim), Put, and Canaan.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Cham' (חָם) may mean 'hot' or 'warm.'
Egypt is called 'the land of Ham' in the Psalms.

scripture_says.text: Japheth was one of Noah's three sons.
He entered the ark with his wife.
He and Shem covered their father's nakedness.
Noah blessed him: 'May God enlarge Japheth, and let him dwell in the tents of Shem.'
His sons spread to the coastlands and isles.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yepheth' (יֶפֶת) is related to 'pathah' meaning 'to enlarge' or 'expand.'
Japheth is traditionally associated with the Indo-European peoples.

scripture_says.text: Nimrod was the son of Cush, grandson of Ham.
He was 'the first on earth to be a mighty man.'
He was 'a mighty hunter before the LORD.'
His kingdom began with Babel, Erech, Accad, and Calneh in Shinar.
He built Nineveh, Rehoboth-Ir, Calah, and Resen in Assyria.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Nimrod' (נִמְרוֹד) may be related to 'marad' meaning 'to rebel.'
Micah refers to Assyria as 'the land of Nimrod.'

scripture_says.text: Originally named Abram, from Ur of the Chaldeans.
God called him to leave his country and go to a land He would show him.
God promised to make him a great nation and bless all families of the earth through him.
He believed God and 'it was counted to him as righteousness.'
God changed his name from Abram to Abraham, 'father of a multitude.'
He received circumcision as a sign of the covenant.
Isaac was born to him and Sarah when Abraham was 100 years old.
He was tested when commanded to sacrifice Isaac.
He lived 175 years and was buried in the cave of Machpelah.
Called 'the friend of God.'

history_records.text: 'Abram' (אַבְרָם) means 'exalted father'; 'Abraham' (אַבְרָהָם) means 'father of a multitude.'
Ur of the Chaldeans has been identified with Tell el-Muqayyar in southern Iraq.

scripture_says.text: Ishmael was the son of Abraham by Hagar.
The angel said he would be 'a wild donkey of a man, his hand against everyone.'
He was circumcised at age 13 when God established the covenant.
God promised to make him fruitful and the father of twelve princes and a great nation.
He and Hagar were sent away after Isaac was weaned.
He became an archer and lived in the wilderness of Paran.
He lived 137 years.
He and Isaac buried Abraham.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yishma'el' (יִשְׁמָעֵאל) means 'God hears.'
Arab tradition traces descent from Ishmael.

scripture_says.text: Isaac was the promised son of Abraham and Sarah, born when Abraham was 100.
His name means 'he laughs' because Sarah laughed at the promise.
Abraham was commanded to sacrifice him on Mount Moriah, but God provided a ram.
He married Rebekah, whom Abraham's servant found.
Rebekah was barren; Isaac prayed and she conceived twins.
He favored Esau, while Rebekah favored Jacob.
He blessed Jacob (thinking him Esau) with the firstborn blessing.
He lived 180 years and was buried by Esau and Jacob.
Hebrews says 'by faith Isaac invoked future blessings on Jacob and Esau.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yitschaq' (יִצְחָק) means 'he laughs.'

scripture_says.text: Esau was the firstborn twin son of Isaac and Rebekah.
He came out red and hairy, so they named him Esau.
He was a skillful hunter, a man of the field.
He sold his birthright to Jacob for a meal of lentil stew.
He married Hittite women, which grieved his parents.
Jacob deceived Isaac and received Esau's blessing.
He planned to kill Jacob but later reconciled with him.
He became the father of the Edomites.
Hebrews calls him 'unholy' for selling his birthright.
Malachi records God saying 'Esau I have hated.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Esav' (עֵשָׂו) may mean 'hairy.' He is also called Edom (אֱדוֹם), meaning 'red.'

scripture_says.text: Jacob was the second-born twin of Isaac and Rebekah, grasping Esau's heel.
He was 'a quiet man, dwelling in tents.'
He bought Esau's birthright for lentil stew.
He deceived Isaac and received the firstborn blessing.
He saw a ladder to heaven with angels at Bethel.
He worked 14 years for Laban to marry Leah and Rachel.
He had twelve sons who became the twelve tribes of Israel.
He wrestled with God at Peniel and was renamed Israel ('he strives with God').
He lived 147 years and was buried in the cave of Machpelah.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Ya'aqov' (יַעֲקֹב) means 'heel-grabber' or 'supplanter.' 'Israel' (יִשְׂרָאֵל) means 'he strives with God.'

scripture_says.text: Laban was the brother of Rebekah and son of Bethuel.
He welcomed Abraham's servant seeking a wife for Isaac.
Jacob fled to him and worked for his two daughters.
He deceived Jacob by giving Leah instead of Rachel.
He changed Jacob's wages ten times.
He pursued Jacob when he left and made a covenant at Mizpah.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Lavan' (לָבָן) means 'white.'

scripture_says.text: Joseph was the eleventh son of Jacob, firstborn of Rachel.
Jacob loved him most and made him a coat of many colors.
He had dreams of his family bowing to him.
His brothers sold him to traders who took him to Egypt.
He served in Potiphar's house and was imprisoned on false charges.
He interpreted dreams for Pharaoh's cupbearer, baker, and Pharaoh himself.
Pharaoh made him second in command over Egypt.
He preserved Egypt and his family during the seven-year famine.
He told his brothers: 'You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good.'
He lived 110 years and made Israel swear to carry his bones to Canaan.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yoseph' (יוֹסֵף) means 'he adds' or 'may he add.'

scripture_says.text: Judah was the fourth son of Jacob and Leah.
He proposed selling Joseph rather than killing him.
He married a Canaanite woman and had three sons: Er, Onan, and Shelah.
He unknowingly fathered Perez and Zerah by Tamar.
He pledged his own life for Benjamin.
Jacob blessed him: 'The scepter shall not depart from Judah.'
The tribe of Judah became the ruling tribe; David and Jesus descended from him.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yehudah' (יְהוּדָה) means 'praise.'

scripture_says.text: Tamar was given in marriage to Er, Judah's firstborn.
After Er died, she was given to Onan according to levirate custom.
Onan also died, and Judah withheld Shelah from her.
She disguised herself as a prostitute and conceived by Judah.
When Judah learned the truth, he said, 'She is more righteous than I.'
She bore twins: Perez and Zerah.
She is listed in the genealogy of Jesus.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Tamar' (תָּמָר) means 'palm tree.'

scripture_says.text: Potiphar was an Egyptian officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard.
He bought Joseph from the Ishmaelites.
He saw that the LORD was with Joseph and made him overseer of his house.
His wife attempted to seduce Joseph, and when he refused, she accused him falsely.
Potiphar was angry and put Joseph in prison.

history_records.text: The Egyptian name 'Potiphar' may mean 'he whom Ra gave.'

scripture_says.text: Asenath was the daughter of Potiphera, priest of On.
Pharaoh gave her to Joseph as his wife.
She bore Joseph two sons: Manasseh and Ephraim.

history_records.text: The Egyptian name 'Asenath' may mean 'belonging to (the goddess) Neith.'
On (Heliopolis) was a major center of sun worship in Egypt.

sources.text: Located at modern Tell Hisn, near Cairo

scripture_says.text: Moses was born to Levite parents during the Egyptian oppression.
His mother hid him three months, then placed him in a basket in the Nile.
Pharaoh's daughter found him and raised him as her son.
He fled Egypt after killing an Egyptian and lived in Midian 40 years.
God appeared to him in a burning bush and commissioned him to deliver Israel.
He performed signs before Pharaoh and led Israel out of Egypt.
He received the Law at Mount Sinai.
He led Israel 40 years in the wilderness.
He was not permitted to enter Canaan because he struck the rock.
He died at 120 years on Mount Nebo; God buried him.
He spoke with God 'face to face, as a man speaks to his friend.'
He appeared with Elijah at Jesus' transfiguration.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Mosheh' (מֹשֶׁה) is explained as 'drawn out' of water.
Josephus records Moses was educated in all Egyptian wisdom and was a military leader.

scripture_says.text: Aaron was Moses' older brother, son of Amram and Jochebed.
God appointed him to be Moses' spokesman.
He performed signs before Pharaoh with his staff.
He made the golden calf when Moses delayed on the mountain.
He was consecrated as Israel's first high priest.
His rod budded as a sign of God's choice of the Levites.
He died on Mount Hor at age 123; Eleazar succeeded him.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Aharon' (אַהֲרֹן) has uncertain meaning, possibly 'mountain of strength.'

question: Who was Pharaoh of the Exodus?

scripture_says.text: Scripture does not name the Pharaoh of the Exodus.
A new king arose 'who did not know Joseph.'
He enslaved Israel and commanded male infants killed.
His daughter found and adopted Moses.
He hardened his heart against the plagues.
He pursued Israel and his army drowned in the Red Sea.

history_records.text: 'Pharaoh' (פַּרְעֹה) is the Hebrew form of Egyptian 'per-aa' meaning 'great house.'
Proposed identifications include Ramesses II (c. 1279-1213 BC) or earlier pharaohs depending on dating.

scripture_says.text: Jethro was a priest of Midian.
He is also called Reuel.
Moses fled to him and married his daughter Zipporah.
He brought Zipporah and Moses' sons to him in the wilderness.
He rejoiced in God's deliverance: 'Now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods.'
He offered sacrifices to God.
He advised Moses to appoint judges to share the burden of leadership.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yithro' (יִתְרוֹ) means 'his excellence.' 'Reuel' means 'friend of God.'

scripture_says.text: Zipporah was one of seven daughters of Jethro (Reuel), priest of Midian.
Moses married her after helping water her flocks.
She bore Moses two sons: Gershom and Eliezer.
She circumcised their son to avert God's anger against Moses.
She and her sons stayed with Jethro during the Exodus and were later reunited with Moses.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Tsipporah' (צִפֹּרָה) means 'bird.'

scripture_says.text: Joshua was the son of Nun, from the tribe of Ephraim.
Moses changed his name from Hoshea to Joshua.
He was Moses' assistant from his youth.
He led Israel's battle against Amalek at Rephidim.
He and Caleb gave a good report as spies and were allowed to enter Canaan.
God commissioned him to succeed Moses.
He led Israel across the Jordan and conquered Canaan.
He divided the land among the tribes.
He renewed the covenant at Shechem: 'As for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.'
He died at 110 years and was buried at Timnath-serah.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yehoshua' (יְהוֹשֻׁעַ) means 'the LORD saves.' The Greek form is 'Jesus' (Ἰησοῦς).

scripture_says.text: Caleb was the son of Jephunneh, a Kenizzite, representing Judah.
He was one of the twelve spies sent to explore Canaan.
He and Joshua gave a faithful report: 'Let us go up at once and occupy it.'
He 'wholly followed the LORD' and was promised the land he surveyed.
At age 85, he was still strong and asked for Hebron as his inheritance.
He drove out the three sons of Anak from Hebron.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Kalev' (כָּלֵב) means 'dog' or may be related to 'whole-hearted.'

scripture_says.text: Korah was a Levite, son of Izhar, grandson of Kohath.
He led a rebellion against Moses and Aaron with Dathan, Abiram, and 250 leaders.
They challenged: 'All the congregation are holy... Why do you exalt yourselves?'
Moses said God would show who is holy.
The earth opened and swallowed Korah, Dathan, Abiram, and their households.
Fire from the LORD consumed the 250 men offering incense.
Korah's sons did not die; descendants became temple singers.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Qorach' (קֹרַח) means 'bald' or 'ice.'

scripture_says.text: Balaam was the son of Beor, from Pethor near the Euphrates.
Balak king of Moab hired him to curse Israel.
God came to Balaam and told him not to curse Israel.
His donkey saw the angel of the LORD and spoke to him.
He blessed Israel four times instead of cursing them.
He prophesied: 'A star shall come out of Jacob, and a scepter shall rise out of Israel.'
He advised Balak to seduce Israel with Moabite women and idolatry.
He was killed by Israel in battle against Midian.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Bil'am' (בִּלְעָם) may mean 'not of the people' or 'destroyer.'
The Deir Alla inscription (c. 840 BC) mentions 'Balaam son of Beor' as a seer.

scripture_says.text: Balak was the son of Zippor, king of Moab.
He feared Israel after they defeated the Amorites.
He sent for Balaam to curse Israel.
He offered Balaam honor and payment.
He built seven altars and offered sacrifices three times for Balaam to curse Israel.
He was angry when Balaam blessed Israel instead.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Balaq' (בָּלָק) means 'devastator' or 'waster.'

scripture_says.text: Phinehas was the son of Eleazar, grandson of Aaron.
He killed Zimri and Cozbi with a spear, stopping a plague in Israel.
God gave him a 'covenant of peace' and 'perpetual priesthood' for his zeal.
He led the war party against Midian.
He inquired of God during the war against Benjamin.
The Psalm says his act 'was counted to him as righteousness.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Pinechas' (פִּינְחָס) is Egyptian in origin, meaning 'the dark-skinned one.'

scripture_says.text: Othniel was the son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother.
He captured Kiriath-sepher and received Caleb's daughter Achsah as wife.
He was the first judge of Israel.
The Spirit of the LORD was upon him.
He defeated Cushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia.
The land had rest 40 years under him.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Othniel' (עָתְנִיאֵל) means 'lion of God' or 'God is my strength.'

scripture_says.text: Ehud was the son of Gera, a Benjaminite.
He was left-handed.
Israel sent tribute to Eglon king of Moab through him.
He made a double-edged sword and hid it on his right thigh.
He told Eglon 'I have a message from God for you' and killed him.
He rallied Israel and defeated Moab; 10,000 warriors fell.
The land had rest 80 years.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Ehud' (אֵהוּד) may mean 'united' or 'strong.'

scripture_says.text: Barak was the son of Abinoam from Kedesh-naphtali.
Deborah commanded him to gather 10,000 men from Naphtali and Zebulun.
He refused to go unless Deborah went with him.
He pursued Sisera's army; the LORD routed them.
He pursued Sisera to Jael's tent.
He sang with Deborah.
Hebrews lists him among the heroes of faith.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Baraq' (בָּרָק) means 'lightning.'

scripture_says.text: Gideon was the son of Joash the Abiezrite, from Manasseh.
The angel of the LORD appeared to him: 'The LORD is with you, O mighty man of valor.'
He tore down his father's altar to Baal and was called Jerubbaal.
He tested God with a fleece twice.
God reduced his army from 32,000 to 300.
He defeated the Midianites with torches, jars, and trumpets.
He refused to be king: 'The LORD will rule over you.'
He made an ephod that became a snare.
He had 70 sons and judged Israel 40 years.
Hebrews lists him among the heroes of faith.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Gid'on' (גִּדְעוֹן) means 'feller' or 'hewer.'

scripture_says.text: Abimelech was Gideon's son by his concubine from Shechem.
He killed 70 of his brothers on one stone; only Jotham escaped.
The men of Shechem made him king.
Jotham told the parable of the trees against him.
He ruled three years.
He destroyed Shechem and sowed it with salt.
A woman dropped a millstone on his head at Thebez.
He had his armor-bearer kill him to avoid shame.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Avimelekh' (אֲבִימֶלֶךְ) means 'my father is king.'

scripture_says.text: Jephthah was a Gileadite, son of a prostitute.
His half-brothers drove him out; he became leader of worthless men.
Gilead's elders asked him to lead against Ammon.
He negotiated with Ammon but they refused peace.
He vowed to sacrifice whatever came from his house first if victorious.
He defeated Ammon; his daughter came out first.
He fulfilled his vow after she mourned two months.
He judged Israel six years.
Hebrews lists him among the heroes of faith.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yiphtach' (יִפְתָּח) means 'he opens.'

scripture_says.text: Samson was the son of Manoah, from the tribe of Dan.
An angel announced his birth; he was to be a Nazirite from the womb.
The Spirit of the LORD began to stir him.
He killed a lion with his bare hands.
He killed 30 Philistines at Ashkelon.
He killed 1,000 Philistines with a donkey's jawbone.
He loved Delilah, who betrayed him to the Philistines.
His hair was cut; his strength left him.
The Philistines gouged out his eyes and imprisoned him.
His hair grew back; he collapsed the temple of Dagon, killing more in death than in life.
He judged Israel 20 years.
Hebrews lists him among the heroes of faith.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Shimshon' (שִׁמְשׁוֹן) is related to 'shemesh' meaning 'sun.'

scripture_says.text: Delilah was a woman in the Valley of Sorek whom Samson loved.
The Philistine lords bribed her with 1,100 pieces of silver each.
She pressed Samson daily to reveal the secret of his strength.
He told her his strength was in his Nazirite vow and uncut hair.
She called a man to shave his seven locks while he slept.
She called out 'The Philistines are upon you!' and he was captured.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Delilah' (דְּלִילָה) may mean 'feeble' or 'languishing.'

scripture_says.text: Samuel was the son of Elkanah and Hannah, dedicated to the LORD before birth.
His mother gave him to Eli the priest at Shiloh.
The LORD called him as a boy and revealed Eli's judgment.
He was established as a prophet; none of his words fell to the ground.
He judged Israel all his life, traveling a circuit.
He anointed Saul as Israel's first king.
He anointed David as Saul's successor.
He died and all Israel mourned him.
Saul consulted him through the medium at Endor after his death.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Shemu'el' (שְׁמוּאֵל) means 'heard of God' or 'name of God.'

scripture_says.text: Eli was priest at Shiloh and judged Israel 40 years.
He thought Hannah was drunk when she prayed silently.
He blessed Hannah and she conceived Samuel.
He raised Samuel at the tabernacle.
His sons Hophni and Phinehas were wicked.
A man of God prophesied judgment on his house.
God revealed to Samuel that Eli's house would be judged.
His sons died and the ark was captured; he fell and broke his neck.
He was 98 years old and heavy when he died.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Eli' (עֵלִי) means 'ascension' or 'my God.'

scripture_says.text: Saul was the son of Kish, from the tribe of Benjamin.
He was a head taller than anyone else in Israel.
Samuel anointed him as Israel's first king.
The Spirit of God rushed upon him and he prophesied.
He defeated the Ammonites at Jabesh-gilead.
He disobeyed by offering sacrifice before Samuel arrived.
He disobeyed by sparing Agag and the best of the Amalekites.
Samuel said 'to obey is better than sacrifice' and declared the kingdom torn from him.
An evil spirit from the LORD tormented him.
He became jealous of David and tried to kill him.
He consulted the medium at Endor before his final battle.
He died on Mount Gilboa; he fell on his own sword.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Sha'ul' (שָׁאוּל) means 'asked for' or 'requested.'

scripture_says.text: Jonathan was the son of Saul.
He attacked the Philistine garrison at Geba with his armor-bearer alone.
He unknowingly violated Saul's oath by eating honey.
The people ransomed him from Saul's death sentence.
His soul was knit to David's soul; he loved him as his own soul.
He made a covenant with David and gave him his robe, armor, sword, bow, and belt.
He warned David of Saul's intent to kill him.
He died with Saul on Mount Gilboa.
David lamented: 'How the mighty have fallen!'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yehonatan' (יְהוֹנָתָן) means 'the LORD has given.'

scripture_says.text: David was the youngest son of Jesse, from Bethlehem of Judah.
Samuel anointed him when he was a shepherd boy.
He played the lyre to soothe Saul's troubled spirit.
He killed Goliath with a sling and stone.
He was a man after God's own heart.
He reigned 7 years in Hebron and 33 years in Jerusalem.
God made a covenant with him: his throne would be established forever.
He committed adultery with Bathsheba and arranged Uriah's death.
Nathan confronted him and he repented.
He wrote many of the Psalms.
He prepared materials for the temple Solomon would build.
He died and was buried in the city of David.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'David' (דָּוִד) means 'beloved.'
The Tel Dan Inscription (9th century BC) mentions 'the house of David.'

scripture_says.text: Uriah was one of David's mighty men.
He was the husband of Bathsheba.
David recalled him from battle to cover the pregnancy.
He refused to go home while the ark and Israel were in tents.
David sent him back with a letter ordering his death.
He was placed in the fiercest fighting and killed.
Nathan's parable condemned David: 'You are the man!'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Uriyah' (אוּרִיָּה) means 'the LORD is my light.'

scripture_says.text: Absalom was David's third son, by Maacah.
He was praised for his beauty; his hair was famously thick.
He killed his half-brother Amnon for raping his sister Tamar.
He fled to Geshur for three years.
Joab arranged his return, but David would not see him for two years.
He stole the hearts of Israel by sitting at the gate.
He declared himself king in Hebron and rebelled against David.
He took David's concubines publicly.
His hair caught in an oak tree; Joab killed him.
David mourned: 'O my son Absalom, my son, my son!'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Avshalom' (אַבְשָׁלוֹם) means 'father of peace.'

scripture_says.text: Solomon was David's son by Bathsheba.
God named him Jedidiah ('beloved of the LORD').
He asked God for wisdom and received it.
He judged wisely between two mothers claiming one child.
He built the temple in Jerusalem.
He was wiser than all men.
He spoke 3,000 proverbs and 1,005 songs.
The Queen of Sheba came to test his wisdom.
He had 700 wives and 300 concubines who turned his heart to other gods.
God raised adversaries against him because of his unfaithfulness.
He reigned 40 years and was buried in Jerusalem.
Jesus said even Solomon's glory did not match the lilies.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Shelomoh' (שְׁלֹמֹה) means 'peaceful.'

scripture_says.text: Nathan was a prophet in David's court.
He delivered God's promise to David of an everlasting dynasty.
He confronted David with the parable of the poor man's lamb.
He declared: 'You are the man!' and pronounced judgment.
He named Solomon 'Jedidiah' at his birth.
He helped Bathsheba secure Solomon's throne against Adonijah.
He wrote chronicles of David and Solomon.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Nathan' (נָתָן) means 'he gave.'

scripture_says.text: Joab was the son of Zeruiah, David's sister.
He was David's military commander.
He killed Abner in revenge for his brother Asahel.
He led the conquest of Jerusalem through the water shaft.
He arranged Uriah's death at David's command.
He killed Absalom against David's orders.
He killed Amasa, his replacement as commander.
He supported Adonijah against Solomon.
Solomon had him executed for his bloodguilt.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yo'av' (יוֹאָב) means 'the LORD is father.'

scripture_says.text: Michal was Saul's younger daughter.
She loved David.
Saul gave her to David for 100 Philistine foreskins.
She helped David escape through a window and deceived Saul's men.
Saul gave her to Palti son of Laish.
David demanded her return when he became king.
She despised David for dancing before the ark.
She had no children to the day of her death.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Mikhal' (מִיכַל) means 'who is like God?' (shortened form).

scripture_says.text: Jeroboam was the son of Nebat, an Ephraimite.
He was Solomon's servant over the forced labor.
The prophet Ahijah tore his garment into twelve pieces, giving ten to Jeroboam.
He fled to Egypt when Solomon sought to kill him.
He became king of the northern tribes after Rehoboam refused to lighten burdens.
He made two golden calves at Bethel and Dan: 'Behold your gods, O Israel.'
He instituted a non-Levitical priesthood and a rival feast.
A prophet condemned his altar; his hand withered.
His sin became the pattern for later kings: 'the sins of Jeroboam.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yarov'am' (יָרָבְעָם) means 'the people increase.'

scripture_says.text: Ahab was the son of Omri, king of Israel.
He married Jezebel and served Baal.
He 'did more to provoke the LORD... than all the kings of Israel before him.'
Elijah prophesied no rain for years.
He called Elijah 'troubler of Israel.'
He witnessed the contest on Mount Carmel.
He coveted Naboth's vineyard; Jezebel had Naboth killed.
Elijah pronounced judgment: dogs would lick his blood.
He humbled himself and God delayed the judgment.
He died in battle at Ramoth-gilead; dogs licked his blood.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Ach'av' (אַחְאָב) means 'brother of the father' or 'uncle.'
The Moabite Stone mentions Omri (Ahab's father) and Israel.

scripture_says.text: Elijah was from Tishbe in Gilead.
He declared no rain would fall except at his word.
Ravens fed him by the brook Cherith.
He raised the widow of Zarephath's son.
He confronted the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel.
He fled from Jezebel and heard God in a still small voice.
He anointed Elisha as his successor.
He confronted Ahab about Naboth's vineyard.
He was taken up to heaven in a whirlwind with a chariot of fire.
Malachi prophesied Elijah would return before the day of the LORD.
He appeared with Moses at Jesus' transfiguration.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Eliyahu' (אֵלִיָּהוּ) means 'my God is the LORD.'

scripture_says.text: Elisha was the son of Shaphat from Abel-meholah.
Elijah cast his mantle on him while he was plowing.
He asked for a double portion of Elijah's spirit.
He received Elijah's mantle and parted the Jordan.
He healed the waters of Jericho.
He multiplied oil for the widow.
He raised the Shunammite's son.
He healed Naaman's leprosy.
He made an axe head float.
He blinded the Aramean army.
A dead man revived when touching his bones.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Elisha' (אֱלִישָׁע) means 'my God is salvation.'

scripture_says.text: Jehu was the son of Jehoshaphat, son of Nimshi.
Elijah was commanded to anoint him king over Israel.
A prophet anointed him and declared he would strike Ahab's house.
He drove his chariot furiously.
He killed King Joram of Israel with an arrow.
He had Jezebel thrown from a window.
He destroyed Baal worship from Israel.
He did not depart from the sins of Jeroboam.
God promised his dynasty would last four generations.
He reigned 28 years.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yehu' (יֵהוּא) means 'the LORD is he.'
The Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III depicts 'Jehu son of Omri' paying tribute.

scripture_says.text: Omri was commander of the army of Israel.
The army made him king when they heard Zimri had killed King Elah.
He besieged Zimri, who killed himself.
He prevailed over Tibni and became sole ruler.
He bought the hill of Samaria and built the capital there.
He 'did evil in the eyes of the LORD, and did worse than all who were before him.'
He reigned 12 years.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Omri' (עָמְרִי) has uncertain meaning.
The Moabite Stone mentions 'Omri king of Israel' who oppressed Moab.
Assyrian records called Israel 'the land of Omri' for over a century.

scripture_says.text: Naboth was a Jezreelite who owned a vineyard near Ahab's palace.
Ahab wanted his vineyard for a vegetable garden.
He refused: 'The LORD forbid that I should give you the inheritance of my fathers.'
Jezebel arranged false witnesses to accuse him of cursing God and the king.
He was stoned to death.
Ahab took possession of his vineyard.
Elijah pronounced judgment on Ahab at the vineyard.
Jehu cited Naboth's blood when killing Joram.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Navot' (נָבוֹת) has uncertain meaning, possibly related to 'fruit.'

scripture_says.text: Rehoboam was Solomon's son.
All Israel came to Shechem to make him king.
He rejected the elders' advice and said he would increase their burden.
Ten tribes rebelled and made Jeroboam king.
He reigned over Judah and Benjamin.
God forbade him to fight Israel.
Shishak of Egypt attacked Jerusalem and took temple treasures.
He reigned 17 years.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Rechav'am' (רְחַבְעָם) means 'the people is enlarged.'
Pharaoh Shishak's invasion is recorded at Karnak Temple, Egypt.

scripture_says.text: Asa was the son of Abijam, king of Judah.
He did what was right in the eyes of the LORD.
He removed the idols and male cult prostitutes.
He removed his grandmother Maacah from being queen mother for her idol.
He relied on the LORD and defeated the Cushite army.
He hired Ben-hadad of Syria against Baasha of Israel.
The prophet Hanani rebuked him for relying on Syria.
He was diseased in his feet but did not seek the LORD.
He reigned 41 years.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Asa' (אָסָא) may mean 'healer.'

scripture_says.text: Jehoshaphat was the son of Asa, king of Judah.
He walked in the ways of his father Asa.
He sent officials to teach the law in the cities of Judah.
He allied with Ahab against Ramoth-gilead.
He asked to inquire of the LORD through Micaiah.
He was almost killed in battle wearing Ahab's robes.
He appointed judges and said: 'The LORD is with you in giving judgment.'
He led Judah in prayer when invaded by Moab, Ammon, and Edom.
God gave them victory without fighting.
He reigned 25 years.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yehoshaphat' (יְהוֹשָׁפָט) means 'the LORD has judged.'

scripture_says.text: Joash was the son of Ahaziah.
He was hidden in the temple six years while Athaliah ruled.
Jehoiada the priest crowned him king at age seven.
He did right in the eyes of the LORD all the days of Jehoiada.
He ordered the repair of the temple.
After Jehoiada's death, he abandoned the LORD and served idols.
He had Zechariah son of Jehoiada stoned for rebuking him.
Hazael of Syria invaded; Joash gave him temple treasures.
His servants conspired and killed him.
He reigned 40 years.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yo'ash' (יוֹאָשׁ) means 'the LORD has given.'

scripture_says.text: Uzziah (also called Azariah) was the son of Amaziah.
He became king at age 16 and reigned 52 years.
He did what was right in the eyes of the LORD.
He sought God during the days of Zechariah and prospered.
He built up Jerusalem's defenses and equipped an army.
His fame spread far.
His heart was proud; he entered the temple to burn incense.
The priests confronted him; he became leprous on his forehead.
He lived in a separate house as a leper until death.
Isaiah's call came in the year King Uzziah died.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Uzziyahu' (עֻזִּיָּהוּ) means 'the LORD is my strength.'
A burial inscription reading 'Here were brought the bones of Uzziah, king of Judah' was found in Jerusalem.

scripture_says.text: Hezekiah was the son of Ahaz, king of Judah.
He did what was right in the eyes of the LORD.
He removed the high places and broke the bronze serpent.
He trusted in the LORD; none like him before or after.
He rebelled against Assyria.
Sennacherib invaded Judah; Hezekiah paid tribute.
The Rabshakeh mocked God; Hezekiah prayed.
Isaiah prophesied deliverance; 185,000 Assyrians died.
He was sick unto death; Isaiah told him to set his house in order.
He prayed and God added 15 years to his life.
He showed Babylonian envoys all his treasures.
Isaiah prophesied Babylon would take everything.
He reigned 29 years.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Chizkiyahu' (חִזְקִיָּהוּ) means 'the LORD strengthens.'
The Taylor Prism records Sennacherib's siege of Jerusalem.
Hezekiah's Tunnel (Siloam Tunnel) was built to supply water during sieges.

scripture_says.text: Manasseh was the son of Hezekiah.
He became king at age 12 and reigned 55 years.
He rebuilt high places and erected altars to Baal.
He built altars to the host of heaven in the temple.
He made his son pass through fire.
He 'shed very much innocent blood.'
He led Judah to do more evil than the nations God destroyed.
Assyria took him captive to Babylon.
He humbled himself and prayed; God restored him.
He removed foreign gods and restored the altar of the LORD.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Menasheh' (מְנַשֶּׁה) means 'causing to forget.'
Assyrian records mention 'Manasseh king of Judah' among vassal kings.

scripture_says.text: Josiah was the son of Amon, king of Judah.
He became king at age 8 and reigned 31 years.
He did what was right; 'he did not turn aside to the right or left.'
He ordered the repair of the temple.
The Book of the Law was found during repairs.
He tore his clothes when he heard the words of the Law.
Huldah the prophetess confirmed judgment was coming but not in his days.
He renewed the covenant and removed all idolatry.
He kept a Passover like none since the judges.
He was killed at Megiddo fighting Pharaoh Neco.
'Before him there was no king like him... nor did any arise after him.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yo'shiyahu' (יֹאשִׁיָּהוּ) means 'the LORD supports.'
Josiah was prophesied by name centuries earlier.

scripture_says.text: Zedekiah (originally Mattaniah) was Josiah's son, placed on throne by Nebuchadnezzar.
He was 21 when he became king and reigned 11 years.
He did evil in the eyes of the LORD.
He rebelled against Babylon.
Nebuchadnezzar besieged Jerusalem for over a year.
He fled but was captured at Jericho.
His sons were killed before his eyes; then he was blinded.
Jeremiah repeatedly warned him but he did not listen.
He was taken in chains to Babylon.
He was the last king of Judah before the exile.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Tsidkiyahu' (צִדְקִיָּהוּ) means 'the LORD is righteousness.'

scripture_says.text: Isaiah was the son of Amoz.
He prophesied during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah.
He saw the Lord seated on a throne; seraphim called 'Holy, holy, holy.'
A seraph touched his lips with a burning coal.
He said 'Here am I! Send me.'
He prophesied the virgin birth: 'Immanuel.'
He prophesied a child born: 'Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God.'
He wrote the Suffering Servant passages.
He counseled Hezekiah during Sennacherib's invasion.
He told Hezekiah he would recover from illness.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yesha'yahu' (יְשַׁעְיָהוּ) means 'the LORD is salvation.'
The Great Isaiah Scroll from the Dead Sea Scrolls contains the complete book.

scripture_says.text: Jeremiah was the son of Hilkiah, a priest from Anathoth.
God called him before he was born: 'I appointed you a prophet to the nations.'
He prophesied during Josiah through Zedekiah, 40+ years.
He was called 'the weeping prophet.'
He prophesied 70 years of exile.
He prophesied a new covenant written on hearts.
He was put in stocks.
He was thrown into a cistern.
Baruch was his scribe.
He was taken to Egypt against his will.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yirmeyahu' (יִרְמְיָהוּ) means 'the LORD exalts' or 'the LORD establishes.'
The Lachish Letters mention a prophet warning Judah during Nebuchadnezzar's invasion.

scripture_says.text: Ezekiel was a priest, son of Buzi.
He was among the exiles by the Chebar canal in Babylon.
He saw visions of God: living creatures and wheels.
God called him 'son of man' (ben adam).
He ate a scroll that was sweet as honey.
He acted out symbolic prophecies: lying on his side, shaving his head.
He saw the glory of the LORD depart from the temple.
He prophesied the valley of dry bones.
He described the battle of Gog and Magog.
He described a future temple.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yechezqel' (יְחֶזְקֵאל) means 'God strengthens.'

scripture_says.text: Daniel was taken captive to Babylon as a youth of royal/noble descent.
He was given the Babylonian name Belteshazzar.
He resolved not to defile himself with the king's food.
God gave him wisdom and ability to interpret dreams and visions.
He interpreted Nebuchadnezzar's dream of the statue.
He interpreted the writing on the wall for Belshazzar.
He was thrown into the lions' den for praying to God.
God shut the lions' mouths.
He received visions of four beasts and the Son of Man.
He received the prophecy of seventy weeks.
Ezekiel mentions Daniel's righteousness.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Daniel' (דָּנִיֵּאל) means 'God is my judge.'

scripture_says.text: Hosea was the son of Beeri.
He prophesied during Uzziah through Hezekiah (Judah) and Jeroboam II (Israel).
God commanded him to marry a promiscuous woman, Gomer.
His children were named Lo-ruhamah ('no mercy') and Lo-ammi ('not my people').
His marriage illustrated Israel's unfaithfulness to God.
He prophesied: 'I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice.'
He declared: 'Out of Egypt I called my son.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Hoshea' (הוֹשֵׁעַ) means 'salvation.'

scripture_says.text: Jonah was the son of Amittai, from Gath-hepher.
God called him to preach to Nineveh, but he fled to Tarshish.
A great storm arose; sailors threw him overboard.
A great fish swallowed him; he was inside three days and nights.
He prayed from the fish's belly.
The fish vomited him onto dry land.
He preached in Nineveh; the city repented.
He was angry that God relented from destroying Nineveh.
God taught him through a plant that grew and died.
Jesus cited Jonah as a sign: 'three days and three nights.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yonah' (יוֹנָה) means 'dove.'

scripture_says.text: Nebuchadnezzar was king of Babylon.
He besieged Jerusalem and carried Judah into exile.
He set up a golden image and demanded worship.
He threw Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego into the fiery furnace.
He saw four men in the fire and blessed their God.
Daniel interpreted his dream of a great tree.
He was driven from men and ate grass like an ox for seven periods.
His reason returned and he praised the Most High.

history_records.text: The Hebrew/Akkadian 'Nebukadnezzar' (נְבֻכַדְנֶאצַּר) means 'Nabu, protect my heir.'
Babylonian Chronicles record his conquest of Jerusalem in 597 BC.

scripture_says.text: Cyrus was king of Persia.
The LORD stirred his spirit to issue a decree to rebuild the temple.
He returned the temple vessels Nebuchadnezzar had taken.
Isaiah named him as God's 'anointed' (mashiach) 150+ years before he lived.
Isaiah prophesied he would rebuild Jerusalem and the temple.
God called him 'my shepherd.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Koresh' (כּוֹרֶשׁ) is the Persian name Kūrush.
The Cyrus Cylinder records his policy of returning exiles and restoring temples.

scripture_says.text: Ezra was a priest descended from Aaron through Zadok.
He was 'a scribe skilled in the Law of Moses.'
He came from Babylon to Jerusalem in Artaxerxes' seventh year.
He had 'set his heart to study the Law... to do it and to teach.'
He led about 1,500 men plus families from Babylon.
He was ashamed to ask the king for guards, trusting God's protection.
He tore his garments when he learned of intermarriage.
He led the people to confess and separate from foreign wives.
He read the Law publicly and the people wept.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Ezra' (עֶזְרָא) means 'help.'

scripture_says.text: Nehemiah was the son of Hacaliah.
He was cupbearer to King Artaxerxes.
He wept when he heard Jerusalem's walls were broken down.
He prayed and fasted, then asked the king to send him to rebuild.
He surveyed the walls at night.
He organized the people to rebuild despite opposition.
Workers held weapons while building.
The wall was completed in 52 days.
He served as governor and did not take the governor's food allowance.
He returned to Artaxerxes, then came back to Jerusalem for reforms.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Nechemyah' (נְחֶמְיָה) means 'the LORD comforts.'

scripture_says.text: Mordecai was a Jew from the tribe of Benjamin.
His ancestor Kish was taken captive from Jerusalem.
He raised his orphaned cousin Esther.
He sat at the king's gate.
He uncovered a plot against the king and it was recorded.
He refused to bow to Haman.
He tore his clothes and mourned when he learned of Haman's decree.
He told Esther deliverance would arise from another place if she remained silent.
The king honored him by having Haman lead him through the city.
He became second to the king and was great among the Jews.

history_records.text: The name 'Mordecai' may derive from the Babylonian god Marduk.

scripture_says.text: Job was from the land of Uz.
He was 'blameless and upright, one who feared God and turned away from evil.'
He was the greatest of all the people of the east.
Satan challenged: 'Does Job fear God for no reason?'
He lost his children, servants, and livestock in one day.
He said 'The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away.'
He was struck with painful sores from head to foot.
His wife said 'Curse God and die.'
Three friends debated with him about the cause of his suffering.
God answered him from the whirlwind.
He repented 'in dust and ashes.'
The LORD restored his fortunes and gave him twice what he had before.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Iyyov' (אִיּוֹב) may mean 'hated' or 'persecuted.'

question: Who was Mary mother of Jesus?

scripture_says.text: Mary was a virgin betrothed to Joseph.
Gabriel announced she would conceive by the Holy Spirit.
She said 'Behold, I am the servant of the Lord.'
She visited Elizabeth and sang the Magnificat.
She gave birth to Jesus in Bethlehem.
She treasured things in her heart.
Simeon told her a sword would pierce her soul.
She was at the wedding in Cana.
She stood at the cross; Jesus entrusted her to John.
She was with the disciples in the upper room.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Maria' (Μαρία) is from Hebrew 'Miryam.'

question: Who was Joseph husband of Mary?

scripture_says.text: Joseph was of the house and lineage of David.
He was a carpenter.
He was betrothed to Mary when she was found with child.
He was a righteous man and planned to divorce her quietly.
An angel told him to take Mary as his wife; the child was from the Holy Spirit.
He named the child Jesus as commanded.
He took Mary and Jesus to Egypt to escape Herod.
He settled in Nazareth after Herod's death.
He went to Jerusalem yearly for Passover.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Iōsēph' (Ἰωσήφ) is from Hebrew 'Yoseph' meaning 'he adds.'

scripture_says.text: John was the son of Zechariah and Elizabeth.
His birth was announced by Gabriel.
He was filled with the Holy Spirit from his mother's womb.
He went in the spirit and power of Elijah.
He preached in the wilderness: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.'
He baptized in the Jordan for repentance.
He said of Jesus: 'Behold, the Lamb of God.'
He baptized Jesus.
He was imprisoned by Herod Antipas for criticizing his marriage.
He was beheaded at Herodias' request.
Jesus said none born of women was greater than John.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Iōannēs' (Ἰωάννης) is from Hebrew 'Yochanan' meaning 'the LORD is gracious.'
Josephus mentions John's baptism and execution by Herod.

scripture_says.text: Peter (Simon) was a fisherman from Bethsaida.
His brother Andrew brought him to Jesus.
Jesus renamed him Cephas/Peter ('rock').
He confessed Jesus as 'the Christ, the Son of the living God.'
He walked on water toward Jesus, then sank.
He denied Jesus three times.
Jesus restored him: 'Feed my sheep.'
He preached at Pentecost; 3,000 were baptized.
He healed the lame man at the Beautiful Gate.
He received the vision to go to Cornelius.
He wrote two epistles.

history_records.text: 'Peter' (Πέτρος) means 'rock' in Greek; 'Cephas' (Κηφᾶς) is Aramaic for 'rock.'
Early tradition records Peter was crucified upside down in Rome under Nero.

scripture_says.text: Paul (Saul) was from Tarsus, a Roman citizen.
He was a Pharisee, educated under Gamaliel.
He persecuted the church, approving Stephen's death.
He encountered the risen Christ on the road to Damascus.
He was called to be apostle to the Gentiles.
He undertook three missionary journeys.
He wrote at least 13 epistles in the New Testament.
He was imprisoned multiple times.
He said 'For me to live is Christ, and to die is gain.'
He fought the good fight, finished the race, kept the faith.

history_records.text: 'Saul' (Σαῦλος) is Hebrew; 'Paul' (Παῦλος) is the Roman/Latin name.
Early tradition records Paul was beheaded in Rome under Nero.

scripture_says.text: Andrew was Simon Peter's brother, a fisherman from Bethsaida.
He was originally a disciple of John the Baptist.
He found Peter and said 'We have found the Messiah.'
He brought the boy with five loaves and two fish to Jesus.
He brought Greeks who wanted to see Jesus.
He is listed among the twelve apostles.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Andreas' (Ἀνδρέας) means 'manly.'

question: Who was James son of Zebedee?

scripture_says.text: James was the son of Zebedee and brother of John.
Jesus called him and John 'Boanerges' (sons of thunder).
He was one of the inner three with Peter and John.
He witnessed the transfiguration.
He asked to sit at Jesus' right hand in the kingdom.
He was killed by Herod Agrippa I.
He was the first apostle to be martyred.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Iakōbos' (Ἰάκωβος) is from Hebrew 'Ya'aqov' (Jacob).

question: Who was John son of Zebedee?

scripture_says.text: John was the son of Zebedee and brother of James.
He was called a 'son of thunder.'
He is called 'the disciple whom Jesus loved.'
He reclined next to Jesus at the Last Supper.
He stood at the cross; Jesus entrusted Mary to him.
He outran Peter to the empty tomb.
He wrote the Gospel of John.
He wrote three epistles.
He received the Revelation on Patmos.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Iōannēs' (Ἰωάννης) is from Hebrew 'Yochanan' meaning 'the LORD is gracious.'
Early tradition says John lived to old age in Ephesus.

question: Who was Matthew the tax collector?

scripture_says.text: Matthew (also called Levi) was a tax collector.
Jesus called him while he sat at the tax booth.
He gave a great feast for Jesus at his house.
Many tax collectors and sinners came to the feast.
He is listed among the twelve apostles.
He is traditionally credited with writing the Gospel of Matthew.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Matthaios' (Μαθθαῖος) is from Hebrew 'Mattityahu' meaning 'gift of the LORD.'

scripture_says.text: Thomas was called 'the Twin' (Didymus).
He said 'Let us also go, that we may die with him' before Lazarus' raising.
He asked Jesus 'How can we know the way?'
He was absent when Jesus first appeared to the disciples.
He said he would not believe unless he saw Jesus' wounds.
Jesus appeared and invited him to touch his wounds.
He confessed 'My Lord and my God!'
Jesus said 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet believed.'

history_records.text: 'Thomas' (Θωμᾶς) is from Aramaic 'te'oma' meaning 'twin.' Greek 'Didymus' also means 'twin.'

scripture_says.text: Judas was one of the twelve apostles.
He was the keeper of the money bag and was a thief.
He asked 'Why was this ointment not sold?' when Mary anointed Jesus.
He betrayed Jesus for thirty pieces of silver.
He identified Jesus with a kiss in Gethsemane.
He regretted his action and returned the silver.
He threw the money in the temple and hanged himself.
Peter said he 'fell headlong' and his body burst open.
Jesus called him 'the son of destruction.'

history_records.text: 'Iscariot' may mean 'man of Kerioth' (a town in Judea) or derive from Latin 'sicarius' (dagger-man).

scripture_says.text: Lazarus was the brother of Mary and Martha, from Bethany.
Jesus loved him.
He became ill and died.
Jesus wept at his tomb.
He had been dead four days when Jesus arrived.
Jesus called 'Lazarus, come out!' and he came out alive.
Many Jews believed because of his resurrection.
The chief priests plotted to kill him too.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Lazaros' (Λάζαρος) is from Hebrew 'El'azar' meaning 'God has helped.'

scripture_says.text: Nicodemus was a Pharisee and ruler of the Jews.
He came to Jesus by night.
He said 'We know that you are a teacher come from God.'
Jesus told him 'You must be born again.'
He asked 'How can these things be?'
He defended Jesus before the Sanhedrin: 'Does our law judge a man without first giving him a hearing?'
He brought 75 pounds of myrrh and aloes to prepare Jesus' body.
He helped Joseph of Arimathea bury Jesus.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Nikodēmos' (Νικόδημος) means 'conqueror of the people.'

scripture_says.text: Joseph was from Arimathea, a city of the Jews.
He was a rich man.
He was a respected member of the council.
He was looking for the kingdom of God.
He was a disciple of Jesus, but secretly for fear of the Jews.
He did not consent to the council's decision.
He asked Pilate for Jesus' body.
He wrapped Jesus in a linen shroud.
He laid Jesus in his own new tomb.

history_records.text: Arimathea is likely Ramathaim-zophim, Samuel's birthplace.

scripture_says.text: Barnabas (Joseph) was a Levite from Cyprus.
The apostles named him Barnabas ('son of encouragement').
He sold a field and laid the money at the apostles' feet.
He brought Saul to the apostles when others were afraid.
He was sent to Antioch and encouraged the believers.
He brought Saul from Tarsus to Antioch.
He and Paul were sent on the first missionary journey.
He wanted to take Mark; Paul disagreed; they parted ways.
He took Mark and sailed to Cyprus.
Paul later called him a fellow worker.

history_records.text: 'Barnabas' (Βαρναβᾶς) is Aramaic meaning 'son of encouragement' or 'son of prophecy.'

scripture_says.text: Timothy was from Lystra, son of a Jewish mother and Greek father.
His mother Eunice and grandmother Lois had sincere faith.
He was well spoken of by believers in Lystra and Iconium.
Paul circumcised him because of the Jews.
Paul called him 'my true child in the faith.'
Paul sent him to various churches as his representative.
Paul wrote two letters to him about church leadership.
Paul urged him 'Let no one despise you for your youth.'
Hebrews mentions his release from prison.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Timotheos' (Τιμόθεος) means 'honoring God.'

scripture_says.text: Luke was a physician.
Paul called him 'the beloved physician.'
He was Paul's companion in his travels.
He wrote an orderly account of Jesus' life and ministry.
He addressed his Gospel and Acts to Theophilus.
He joined Paul at Troas (the 'we' sections begin).
He was with Paul in Rome during his imprisonment.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Loukas' (Λουκᾶς) is a shortened form of Lucanus.
Early tradition identifies Luke as a Gentile, possibly from Antioch.

scripture_says.text: Stephen was one of the seven chosen to serve tables.
He was a man 'full of faith and of the Holy Spirit.'
He did great wonders and signs among the people.
He was accused of speaking against Moses and God.
His face was like the face of an angel.
He gave a speech recounting Israel's history.
He saw Jesus standing at the right hand of God.
He was stoned to death.
He prayed 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them.'
He was the first Christian martyr.
Saul approved of his death.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Stephanos' (Στέφανος) means 'crown' or 'wreath.'

question: Who was James brother of Jesus?

scripture_says.text: James was a brother of Jesus.
Jesus' brothers did not believe in him during his ministry.
The risen Jesus appeared to James.
He was a pillar of the Jerusalem church.
Paul visited him after his conversion.
He presided at the Jerusalem council.
He gave the judgment that Gentiles need not be circumcised.
He wrote the epistle of James.
He called himself 'a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'Iakōbos' (Ἰάκωβος) is from Hebrew 'Ya'aqov.'
Josephus records James was stoned to death in 62 AD.

scripture_says.text: Herod was king of Judea when Jesus was born.
The wise men came to him asking where the King of the Jews was born.
He was troubled when he heard of the newborn king.
He asked the wise men to report the child's location.
He killed all male children two years and under in Bethlehem.
Joseph was warned in a dream to flee to Egypt.
After his death, an angel told Joseph to return.

history_records.text: Herod reigned from 37-4 BC, appointed by Rome.
He rebuilt the Jerusalem temple on a grand scale.

scripture_says.text: Herod Antipas was tetrarch of Galilee and Perea.
He married Herodias, his brother Philip's wife.
John the Baptist rebuked him for this.
He arrested and imprisoned John.
He had John beheaded at Herodias' request.
He thought Jesus might be John risen from the dead.
He wanted to see Jesus perform a sign.
Pilate sent Jesus to him; he mocked Jesus and sent him back.

history_records.text: Herod Antipas ruled from 4 BC to 39 AD.

scripture_says.text: Pilate was the Roman governor of Judea.
Jesus was brought to him for trial.
He asked Jesus 'Are you the King of the Jews?'
He found no guilt in Jesus.
He offered to release Jesus or Barabbas.
His wife warned him to have nothing to do with 'that righteous man.'
He washed his hands, saying 'I am innocent of this man's blood.'
He had Jesus flogged and handed him over to be crucified.
He wrote the inscription 'Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews.'
He gave Jesus' body to Joseph of Arimathea.

history_records.text: Pilate governed Judea from 26-36 AD.
The Pilate Stone, found at Caesarea, confirms his title as prefect.

scripture_says.text: Caiaphas was high priest that year.
He prophesied 'it is better that one man should die for the people.'
Jesus was taken to his house after arrest.
He asked Jesus 'Are you the Christ, the Son of the Blessed?'
He tore his robes and said Jesus had blasphemed.
He participated in Peter and John's trial.

history_records.text: Caiaphas (Joseph Caiaphas) was high priest from 18-36 AD.
An ossuary inscribed 'Joseph son of Caiaphas' was discovered in Jerusalem.

scripture_says.text: Joel was the son of Pethuel.
He prophesied about a devastating locust plague.
He called for repentance: 'Rend your hearts and not your garments.'
He prophesied the outpouring of the Spirit on all flesh.
He described the Day of the LORD.
Peter quoted Joel at Pentecost.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yo'el' (יוֹאֵל) means 'the LORD is God.'

scripture_says.text: Amos was a shepherd from Tekoa.
He was also a dresser of sycamore figs.
He prophesied during Uzziah (Judah) and Jeroboam II (Israel).
He declared judgment on surrounding nations and Israel.
He said 'I was no prophet... but the LORD took me.'
Amaziah the priest told him to flee to Judah.
