He proclaimed 'Let justice roll down like waters.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Amos' (עָמוֹס) means 'burden-bearer.'

scripture_says.text: Obadiah received a vision concerning Edom.
He prophesied judgment on Edom for violence against Jacob.
Edom rejoiced when Jerusalem fell.
He declared 'The kingdom shall be the LORD's.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Ovadyah' (עֹבַדְיָה) means 'servant of the LORD.'
Obadiah is the shortest book in the Old Testament (21 verses).

scripture_says.text: Micah was from Moresheth.
He prophesied during Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah.
He prophesied Samaria's destruction.
He condemned leaders who 'tear the skin from my people.'
He prophesied the Messiah's birth in Bethlehem.
He summarized what God requires: 'Do justice, love kindness, walk humbly.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Mikhah' (מִיכָה) means 'Who is like the LORD?'

scripture_says.text: Nahum was from Elkosh.
He prophesied the fall of Nineveh.
He declared God is 'slow to anger and great in power.'
He described Nineveh's destruction in vivid detail.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Nachum' (נַחוּם) means 'comfort' or 'consolation.'
Nineveh fell to Babylon in 612 BC, fulfilling Nahum's prophecy.

scripture_says.text: Habakkuk was a prophet who questioned God about injustice.
God answered that He was raising up the Chaldeans.
He asked how God could use wicked Babylon to judge.
He stood on his watchtower to wait for God's answer.
God told him to write the vision: 'The righteous shall live by his faith.'
He ended with a prayer of faith despite circumstances.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Chavaqquq' (חֲבַקּוּק) may mean 'embrace.'

scripture_says.text: Zephaniah was son of Cushi, great-great-grandson of Hezekiah.
He prophesied during the reign of Josiah.
He proclaimed universal judgment on the Day of the LORD.
He called Judah to seek the LORD before the day of anger.
He prophesied restoration: 'The LORD your God is in your midst.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Tsephanyah' (צְפַנְיָה) means 'the LORD has hidden.'

scripture_says.text: Haggai prophesied in the second year of Darius.
He urged the people to rebuild the temple.
He asked why they lived in paneled houses while God's house lay in ruins.
He encouraged Zerubbabel and Joshua the high priest.
He promised 'The glory of this latter house shall be greater.'
He declared Zerubbabel chosen 'like a signet ring.'

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Chaggai' (חַגַּי) means 'festal' or 'born on a feast day.'

scripture_says.text: Zechariah was son of Berechiah, grandson of Iddo.
He prophesied during the second year of Darius.
He received eight night visions.
He prophesied the Branch who would build the LORD's temple.
He prophesied the king coming on a donkey.
He prophesied thirty pieces of silver thrown to the potter.
He prophesied 'They will look on me whom they have pierced.'
He prophesied a fountain opened for sin.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Zekharyah' (זְכַרְיָה) means 'the LORD remembers.'

scripture_says.text: Malachi delivered an oracle, the word of the LORD to Israel.
He confronted priests for offering defiled sacrifices.
He condemned divorce: 'I hate divorce, says the LORD.'
He prophesied a messenger to prepare the way.
He called Israel to return to God in tithes.
He prophesied Elijah would come before the Day of the LORD.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Mal'akhi' (מַלְאָכִי) means 'my messenger.'
Malachi is the last prophetic voice before the 400 years of silence.

scripture_says.text: Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite.
They came to comfort Job when they heard of his suffering.
They sat with him seven days in silence.
They argued Job must have sinned to deserve such suffering.
God said they had not spoken what was right.
Job prayed for them and God accepted them.

history_records.text: Teman was associated with Edom (wisdom tradition).

scripture_says.text: Elihu was the son of Barachel the Buzite, of the family of Ram.
He was angry at Job for justifying himself rather than God.
He was angry at the three friends for condemning Job without answer.
He waited to speak because he was young.
He claimed to speak for God without partiality.
He emphasized God's justice and sovereignty.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Elihu' (אֱלִיהוּא) means 'He is my God.'

scripture_says.text: Philip was from Bethsaida, the city of Andrew and Peter.
Jesus found him and said 'Follow me.'
He found Nathanael and said 'We have found him of whom Moses... wrote.'
Jesus asked him where to buy bread for the 5,000.
Greeks came to him asking to see Jesus.
He asked Jesus 'Show us the Father.'
Jesus replied 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'Philippos' (Φίλιππος) means 'lover of horses.'

scripture_says.text: Bartholomew is listed among the twelve apostles.
He is often identified with Nathanael.
Jesus said of Nathanael 'Behold, an Israelite indeed, in whom is no deceit.'
Nathanael confessed 'Rabbi, you are the Son of God!'

history_records.text: 'Bartholomew' (Βαρθολομαῖος) is Aramaic for 'son of Tolmai.'

scripture_says.text: Simon is called 'the Zealot' (or 'Cananaean').
He was one of the twelve apostles.

history_records.text: 'Zealot' (ζηλωτής) indicates zeal, possibly for the law or a political movement.
'Cananaean' is from Aramaic 'qan'an' meaning 'zealous.'

question: Who was James son of Alphaeus?

scripture_says.text: James son of Alphaeus was one of the twelve apostles.
He is sometimes called 'James the Less.'

history_records.text: The Greek 'Iakōbos' (Ἰάκωβος) is from Hebrew 'Ya'aqov.'

scripture_says.text: Thaddaeus is listed among the twelve apostles.
He is also called 'Judas son of James' (not Iscariot).
He asked Jesus why He would manifest Himself to disciples and not the world.

history_records.text: 'Thaddaeus' (Θαδδαῖος) may mean 'courageous heart.'

scripture_says.text: Silas (Silvanus) was a leader among the brothers in Jerusalem.
He was sent with Paul and Barnabas to deliver the Jerusalem council's decision.
He was a prophet who encouraged the believers.
Paul chose him for the second missionary journey.
He was imprisoned with Paul in Philippi.
They sang hymns at midnight; an earthquake opened the doors.
He co-authored letters with Paul.
Peter mentions him as a faithful brother.

history_records.text: 'Silas' (Σιλᾶς) is shortened from 'Silvanus' (Latin, 'of the forest').

scripture_says.text: Titus was a Greek convert, uncircumcised.
Paul called him 'my true child in a common faith.'
Paul took him to the Jerusalem council.
He was not compelled to be circumcised.
Paul sent him to Corinth to help with the collection.
He brought good news from Corinth to Paul.
Paul left him in Crete to appoint elders.
Paul asked him to come to Nicopolis.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Titos' (Τίτος) is a Latin name of uncertain meaning.

scripture_says.text: Mark (John Mark) was the son of Mary in Jerusalem.
He was Barnabas' cousin.
He accompanied Paul and Barnabas from Jerusalem.
He went with them on the first missionary journey.
He left them at Perga and returned to Jerusalem.
Paul refused to take him again; Barnabas took him to Cyprus.
Paul later said 'Get Mark... he is useful to me for ministry.'
Peter called him 'my son.'

history_records.text: 'Mark' (Μᾶρκος) is Latin Marcus. 'John' (Ἰωάννης) is his Hebrew name.
Early tradition credits Mark with writing the Gospel based on Peter's preaching.

scripture_says.text: Apollos was a Jew from Alexandria.
He was an eloquent man, competent in the Scriptures.
He knew only the baptism of John.
Priscilla and Aquila explained the way of God more accurately.
He greatly helped believers in Achaia.
He powerfully refuted the Jews in public.
Some at Corinth said 'I follow Apollos.'
Paul said 'I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the growth.'

history_records.text: 'Apollos' (Ἀπολλῶς) is shortened from Apollonius.

scripture_says.text: Aquila was a Jew from Pontus.
He and his wife Priscilla were expelled from Rome.
Paul stayed and worked with them as tentmakers.
They went to Ephesus with Paul.
They instructed Apollos more accurately.
Paul sent greetings to them.

history_records.text: 'Aquila' (Ἀκύλας) is Latin for 'eagle.'

scripture_says.text: Philip was one of the seven chosen to serve tables.
After Stephen's death, he went to Samaria and proclaimed Christ.
He performed signs; many believed and were baptized.
An angel directed him to the road to Gaza.
He met an Ethiopian eunuch reading Isaiah.
He explained Isaiah 53 and baptized the eunuch.
The Spirit caught him away to Azotus.
He preached in all the towns to Caesarea.
Paul stayed at his house; he had four prophesying daughters.

history_records.text: The Greek 'Philippos' (Φίλιππος) means 'lover of horses.'

scripture_says.text: Cornelius was a centurion of the Italian Cohort in Caesarea.
He was a devout man who feared God.
He gave alms generously and prayed continually.
An angel told him to send for Peter.
He gathered his relatives and close friends.
He fell at Peter's feet; Peter told him to stand.
The Holy Spirit fell on all who heard Peter's message.
He and his household were baptized.

history_records.text: 'Cornelius' is a Roman family name.

scripture_says.text: Herod (Agrippa I) killed James the brother of John with the sword.
He arrested Peter during Passover.
An angel freed Peter from prison.
He had the guards executed.
He gave an oration; the people shouted 'The voice of a god!'
He did not give God glory; an angel struck him.
He was eaten by worms and died.

history_records.text: Herod Agrippa I (10 BC - 44 AD) was grandson of Herod the Great.

scripture_says.text: Felix was the governor of Judea.
Paul was sent to him by Claudius Lysias.
He heard the accusations against Paul.
He had accurate knowledge of the Way.
He heard Paul speak about faith in Christ Jesus.
He was alarmed when Paul spoke of righteousness and judgment.
He hoped Paul would give him money.
He left Paul in prison to do the Jews a favor.

history_records.text: Antonius Felix was procurator of Judea c. 52-60 AD.

scripture_says.text: Festus succeeded Felix as governor.
The Jews asked him to transfer Paul to Jerusalem.
He asked Paul if he was willing to go to Jerusalem.
Paul appealed to Caesar.
Festus said 'To Caesar you shall go.'
He consulted King Agrippa about Paul's case.
He said Paul was mad from too much learning.
He agreed Paul could have been set free if he had not appealed.

history_records.text: Porcius Festus was procurator c. 60-62 AD.

question: Where was the Garden of Eden?

scripture_says.text: God planted a garden in Eden, in the east.
A river flowed out of Eden and divided into four: Pishon, Gihon, Tigris, Euphrates.
The Pishon flowed around the land of Havilah (gold).
The Gihon flowed around the land of Cush.
Cherubim and a flaming sword guarded the way to the tree of life.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Eden' (עֵדֶן) means 'delight' or 'pleasure.'
The location is debated; the Tigris and Euphrates suggest Mesopotamia.

question: Where was the land of Canaan?

scripture_says.text: Canaan was the land promised to Abraham and his descendants.
It extended from Sidon toward Gerar to Gaza, toward Sodom and Gomorrah.
It was described as 'a land flowing with milk and honey.'
The spies explored it from the wilderness of Zin to Rehob.
Joshua led Israel to conquer it.

history_records.text: Canaan corresponds roughly to modern Israel, Palestine, Lebanon, and coastal Syria.
Canaanites are attested in Egyptian texts and the Amarna Letters.

question: Where was Egypt in biblical times?

scripture_says.text: Egypt is the land along the Nile River.
Abraham went down to Egypt during famine.
Joseph was sold into Egypt and became second to Pharaoh.
Israel lived in the land of Goshen in Egypt.
Israel was enslaved there 430 years.
God brought them out with signs and wonders.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Mitsrayim' (מִצְרַיִם) is Egypt.
Egypt's civilization along the Nile is one of the oldest in history.

scripture_says.text: Babylon was in the land of Shinar.
The Tower of Babel was built there.
Nebuchadnezzar was king of Babylon.
Judah was exiled to Babylon.
Babylon fell to Persia under Cyrus.
In Revelation, 'Babylon the Great' falls.

history_records.text: Babylon was located on the Euphrates River in modern Iraq.
The Hebrew 'Bavel' (בָּבֶל) is related to 'balal' (to confuse).

scripture_says.text: Asshur (Assyria) was founded by those who went from Shinar.
Nineveh, Rehoboth-Ir, Calah, and Resen were Assyrian cities.
Assyria conquered the northern kingdom of Israel in 722 BC.
Sennacherib of Assyria invaded Judah under Hezekiah.
God called Assyria 'the rod of my anger.'

history_records.text: Assyria was located in northern Mesopotamia (modern northern Iraq).
Major cities included Nineveh, Assur, and Nimrud.

scripture_says.text: Galilee was the northern region of Israel.
Jesus grew up in Nazareth of Galilee.
Jesus began his ministry in Galilee.
He called disciples by the Sea of Galilee.
Isaiah prophesied: 'Galilee of the Gentiles... the people have seen a great light.'
The risen Jesus appeared to disciples in Galilee.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Galil' (גָּלִיל) means 'circuit' or 'region.'
Galilee was a fertile region around the Sea of Galilee.

scripture_says.text: Omri bought the hill of Samaria and built the capital there.
Samaria became the capital of the northern kingdom.
It fell to Assyria in 722 BC.
In Jesus' time, Samaria was the region between Judea and Galilee.
Jews had no dealings with Samaritans.
Jesus spoke with the Samaritan woman at the well.
Philip preached in Samaria with great results.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Shomeron' (שֹׁמְרוֹן) means 'watch-mountain.'
Samaritans were descendants of Israelites and Assyrian settlers.

scripture_says.text: Judea was the southern region of Israel, named for Judah.
Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judea.
John the Baptist preached in the wilderness of Judea.
Jerusalem was in Judea.
People came from Judea to hear Jesus.
The gospel spread 'in all Judea and Samaria.'

history_records.text: Judea was a Roman province from 6 AD.
The Greek 'Ioudaia' (Ἰουδαία) corresponds to the land of Judah.

scripture_says.text: Jerusalem (Salem) was the city of Melchizedek.
David conquered it from the Jebusites.
David made it his capital.
Solomon built the temple there.
God chose it as the place for His name.
It was destroyed by Babylon in 586 BC.
Jesus wept over it.
Jesus was crucified and rose there.
The early church began there at Pentecost.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yerushalayim' (יְרוּשָׁלַיִם) may mean 'foundation of peace.'
Jerusalem is mentioned in Egyptian Execration Texts (19th-18th century BC).

scripture_says.text: Bethlehem was in the territory of Judah.
Rachel was buried near Bethlehem.
Ruth and Naomi returned to Bethlehem.
David was from Bethlehem.
Micah prophesied the ruler of Israel would come from Bethlehem.
Jesus was born in Bethlehem.
Herod killed the infants in Bethlehem.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Beth Lechem' (בֵּית לֶחֶם) means 'house of bread.'
Bethlehem is about 5 miles south of Jerusalem.

scripture_says.text: Joseph and Mary lived in Nazareth.
Jesus grew up in Nazareth.
Nathanael asked 'Can anything good come out of Nazareth?'
Jesus taught in the synagogue there; they tried to throw him off a cliff.
He was called 'Jesus of Nazareth.'
Matthew said this fulfilled 'He shall be called a Nazarene.'

history_records.text: Nazareth was a small village in lower Galilee.
Nazareth is not mentioned in the Old Testament.

scripture_says.text: Capernaum was on the Sea of Galilee.
Jesus made it his home base for ministry.
Peter's house was there.
Jesus taught in the synagogue there.
Many miracles occurred there.
Jesus pronounced woe on Capernaum for unbelief.

history_records.text: The name means 'village of Nahum' in Hebrew.
Ruins of a synagogue and Peter's house have been excavated.

scripture_says.text: Jericho was in the Jordan Valley, near the Dead Sea.
Israel crossed the Jordan near Jericho.
Its walls fell when Israel marched around it.
Joshua cursed whoever rebuilt it.
Elijah and Elisha passed through Jericho.
Jesus healed blind Bartimaeus there.
Zacchaeus lived in Jericho.
The Good Samaritan parable was set on the road from Jerusalem to Jericho.

history_records.text: Jericho is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities.
The Hebrew 'Yericho' (יְרִיחוֹ) may relate to 'moon' or 'fragrance.'

scripture_says.text: Damascus is mentioned as early as Abraham's time.
David conquered Damascus.
Syria (Aram) with Damascus was often Israel's enemy.
Naaman was from Damascus.
Saul was traveling to Damascus when he encountered Jesus.
He was baptized there by Ananias.
He escaped in a basket through the wall.

history_records.text: Damascus is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities.
The Aramaic 'Dammeseq' appears in ancient Near Eastern texts.

scripture_says.text: Believers scattered after Stephen's death went to Antioch.
The disciples were first called Christians in Antioch.
Barnabas brought Saul there from Tarsus.
Prophets and teachers were there, including Barnabas and Saul.
The church sent Paul and Barnabas on mission.
Paul confronted Peter there about Gentile fellowship.
The Jerusalem council's letter was sent to Antioch.

history_records.text: Antioch on the Orontes was the third largest city in the Roman Empire.
Founded by Seleucus I around 300 BC.

scripture_says.text: Paul visited Ephesus briefly on his second journey.
He stayed about three years on his third journey.
Extraordinary miracles occurred there.
Many burned their magic books.
The riot occurred at the theater over Diana worship.
Paul met the Ephesian elders at Miletus.
Paul wrote the letter to the Ephesians.
Timothy was in Ephesus.
Revelation includes a letter to the church in Ephesus.

history_records.text: Ephesus was a major port city in Asia Minor (modern Turkey).
The Temple of Artemis (Diana) was one of the Seven Wonders.

scripture_says.text: Paul came to Corinth after Athens.
He stayed a year and six months teaching.
He met Aquila and Priscilla there.
He was brought before Gallio.
He wrote two letters to the Corinthians.
The church had divisions and moral issues.

history_records.text: Corinth was a major commercial center on the isthmus connecting mainland Greece to the Peloponnese.
Known for its immorality; 'to Corinthianize' meant to act immorally.
The Gallio Inscription confirms the date of Paul's visit.

scripture_says.text: Jews from Rome were at Pentecost.
Claudius expelled Jews from Rome.
Paul desired to visit Rome.
Paul wrote his letter to the Romans.
Paul appealed to Caesar and was sent to Rome.
He lived there two years in his own rented dwelling.

history_records.text: Rome was the capital of the Roman Empire.
Paul was likely martyred in Rome under Nero.

scripture_says.text: Moses saw the burning bush at Horeb/Sinai.
God descended on Mount Sinai in fire and smoke.
Moses received the Law there.
The mountain trembled and there was thunder and lightning.
Moses was on the mountain 40 days.
Elijah traveled 40 days to Horeb and heard God's voice.

history_records.text: Sinai and Horeb appear to be the same mountain.
Traditional location is Jebel Musa in the Sinai Peninsula.

scripture_says.text: David captured the stronghold of Zion (City of David).
Zion became synonymous with Jerusalem.
God dwells in Zion.
Out of Zion shall go forth the law.
The Lamb stands on Mount Zion with 144,000.
Believers come to 'Mount Zion, the city of the living God.'

history_records.text: Originally the southeast hill of Jerusalem; later applied to the Temple Mount.

question: Where was the Mount of Olives?

scripture_says.text: The Mount of Olives is east of Jerusalem.
David ascended it weeping when fleeing Absalom.
Jesus taught on the mount overlooking the temple.
He descended it on a donkey at the triumphal entry.
Gethsemane was at its base.
Jesus ascended from there.
Zechariah prophesied the LORD would stand on it.

history_records.text: The Mount of Olives is a ridge east of Jerusalem, across the Kidron Valley.
Named for the olive groves that covered it.

scripture_says.text: Elijah challenged the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel.
Fire fell from heaven and consumed his sacrifice.
The prophets of Baal were killed at the Brook Kishon.
Elijah went to the top and prayed for rain.
Elisha was there when the Shunammite sought him.

history_records.text: Mount Carmel is a coastal mountain range in northern Israel.
The Hebrew 'Karmel' (כַּרְמֶל) means 'garden-land' or 'fruitful field.'

scripture_says.text: Lot chose the Jordan Valley as far as Zoar.
Israel crossed the Jordan on dry ground.
Elijah parted the Jordan with his cloak.
Naaman washed in the Jordan and was healed.
John baptized in the Jordan.
Jesus was baptized in the Jordan.

history_records.text: The Jordan flows from Mount Hermon to the Dead Sea.
The Hebrew 'Yarden' (יַרְדֵּן) means 'descender.'

question: Where was the Sea of Galilee?

scripture_says.text: Also called the Sea of Tiberias and Lake of Gennesaret.
Jesus called disciples by the sea.
He taught from a boat on the sea.
He calmed a storm on the sea.
He walked on the water.
He appeared to disciples there after resurrection.

history_records.text: The Sea of Galilee is a freshwater lake in northern Israel.
About 13 miles long and 8 miles wide.

scripture_says.text: Called the Salt Sea or Sea of the Arabah.
Located at the southern end of the Jordan Valley.
Sodom and Gomorrah were near this sea.
Lot's wife became a pillar of salt near there.
Ezekiel prophesied healing waters flowing to the Dead Sea.

history_records.text: The Dead Sea is the lowest point on earth's surface.
Its high salt content supports no fish life.

scripture_says.text: Golgotha means 'Place of a Skull.'
Jesus was crucified there.
It was near the city.
A garden with a new tomb was nearby.

history_records.text: The Aramaic 'Golgotha' and Latin 'Calvary' both mean 'skull.'
The Church of the Holy Sepulchre is the traditional site.

question: Where was the Garden of Gethsemane?

scripture_says.text: Gethsemane was at the foot of the Mount of Olives.
Jesus often went there with his disciples.
He prayed there before his arrest.
He was in agony; his sweat was like drops of blood.
Judas came there with soldiers to arrest him.

history_records.text: The name 'Gethsemane' means 'oil press' in Aramaic/Hebrew.
Located across the Kidron Valley from Jerusalem.

question: Where was the road to Emmaus?

scripture_says.text: Emmaus was about seven miles from Jerusalem.
Two disciples walked there on resurrection day.
Jesus joined them but they did not recognize him.
He explained the scriptures concerning himself.
Their eyes were opened when he broke bread.
He vanished from their sight.

history_records.text: The exact location of Emmaus is debated.

sources.text: Proposals include El-Qubeibeh, Abu Ghosh, and others

scripture_says.text: Nineveh was built by those from Shinar.
It was the capital of Assyria.
God sent Jonah to preach there.
It was 'an exceedingly great city, three days' journey.'
The people repented at Jonah's preaching.
Nahum prophesied its destruction.

history_records.text: Nineveh was on the Tigris River, opposite modern Mosul, Iraq.
It fell to Babylon in 612 BC.

scripture_says.text: Tyre was a Phoenician coastal city.
Hiram king of Tyre supplied materials for the temple.
Ezekiel prophesied judgment against Tyre.
Jesus visited the region of Tyre and Sidon.
He healed the Syrophoenician woman's daughter there.
Paul stayed with disciples there for seven days.

history_records.text: Tyre was known for its purple dye trade and maritime power.
Alexander the Great conquered it in 332 BC by building a causeway.

scripture_says.text: Sodom was in the Jordan Valley, well-watered.
Lot chose to live there.
Its people were 'wicked, great sinners against the LORD.'
God destroyed it with fire and brimstone.
Abraham saw smoke rising 'like the smoke of a furnace.'
It became a byword for wickedness and judgment.

history_records.text: Location debated; possibly near the Dead Sea.

sources.text: Proposed sites include Bab edh-Dhra, Tall el-Hammam

question: Where was Ur of the Chaldees?

scripture_says.text: Terah and Abram came from Ur of the Chaldeans.
God called Abram out of Ur.
Stephen said God appeared to Abraham 'while he was in Mesopotamia, before he lived in Haran.'

history_records.text: Ur was a major Sumerian city in southern Mesopotamia.
Excavated by Leonard Woolley (1922-1934), revealing royal tombs and a ziggurat.

sources.text: British Museum and University of Pennsylvania excavations

scripture_says.text: Terah took his family from Ur to Haran.
Terah died in Haran.
God called Abram to leave Haran.
Jacob fled to Laban in Haran.
He met Rachel at a well near Haran.

history_records.text: Haran was in upper Mesopotamia, on a trade route.
An important center for moon-god worship in ancient times.

scripture_says.text: Abraham built an altar near Bethel.
Jacob dreamed of a ladder there; he called it 'the house of God.'
The name was changed from Luz to Bethel.
Jeroboam set up a golden calf there.
The prophets condemned Bethel's idolatry.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Beth-El' (בֵּית־אֵל) means 'house of God.'
Located about 12 miles north of Jerusalem.

scripture_says.text: The tabernacle was set up at Shiloh after the conquest.
The land was divided among tribes at Shiloh.
Hannah prayed there and dedicated Samuel.
Eli judged Israel at Shiloh.
The ark was taken from Shiloh to battle.
God abandoned Shiloh because of Israel's sin.

history_records.text: Shiloh was the religious center of Israel before Jerusalem.

sources.text: Khirbet Seilun, about 20 miles north of Jerusalem

scripture_says.text: Abraham lived by the oaks of Mamre at Hebron.
He bought the cave of Machpelah at Hebron for a burial site.
The patriarchs and their wives were buried there.
The spies saw the descendants of Anak at Hebron.
Caleb conquered Hebron.
David was anointed king at Hebron and ruled there seven years.

history_records.text: Hebron is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities.
The Cave of the Patriarchs (Machpelah) is a major site.

scripture_says.text: In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.
God created by speaking: 'Let there be light.'
He created in six days and rested on the seventh.
He created man in His image, male and female.
He formed man from dust and breathed life into him.
All things were created through the Word (Logos).

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'bara' (בָּרָא) is used exclusively for divine creation.

question: What was the fall of man?

scripture_says.text: The serpent deceived Eve about the forbidden tree.
Eve saw the fruit was good, desirable, and would make one wise.
She ate and gave to Adam; he ate.
Their eyes were opened and they knew they were naked.
They hid from God and were afraid.
God cursed the serpent, the ground, and pronounced consequences.
They were expelled from Eden.
Through one man sin entered the world, and death through sin.
God saw that wickedness was great; every intention was only evil.
He decided to destroy all flesh with a flood.
Noah found favor and was commanded to build an ark.
Noah, his family, and animals entered the ark.
The fountains of the deep and windows of heaven opened.
Rain fell 40 days and nights; waters covered the earth.
All flesh died except those in the ark.
After a year, Noah left the ark and offered sacrifice.
God made a covenant with the rainbow as sign.

history_records.text: Flood narratives exist in many ancient cultures (Gilgamesh, Atrahasis).

question: What was the Tower of Babel?

scripture_says.text: The whole earth had one language.
People settled in Shinar and decided to build a city and tower.
They said 'Let us make a name for ourselves.'
The LORD came down to see the city and tower.
He confused their language so they could not understand each other.
He scattered them over the face of the earth.
The place was called Babel because there the LORD confused language.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Babel' (בָּבֶל) is related to 'balal' (to confuse).
Ziggurats (stepped towers) were common in Mesopotamia.

question: What was the call of Abraham?

scripture_says.text: God told Abram to leave his country, kindred, and father's house.
He promised to make him a great nation and bless him.
All families of the earth would be blessed through him.
Abram was 75 years old when he left Haran.
He took Sarai, Lot, and all their possessions to Canaan.
God appeared and promised the land to his offspring.
God commanded Israel to take a lamb on the 10th day of the month.
They were to kill it at twilight on the 14th day.
They put blood on the doorposts and lintel.
They ate the lamb roasted, with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.
The LORD passed over houses with blood; the firstborn of Egypt died.
Passover was to be kept as a memorial forever.
Christ is our Passover lamb, sacrificed for us.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Pesach' (פֶּסַח) means 'to pass over' or 'skip.'

question: What was the crossing of the Red Sea?

scripture_says.text: Pharaoh pursued Israel with chariots and horsemen.
Israel was terrified; Moses said 'Fear not, stand firm.'
God told Moses to stretch out his staff over the sea.
The LORD drove the sea back with a strong east wind all night.
Israel walked through on dry ground, waters a wall on each side.
The Egyptians pursued; God threw them into confusion.
The waters returned and covered the army; not one remained.
Israel saw the great power and believed.

history_records.text: The Hebrew 'Yam Suph' (יַם־סוּף) means 'Sea of Reeds.'

question: What was the giving of the law at Sinai?

scripture_says.text: Israel camped at Mount Sinai in the third month after leaving Egypt.
God offered to make them a kingdom of priests if they obeyed.
The mountain was covered with smoke; it trembled.
God spoke the Ten Commandments.
The people were afraid and stood far off.
Moses received the tablets of stone written by God.
He was on the mountain 40 days and nights.
Goliath was a Philistine champion, over nine feet tall.
He challenged Israel to send a champion for single combat.
Israel was dismayed and afraid.
David was a youth, tending his father's sheep.
He said 'Who is this uncircumcised Philistine?'
He refused Saul's armor and took five stones and a sling.
He said 'I come to you in the name of the LORD of hosts.'
He struck Goliath in the forehead; the stone sank in.
He cut off Goliath's head with his own sword.

question: What was the birth of Jesus?

scripture_says.text: Mary was found with child from the Holy Spirit.
Joseph was told in a dream to take Mary as his wife.
A decree from Caesar Augustus required registration.
Joseph and Mary went to Bethlehem.
She gave birth to her firstborn and laid him in a manger.
Angels announced the birth to shepherds.
Wise men came from the east following a star.
They presented gold, frankincense, and myrrh.

history_records.text: Quirinius was governor of Syria during a census.
Jesus was likely born between 6-4 BC before Herod's death.

question: What was the baptism of Jesus?

scripture_says.text: Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John.
John tried to prevent him: 'I need to be baptized by you.'
Jesus said it was fitting 'to fulfill all righteousness.'
When Jesus came up from the water, the heavens opened.
The Spirit descended on him like a dove.
A voice from heaven said 'This is my beloved Son.'

question: What was the temptation of Jesus?

scripture_says.text: Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness.
He fasted 40 days and nights.
The devil tempted him to turn stones to bread.
Jesus answered 'Man shall not live by bread alone.'
The devil took him to the temple pinnacle.
Jesus answered 'You shall not put the Lord your God to the test.'
The devil showed him all kingdoms and offered them.
Jesus answered 'You shall worship the Lord your God only.'
The devil left; angels came and ministered to him.
Jesus took Peter, James, and John up a high mountain.
He was transfigured; his face shone like the sun.
His clothes became white as light.
Moses and Elijah appeared, talking with him.
Peter offered to make three tents.
A bright cloud overshadowed them.
A voice said 'This is my beloved Son... listen to him.'
The disciples fell on their faces in fear.

history_records.text: Traditional location is Mount Tabor or Mount Hermon.

scripture_says.text: Jesus ate the Passover with his disciples.
He washed the disciples' feet.
He announced one of them would betray him.
He took bread, blessed it, and said 'This is my body.'
He took the cup and said 'This is my blood of the covenant.'
He said 'Do this in remembrance of me.'
He gave them a new commandment: 'Love one another.'

history_records.text: The traditional site is the Upper Room (Cenacle) on Mount Zion.

scripture_says.text: Jesus was scourged and mocked.
He carried his cross; Simon of Cyrene helped.
He was crucified at Golgotha between two robbers.
Pilate's inscription read 'Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews.'
He said 'Father, forgive them.'
Darkness covered the land from the sixth to ninth hour.
He cried 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'
He said 'It is finished' and gave up his spirit.
The temple curtain was torn from top to bottom.

history_records.text: Crucifixion was a Roman method of execution.
Tacitus confirms Jesus was executed under Pontius Pilate.

scripture_says.text: On the third day, women came to the tomb and found it empty.
An angel said 'He is not here, for he has risen.'
Jesus appeared to Mary Magdalene.
He appeared to the disciples in the upper room.
He showed Thomas his wounds.
He appeared to over 500 brothers at one time.
He appeared to James, then to all the apostles.
He appeared over 40 days, speaking of the kingdom.

history_records.text: The resurrection is the central event of Christian faith.

scripture_says.text: Jesus led disciples out to Bethany.
He lifted up his hands and blessed them.
He was taken up before their eyes.
A cloud took him out of their sight.
Two men in white said he would return the same way.
He sat down at the right hand of God.

history_records.text: The ascension occurred 40 days after resurrection.

scripture_says.text: The disciples were gathered in one place.
A sound like a rushing wind filled the house.
Tongues as of fire appeared on each of them.
They were all filled with the Holy Spirit.
They began to speak in other tongues.
Jews from every nation heard them in their own languages.
Peter preached; about 3,000 were baptized.

history_records.text: Pentecost (Shavuot) was 50 days after Passover.

question: What was the binding of Isaac?

scripture_says.text: God tested Abraham, commanding him to offer Isaac as a burnt offering on Mount Moriah
Abraham bound Isaac on the altar, but the angel of the LORD stopped him, providing a ram instead
Abraham named the place 'The LORD Will Provide' (Jehovah-jireh)
By faith Abraham offered Isaac, accounting that God was able to raise him from the dead

history_records.text: Jewish tradition identifies Mount Moriah with the Temple Mount in Jerusalem (2 Chr.3.1)
Called the Akedah (binding) in Jewish tradition, commemorated during Rosh Hashanah

scripture_says.text: Jacob dreamed of a ladder set up on earth with its top reaching to heaven, angels ascending and descending
The LORD stood above it and confirmed the Abrahamic covenant to Jacob
Jacob said 'This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven'
Jacob named the place Bethel (House of God)

history_records.text: Jesus references this vision in John 1.51, speaking of angels ascending and descending on the Son of Man

scripture_says.text: Joseph dreamed his brothers' sheaves bowed down to his sheaf
Joseph dreamed the sun, moon, and eleven stars bowed down to him
His father rebuked him but kept the matter in mind
The dreams were fulfilled when his brothers bowed to him in Egypt
The angel of the LORD appeared to Moses in a flame of fire from the midst of a bush that burned but was not consumed
God called to Moses from the bush and identified Himself as the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
God revealed His name as 'I AM WHO I AM' (YHWH)
God commissioned Moses to bring Israel out of Egypt

history_records.text: Traditional site at St. Catherine's Monastery on Mount Sinai (Jebel Musa)
Stephen recounts this event in Acts 7.30-34

scripture_says.text: While Moses was on the mountain, the people asked Aaron to make gods to go before them
Aaron fashioned a golden calf from their earrings and they said 'These are your gods, O Israel'
Moses broke the tablets, burned the calf, ground it to powder, and made the people drink it
About three thousand men fell that day as judgment

history_records.text: The calf likely imitated Egyptian Apis bull worship, familiar to Israel from 400 years in Egypt
Stephen references this in Acts 7.41 as an example of Israel's idolatry

question: What was the fall of Jericho?

scripture_says.text: The LORD instructed Israel to march around the city once daily for six days, seven times on the seventh day
The people shouted, the wall fell down flat, and Israel took the city
Only Rahab and her household were spared because she hid the spies
Joshua pronounced a curse on anyone who would rebuild Jericho

history_records.text: Archaeologist Kathleen Kenyon excavated Jericho 1952-1958, finding collapsed walls though dating is debated
The curse was fulfilled when Hiel of Bethel rebuilt it, losing his sons (1 Ki.16.34)

question: What happened with Samson and Delilah?

scripture_says.text: Samson loved Delilah, and the Philistine lords bribed her to discover the source of his strength
Samson revealed his strength came from his Nazirite vow - his hair had never been cut
She had his hair shaved, his strength left, and the Philistines gouged out his eyes
His hair grew back; he pulled down the temple of Dagon, killing more Philistines in his death than in his life

history_records.text: Dagon was a major Philistine deity, temples found at Gaza and Ashdod in archaeological excavations

scripture_says.text: The LORD called Samuel three times at night; Eli told him to respond 'Speak, LORD, for Your servant hears'
God revealed judgment coming on Eli's house for his sons' sins
Samuel grew, the LORD was with him, and all Israel knew he was established as a prophet
The word of the LORD was rare in those days; there was no frequent vision

question: What was Saul's anointing as king?

scripture_says.text: The LORD told Samuel that Saul would be the one to save Israel from the Philistines
Samuel poured oil on Saul's head and kissed him, saying the LORD had anointed him commander
The Spirit of the LORD would come upon him and he would be turned into another man
Samuel presented Saul to the people: 'There is none like him among all the people'

history_records.text: Saul's anointing marks the transition from the judges period to the monarchy in Israel (c. 1050 BC)

scripture_says.text: The LORD sent Samuel to Jesse the Bethlehemite, for He had provided a king among his sons
The LORD said 'Man looks at the outward appearance, but the LORD looks at the heart'
Samuel anointed David in the midst of his brothers, and the Spirit of the LORD came upon David from that day forward
David was anointed king over Israel at age 30, after first being anointed king over Judah

history_records.text: David was anointed three times: privately by Samuel (1 Sam 16), over Judah (2 Sam 2.4), and over all Israel (2 Sam 5.3)

question: What was Solomon's request for wisdom?

scripture_says.text: At Gibeon the LORD appeared to Solomon in a dream: 'Ask what I shall give you'
Solomon asked for an understanding heart to judge the people and discern between good and evil
The LORD was pleased and gave him a wise and understanding heart, with no one like him before or after
God also gave him riches and honor, and promised long life if he walked in His ways

history_records.text: Solomon's wisdom became legendary throughout the ancient Near East (1 Ki.4.29-34)

scripture_says.text: Elijah challenged 450 prophets of Baal and 400 prophets of Asherah before all Israel
The test: the God who answers by fire, He is God
The prophets of Baal called on their god from morning until evening with no answer
The fire of the LORD fell and consumed the sacrifice, and the people said 'The LORD, He is God'

history_records.text: Mount Carmel is a coastal mountain range in northern Israel, traditionally associated with Elijah
Baal worship was promoted by Jezebel, a Phoenician princess (1 Ki.16.31)

question: What was Elijah being taken to heaven?

scripture_says.text: A chariot of fire and horses of fire separated Elijah and Elisha, and Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven
Elisha cried 'My father, my father, the chariot of Israel and its horsemen!'
Elisha took up Elijah's mantle and struck the waters, which parted as they had for Elijah
Elisha had asked for a double portion of Elijah's spirit

history_records.text: Elijah is one of two people recorded as not dying (Enoch in Gen.5.24 is the other)
Malachi 4.5 prophesies Elijah's return before the day of the LORD

question: What was the fall of Samaria?

scripture_says.text: The king of Assyria besieged Samaria for three years; in the ninth year of Hoshea, Samaria was taken
Israel was carried away to Assyria and placed in Halah, by the Habor River, and in the cities of the Medes
This happened because Israel sinned against the LORD, served other gods, and did not keep His commandments
The king of Assyria brought people from other nations to inhabit the cities of Samaria

history_records.text: Assyrian records confirm Sargon II conquered Samaria in 722 BC, deporting 27,290 inhabitants
The Assyrian policy of population exchange led to the mixed Samaritan population of later centuries

question: What was the fall of Jerusalem?

scripture_says.text: Nebuchadnezzar besieged Jerusalem; the city was broken into after the famine became severe
Nebuzaradan burned the house of the LORD, the king's house, and all the houses of Jerusalem
The rest of the people left in the city were carried away captive to Babylon
God sent warnings through His messengers but they mocked them until wrath arose with no remedy

history_records.text: The Babylonian Chronicles confirm Nebuchadnezzar's campaigns against Judah
Jerusalem fell in 586 BC, ending the Davidic monarchy until the return under Cyrus

question: What was the return from exile?

scripture_says.text: Cyrus king of Persia proclaimed that the LORD had charged him to build a temple in Jerusalem
The heads of fathers' houses of Judah and Benjamin, with priests and Levites, rose up to rebuild the temple
The whole assembly together was 42,360, besides servants and singers
Isaiah prophesied Cyrus by name as the LORD's shepherd who would rebuild Jerusalem

history_records.text: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC) records Cyrus's policy of allowing exiled peoples to return home
Three returns: Zerubbabel (538 BC), Ezra (458 BC), Nehemiah (445 BC)

question: What was the rebuilding of the temple?

scripture_says.text: In the second year after returning, they began the work; when the foundation was laid, they praised the LORD
The people of the land discouraged the builders and frustrated their purpose throughout Cyrus's reign
The temple was finished in the sixth year of King Darius, according to the prophesying of Haggai and Zechariah
Some wept who had seen the first temple, for the second seemed as nothing in comparison

history_records.text: The Second Temple was completed in 516 BC, 70 years after the first temple's destruction (586 BC)
Later expanded greatly by Herod the Great (begun 20 BC), the temple Jesus knew

question: What was the wedding at Cana?

scripture_says.text: There was a wedding in Cana of Galilee; the mother of Jesus was there, and Jesus and His disciples were invited
When the wine ran out, the mother of Jesus said to Him 'They have no wine'
Jesus told the servants to fill six stone waterpots; when drawn out, the water had become wine
This beginning of signs Jesus did in Cana of Galilee, and manifested His glory, and His disciples believed

history_records.text: Cana of Galilee is traditionally identified with Kafr Kanna or Khirbet Qana, both in the Galilee region

question: What was the Sermon on the Mount?

scripture_says.text: Seeing the multitudes, Jesus went up on a mountain, and when He sat down, His disciples came and He taught them
He taught the Beatitudes: blessed are the poor in spirit, those who mourn, the meek, etc.
He said He did not come to destroy the Law but to fulfill it
The people were astonished at His teaching, for He taught as one having authority

history_records.text: Traditional site is the Mount of Beatitudes near Capernaum on the northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee

question: What was the feeding of the five thousand?

scripture_says.text: The disciples had only five loaves and two fish for a great multitude
Jesus blessed and broke the loaves; all ate and were satisfied, with twelve baskets left over
Those who ate were about five thousand men, besides women and children
The people said 'This is truly the Prophet who is to come into the world'

history_records.text: This miracle is recorded in all four Gospels (Mat.14, Mar.6, Luk.9, Joh.6)
Traditional site at Tabgha on the northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee

question: What was Jesus walking on water?

scripture_says.text: In the fourth watch of the night Jesus went to them, walking on the sea
The disciples were troubled, but Jesus said 'Be of good cheer! It is I; do not be afraid'
Peter walked on the water toward Jesus, but when he saw the wind, he was afraid and began to sink
When He got into the boat, the wind ceased, and they were greatly amazed
Jesus sent disciples for a donkey, fulfilling Zechariah: 'Your King is coming to you, lowly, sitting on a donkey'
The multitude spread their garments and branches, crying 'Hosanna to the Son of David!'
As He drew near, He wept over Jerusalem, saying 'If you had known the things that make for your peace'
His disciples did not understand at first, but when Jesus was glorified, they remembered

history_records.text: This event is commemorated on Palm Sunday in Christian tradition
The route traditionally goes from the Mount of Olives down through the Kidron Valley

question: What was the cleansing of the temple?

scripture_says.text: Jesus drove out those who bought and sold in the temple, saying 'My house shall be called a house of prayer'
He made a whip of cords and drove out those selling oxen, sheep, and doves
He said they had made the house of prayer 'a den of thieves'
His disciples remembered it was written: 'Zeal for Your house has eaten Me up'

history_records.text: The Court of the Gentiles had become a marketplace for sacrificial animals and money exchange
John records an early cleansing (Joh.2), the Synoptics record one in the final week

scripture_says.text: Jesus took Peter, James, and John and began to be sorrowful and deeply distressed
He prayed 'O My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass from Me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as You will'
His sweat became like great drops of blood falling to the ground
He told His disciples 'The hour is at hand, and the Son of Man is being betrayed into the hands of sinners'

history_records.text: Gethsemane (Hebrew: oil press) was an olive grove on the Mount of Olives (Joh.18.1)
The traditional site contains ancient olive trees, though their exact age is debated

scripture_says.text: Peter said he would never deny Jesus; Jesus said before the rooster crowed, Peter would deny Him three times
Peter denied knowing Jesus three times in the courtyard of the high priest
When the rooster crowed, Peter remembered Jesus' words and went out and wept bitterly
After the resurrection, Jesus three times asked Peter 'Do you love Me?' and restored him

history_records.text: The Church of St. Peter in Gallicantu ('of the cock-crowing') marks the traditional site

question: What happened on the road to Emmaus?

scripture_says.text: Two disciples walked to Emmaus; Jesus drew near and walked with them, but their eyes were restrained
Beginning at Moses and all the Prophets, He expounded to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning Himself
As He blessed and broke bread, their eyes were opened and they knew Him; then He vanished
They said 'Did not our heart burn within us while He talked with us on the road?'

history_records.text: Emmaus was about seven miles (60 stadia) from Jerusalem; several sites have been proposed

scripture_says.text: Thomas was not present when Jesus appeared; he said he would not believe unless he saw and touched the wounds
Eight days later Jesus appeared and said to Thomas 'Reach your finger here, and look at My hands'
Thomas answered 'My Lord and my God!'
Jesus said 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed'

history_records.text: Thomas is also called Didymus (the Twin) in John 11.16, 20.24, 21.2
Early church tradition records Thomas bringing the gospel to India

scripture_says.text: Jesus said 'All authority has been given to Me in heaven and on earth'
'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them... teaching them to observe all things'
'Go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature'
'You shall receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you; and you shall be witnesses'

history_records.text: Given on a mountain in Galilee (Mat.28.16) and at the Mount of Olives before the ascension (Act.1.8-12)

scripture_says.text: Stephen, full of faith and power, did great wonders and signs among the people
When they heard his defense, they gnashed their teeth; he saw Jesus standing at the right hand of God
They cast him out of the city and stoned him; he said 'Lord, do not charge them with this sin'
Saul was consenting to his death; a great persecution arose against the church

history_records.text: Stephen is recorded as the first Christian martyr (c. AD 34-35)
The traditional site is outside the Damascus Gate, now marked by St. Stephen's Church

scripture_says.text: Saul, breathing threats and murder against the disciples, went to Damascus to arrest Christians
A light from heaven shone around him; a voice said 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting Me?'
He arose blind and neither ate nor drank for three days
Ananias laid hands on him, something like scales fell from his eyes, and he was baptized

history_records.text: Paul recounts his conversion three times in Acts (9, 22, 26) and references it in Galatians 1.13-17
The road to Damascus is approximately 135 miles northeast of Jerusalem

scripture_says.text: Cornelius was a centurion, a devout man who feared God and prayed always
An angel told him to send for Peter in Joppa
While Peter spoke, the Holy Spirit fell on all who heard; they spoke with tongues and magnified God
Peter said 'Can anyone forbid water, that these should not be baptized?' and commanded them to be baptized

history_records.text: Caesarea Maritima was the Roman administrative capital of Judea, where Roman soldiers were stationed
This marks the first recorded Gentile conversion and reception of the Holy Spirit

scripture_says.text: Men from Judea taught that Gentiles must be circumcised; Paul and Barnabas were sent to Jerusalem about this
Peter testified how God gave the Holy Spirit to Gentiles just as to Jewish believers
James gave judgment: not to trouble Gentiles but write them to abstain from idols, sexual immorality, things strangled, and blood
The letter stated 'It seemed good to the Holy Spirit, and to us, to lay upon you no greater burden'

history_records.text: The council is dated to approximately AD 49-50
This decision established that Gentile believers did not need to become Jewish proselytes

scripture_says.text: A tempestuous wind called Euroclydon arose; the ship was caught and driven along
Paul said an angel of God had appeared: the ship would be lost but all lives would be spared
The ship ran aground and broke up; all 276 people escaped safely to land
They learned the island was called Malta

history_records.text: Malta (Melita) is an island in the central Mediterranean, 60 miles south of Sicily
Paul's journey to Rome as a prisoner is dated to approximately AD 59-60

question: What was the conflict between Jacob and Esau?

scripture_says.text: Two nations were in Rebekah's womb; the older would serve the younger
Esau sold his birthright to Jacob for a bowl of stew
Jacob deceived Isaac and received the blessing meant for Esau
After 20 years, Esau ran to meet Jacob and embraced him with tears

history_records.text: Esau became the father of the Edomites (Gen.36.1), who had ongoing conflict with Israel
Malachi 1.2-3 and Romans 9.13 reference 'Jacob I loved, Esau I hated'

question: What were the ten plagues of Egypt?

scripture_says.text: Water to blood - all water in Egypt became blood
Frogs covered the land of Egypt
Lice (gnats) came upon man and beast
Swarms of flies filled Egypt, but not Goshen where Israel lived
Pestilence killed all Egyptian livestock
Boils broke out on man and beast
Hail and fire struck Egypt, but not Goshen
Locusts covered the land and ate everything
Darkness covered Egypt for three days
Death of the firstborn throughout Egypt

history_records.text: The plagues demonstrated power over Egyptian deities (Exo.12.12)
The Ipuwer Papyrus describes calamities in Egypt, though dating is debated

question: What was the report of the twelve spies?

scripture_says.text: The LORD commanded Moses to send men to spy out the land of Canaan
The spies reported the land flowed with milk and honey, but the people were strong and cities fortified
Caleb and Joshua urged advance; the other ten said 'We are not able' and spoke of giants
God declared that those who had seen His signs but did not believe would not see the land

history_records.text: The 40-year wilderness wandering resulted from this unbelief (Num.14.33-34)
Only Joshua and Caleb of that generation entered the land (Num.14.30)

scripture_says.text: Korah, Dathan, and Abiram with 250 leaders rose up against Moses, saying 'All the congregation is holy'
Moses said if these men die a natural death, the LORD has not sent me; if the earth opens, you will know
The earth opened and swallowed them alive with all their households
Fire came out from the LORD and consumed the 250 men offering incense

history_records.text: Jude 11 warns of 'the rebellion of Korah'
The bronze censers were made into a covering for the altar as a memorial (Num.16.39-40)

scripture_says.text: The people spoke against God and Moses; the LORD sent fiery serpents that bit and killed many
Moses made a bronze serpent on a pole; anyone bitten who looked at it lived
Jesus said 'As Moses lifted up the serpent, so must the Son of Man be lifted up'
Hezekiah later destroyed it because Israel had been burning incense to it, calling it Nehushtan
Balak king of Moab summoned Balaam to curse Israel
The LORD opened the donkey's mouth to speak to Balaam
Balaam said 'How shall I curse whom God has not cursed?'
Balaam prophesied 'A Star shall come out of Jacob; a Scepter shall rise out of Israel'

history_records.text: The Deir Alla inscription (c. 840 BC) mentions 'Balaam son of Beor' as a seer
Balaam later counseled Moab to seduce Israel through immorality (Num.31.16, Rev.2.14)

question: What was the crossing of the Jordan?

scripture_says.text: When the priests' feet touched the water, the Jordan stopped flowing and stood in a heap
The priests stood firm on dry ground in the midst of the Jordan while all Israel crossed
Twelve stones were set up as a memorial, 'a sign among you'
After crossing, they celebrated Passover; the manna ceased the next day

history_records.text: The crossing occurred at flood stage during harvest time (Jos.3.15)
The location was near Jericho, traditionally near Adam (modern Damiya)

question: What was the day the sun stood still?

scripture_says.text: Joshua said 'Sun, stand still over Gibeon, and moon in the Valley of Aijalon.' The sun stopped for about a whole day
There has been no day like it before or after, when the LORD heeded the voice of a man
The LORD cast down large hailstones from heaven; more died from hailstones than by the sword
Joshua took all these kings and their land at one time, because the LORD God of Israel fought for Israel

history_records.text: The Book of Jasher is referenced as containing this account (Jos.10.13)

scripture_says.text: Gideon asked God for a sign: let dew be on the fleece only, with dry ground around it
It was so; he wrung a bowl full of water from the fleece
Gideon asked again: let the fleece be dry and dew on all the ground; God did so that night
The LORD said He would save Israel with 300 men who lapped water like dogs
Hannah, in bitterness of soul, prayed and vowed to give her son to the LORD all his life
Eli thought she was drunk because her lips moved without sound, but she explained her grief
She conceived and bore a son, naming him Samuel ('heard by God')
Hannah's song of thanksgiving: 'My heart rejoices in the LORD; my horn is exalted in the LORD'

history_records.text: Hannah's song contains themes echoed in Mary's Magnificat (Luk.1.46-55)

question: What was the capture of the ark?

scripture_says.text: Israel was defeated; the ark of God was captured; Hophni and Phinehas were killed
Phinehas' wife named her son Ichabod ('the glory has departed')
Dagon fell before the ark; the next day his head and hands were broken off
The ark was in Philistine territory seven months, causing tumors and death

history_records.text: Dagon was a major Philistine deity, temples found at Gaza, Ashdod, and Beth-shean

question: What was David's sin with Bathsheba?

scripture_says.text: David saw Bathsheba bathing, sent for her, and lay with her
She conceived and sent word to David, 'I am with child'
David had Uriah placed in fierce battle where he was killed
Nathan the prophet said to David, 'You are the man!' and pronounced judgment
Absalom stole the hearts of Israel by standing at the gate and promising better justice
Absalom proclaimed himself king at Hebron; David fled Jerusalem
Absalom's head caught in an oak tree; Joab struck him with three spears
David mourned: 'O my son Absalom—my son, my son Absalom—if only I had died in your place!'

question: What was the building of Solomon's temple?

scripture_says.text: In the 480th year after the exodus, in Solomon's fourth year, he began building the temple
The temple was finished in the eleventh year, having taken seven years to build
When the ark was brought in, the cloud of the LORD's glory filled the house
The LORD said, 'I have consecrated this house which you have built to put My name there forever'

history_records.text: Solomon's temple stood from approximately 966-586 BC until destroyed by Nebuchadnezzar
The temple was built with materials from Hiram of Tyre (1 Ki.5.1-12)

question: What was the division of the kingdom?

scripture_says.text: Because of Solomon's idolatry, God said He would tear the kingdom from his son, leaving only one tribe
Rehoboam rejected the elders' counsel and answered the people harshly
Israel said 'What portion have we in David?' and departed to their tents
Israel made Jeroboam king; only Judah followed the house of David

history_records.text: The division occurred c. 930 BC after Solomon's death
The northern kingdom (Israel) had 10 tribes; the southern (Judah) had Judah and Benjamin

question: What happened with Elisha and the bears?

scripture_says.text: Young men from Bethel mocked Elisha, saying 'Go up, you baldhead!'
Elisha turned and cursed them in the name of the LORD; two female bears mauled forty-two of them
Elisha went from there to Mount Carmel, and from there returned to Samaria

history_records.text: The Hebrew word translated 'young men' (na'ar) can refer to young adults, not children
Bethel was a center of calf worship (1 Ki.12.29); the mockery may have been religious rejection

scripture_says.text: Naaman was commander of Syria's army, a great man but a leper
Elisha sent a messenger: 'Go wash in the Jordan seven times'
Naaman dipped seven times; his flesh was restored like a little child's and he was clean
Naaman declared 'Indeed, now I know there is no God in all the earth except in Israel'

history_records.text: Jesus referenced Naaman as an example of God's grace to Gentiles (Luk.4.27)

question: What was Hezekiah's illness and healing?

scripture_says.text: Isaiah told Hezekiah, 'Set your house in order, for you shall die and not live'
Hezekiah turned to the wall and prayed, weeping bitterly
The LORD said, 'I have heard your prayer, seen your tears; I will add fifteen years to your life'
The sign: the shadow went backward ten degrees on the sundial of Ahaz

history_records.text: This event occurred c. 701 BC during the Assyrian threat (2 Ki.20.6)
Isaiah 38-39 provides the parallel account with Hezekiah's psalm of thanksgiving

scripture_says.text: Hilkiah the priest found the Book of the Law in the house of the LORD
When the king heard the words of the Law, he tore his clothes
The king made a covenant before the LORD to keep His commandments; all the people joined
There was no king like him who turned to the LORD with all his heart, soul, and might

history_records.text: Josiah's reforms (c. 622 BC) were the last major revival before Jerusalem's fall
The 'Book of the Law' was likely Deuteronomy, found during temple repairs

question: What was the annunciation to Mary?

scripture_says.text: Gabriel was sent to Nazareth, to a virgin betrothed to Joseph, of the house of David
Gabriel said she would conceive and bear a Son called Jesus, who would reign forever
Mary asked how this could be; Gabriel said the Holy Spirit would come upon her
Mary said, 'Behold the maidservant of the Lord! Let it be to me according to your word'

history_records.text: The Church of the Annunciation in Nazareth marks the traditional site

question: What was Mary's visit to Elizabeth?

scripture_says.text: Mary went with haste to the hill country, to a city of Judah, and greeted Elizabeth
When Elizabeth heard Mary's greeting, the baby leaped in her womb; Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit
Elizabeth said, 'Why is this granted to me, that the mother of my Lord should come to me?'
Mary's song (Magnificat): 'My soul magnifies the Lord, and my spirit has rejoiced in God my Savior'

history_records.text: Traditional site is Ein Karem, west of Jerusalem, now marked by the Church of the Visitation

question: What was the angels' announcement to the shepherds?

scripture_says.text: Shepherds were in the field keeping watch; an angel of the Lord appeared and glory shone around them
The angel said, 'There is born to you this day in the city of David a Savior, who is Christ the Lord'
A multitude of the heavenly host praised God: 'Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace'
The shepherds found Mary, Joseph, and the baby, and made widely known what they had been told

history_records.text: Shepherds' Field near Bethlehem is the traditional site, now marked by chapels

question: What was the visit of the Magi?

scripture_says.text: Wise men from the East came to Jerusalem saying, 'Where is He who has been born King of the Jews? We have seen His star'
The star went before them and stood over where the child was; they fell down and worshiped Him
They presented gifts: gold, frankincense, and myrrh
Being warned in a dream, they departed to their own country another way

history_records.text: Magi were Persian or Babylonian astrologers/scholars
The number three is traditional but not stated in Scripture; only three gifts are mentioned

question: What was the flight to Egypt?

scripture_says.text: An angel appeared to Joseph in a dream: 'Arise, take the child and His mother, flee to Egypt'
Joseph departed by night and stayed in Egypt until Herod's death, fulfilling 'Out of Egypt I called My Son'
Herod killed all male children in Bethlehem two years old and under
After Herod's death, an angel told Joseph to return to the land of Israel

history_records.text: Herod the Great died in 4 BC; the flight to Egypt and return occurred before this date
Josephus records Herod's cruelty, including killing his own sons

question: What was Jesus at the temple at age twelve?

scripture_says.text: Jesus' parents went to Jerusalem every year for Passover; when He was twelve, He went with them
After the feast, Jesus stayed behind; His parents thought He was in the company and sought Him
After three days they found Him in the temple, sitting among the teachers, listening and asking questions
Jesus said, 'Did you not know that I must be about My Father's business?'

history_records.text: At twelve, Jewish boys prepared for their responsibilities under the law (bar mitzvah age is 13)

question: What was the sending of the twelve?

scripture_says.text: Jesus called His twelve disciples and gave them power over unclean spirits and to heal every disease
He sent them to the lost sheep of Israel: 'Heal the sick, raise the dead, cleanse lepers, cast out demons'
He told them to take no gold, silver, bag, or extra clothing
They went out preaching repentance, cast out demons, and anointed the sick with oil and healed them

question: What was the sending of the seventy?

scripture_says.text: The Lord appointed seventy others and sent them two by two before His face into every city and place
'The harvest is plentiful, but the laborers are few; I send you out as lambs among wolves'
'Heal the sick there, and say to them, "The kingdom of God has come near to you"'
The seventy returned with joy saying even demons were subject to them in His name

history_records.text: Some manuscripts read 'seventy-two' instead of seventy

question: What was the raising of Lazarus?

scripture_says.text: The sisters sent word, 'Lord, he whom You love is sick.' Jesus said, 'This sickness is for the glory of God'
When Jesus came, Lazarus had already been in the tomb four days
Jesus said, 'I am the resurrection and the life. He who believes in Me, though he may die, he shall live'
Jesus cried with a loud voice, 'Lazarus, come forth!' And he who had died came out

history_records.text: Traditional site is at Bethany (modern al-Eizariya, 'place of Lazarus'), 2 miles east of Jerusalem

question: What was the anointing at Bethany?

scripture_says.text: Mary took a pound of costly spikenard, anointed Jesus' feet, and wiped His feet with her hair
A woman broke an alabaster flask of very costly spikenard and poured it on His head
Some indignantly said it could have been sold for more than three hundred denarii for the poor
Jesus said she had anointed His body for burial; wherever the gospel is preached, this would be told

history_records.text: Spikenard (nard) was an expensive aromatic oil imported from the Himalayas
300 denarii was nearly a year's wages for a laborer

question: What was the miracle of creation?

scripture_says.text: In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth
God said 'Let there be light,' and there was light
By the word of the LORD the heavens were made, and all the host of them by the breath of His mouth
By faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the word of God
Enoch walked with God three hundred years; then he was not, for God took him
By faith Enoch was taken away so that he did not see death, and was not found because God had taken him

history_records.text: Enoch is one of only two people in Scripture recorded as not dying (Elijah is the other)

question: What was the flood as a miracle?

scripture_says.text: The fountains of the great deep were broken up, the windows of heaven were opened, and it rained forty days
The waters prevailed exceedingly; all the high hills under the whole heaven were covered
God remembered Noah; He made a wind pass over the earth and the waters subsided
The world that then existed perished, being flooded with water

history_records.text: Flood narratives exist in many ancient cultures (Gilgamesh Epic, Atrahasis)
The biblical account provides specific dimensions and timeline

question: What was the confusion of languages at Babel?

scripture_says.text: The LORD said 'Come, let Us go down and there confuse their language'
The LORD scattered them abroad; the name is Babel because there He confused the language of all the earth
The whole earth had one language and one speech before this event

history_records.text: Ancient ziggurats in Mesopotamia may relate to the 'tower with its top in the heavens'

question: What was the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah?

scripture_says.text: The LORD rained brimstone and fire on Sodom and Gomorrah from the LORD out of heaven
Abraham looked toward Sodom; the smoke of the land went up like the smoke of a furnace
Lot's wife looked back and became a pillar of salt
He condemned the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah to destruction, making them an example

history_records.text: Possible locations include the southern end of the Dead Sea, where geological evidence of destruction exists

question: What was Lot's wife becoming a pillar of salt?

scripture_says.text: The angels said, 'Escape for your life! Do not look behind you or stay anywhere in the plain'
But his wife looked back behind him, and she became a pillar of salt
Jesus said, 'Remember Lot's wife'

history_records.text: The Dead Sea region contains numerous salt formations and pillar-shaped rock formations

question: What was the miracle of Isaac's birth?

scripture_says.text: Abraham was one hundred years old; Sarah was ninety
Sarah had passed the age of childbearing; she laughed within herself
The LORD visited Sarah as He had said; she conceived and bore Abraham a son in his old age
By faith Sarah herself received strength to conceive seed when she was past age

question: What was Moses' rod becoming a serpent?

scripture_says.text: Moses cast down his rod; it became a serpent. He caught it by the tail and it became a rod again
Aaron's rod became a serpent before Pharaoh; the magicians did likewise, but Aaron's rod swallowed theirs
Aaron spoke all the words and did the signs; the people believed

question: What was Moses' hand becoming leprous?

scripture_says.text: Moses put his hand in his bosom; when he took it out, it was leprous, white as snow. He put it back and it was restored
This was a second sign if they would not believe the first

question: What was the parting of the Red Sea?

scripture_says.text: Moses stretched out his hand; the LORD caused the sea to go back by a strong east wind; the waters were divided
Moses stretched out his hand; the sea returned; the waters covered the chariots and horsemen of Pharaoh's army
With the blast of Your nostrils the waters were gathered together; the floods stood upright like a heap
By faith they passed through the Red Sea as by dry land

history_records.text: Various theories exist about the crossing location (Gulf of Suez, Gulf of Aqaba, or Reed Sea marshes)

question: What was the healing of waters at Marah?

scripture_says.text: They could not drink the waters of Marah because they were bitter
The LORD showed Moses a tree; when he cast it into the waters, the waters were made sweet

history_records.text: Marah means 'bitter' in Hebrew

question: What was the manna from heaven?

scripture_says.text: A small round substance, fine as frost, lay on the ground; Moses said 'This is the bread which the LORD has given'
Israel ate manna forty years until they came to the border of Canaan
He fed you with manna that you did not know, to teach that man does not live by bread alone
Jesus said, 'I am the bread of life' after they mentioned the manna Moses gave

history_records.text: Various natural explanations have been proposed (tamarisk secretions) but none match the biblical description

question: What was water from the rock?

scripture_says.text: The LORD told Moses to strike the rock at Horeb; water came out of it for the people to drink
At Kadesh, Moses was told to speak to the rock; instead he struck it twice; water came out abundantly
They drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and that Rock was Christ
He split the rocks in the wilderness and gave them drink in abundance

question: What was the provision of quail?

scripture_says.text: In the evening quail came up and covered the camp
A wind brought quail from the sea, about two cubits deep on the ground; they gathered them all day
While the meat was still between their teeth, the wrath of the LORD was aroused against them
He caused an east wind to blow and rained meat on them like dust

history_records.text: Quail migrate through the Sinai region, though the quantity described is miraculous

question: What was Aaron's rod that budded?

scripture_says.text: The LORD told Moses to collect a rod from each tribe's leader; the man God chose would have his rod bud
The rod of Aaron for the house of Levi had sprouted, put forth buds, produced blossoms, and yielded almonds
The LORD said to put Aaron's rod back before the testimony, as a sign against the rebels
The ark contained the golden pot of manna, Aaron's rod that budded, and the tablets of the covenant

question: What was the parting of the Jordan?

scripture_says.text: When the priests' feet dipped in the edge of the water, the Jordan was cut off and stood still in a heap far away
The priests stood firm on dry ground in the midst of the Jordan until all Israel crossed
The LORD dried up the Jordan before you until you had crossed, as He did to the Red Sea
Elijah struck the waters with his mantle; they divided so he and Elisha crossed on dry ground

history_records.text: The crossing occurred during flood season when the Jordan overflows its banks (Jos.3.15)

question: What was the falling of Jericho's walls?

scripture_says.text: The people shouted; the wall fell down flat so that the people went up into the city
When you hear the trumpet sound, all the people shall shout; the wall will fall down flat
By faith the walls of Jericho fell down after they were encircled for seven days

history_records.text: Kathleen Kenyon's excavations (1952-1958) found collapsed walls, though dating is debated

scripture_says.text: The Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him, and he tore the lion apart with his bare hands
The Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him; he found a fresh jawbone and killed a thousand men
Samson carried the doors of the gate of Gaza to the top of a hill before Hebron
He pushed the pillars of Dagon's temple; the temple fell on the lords and all the people

history_records.text: Samson was a Nazirite from birth, his strength connected to his uncut hair as a sign of his vow

question: What was the widow's oil multiplied?

scripture_says.text: Elisha asked what she had; she said a jar of oil. He told her to borrow vessels and pour
The oil flowed until all the vessels were full; when there was no more vessel, the oil ceased
Elisha said, 'Go, sell the oil and pay your debt, and you and your sons live on the rest'

question: What was the raising of the Shunammite's son?

scripture_says.text: Elisha promised the Shunammite woman a son; she conceived and bore a son
When the child died, Elisha stretched himself on him; the child sneezed seven times and opened his eyes
Elisha called her and said 'Pick up your son'; she fell at his feet and took up her son

question: What was the healing of the poisonous stew?

scripture_says.text: One gathered wild gourds and shred them into the pot; when they ate, they cried 'There is death in the pot!'
Elisha said, 'Bring some flour.' He cast it into the pot and said, 'Serve the people.' There was no harm

question: What was Elisha feeding a hundred men?

scripture_says.text: A man brought twenty loaves of barley bread; his servant asked how to set this before a hundred men
Elisha said 'Give it to the people that they may eat, for thus says the LORD: They shall eat and have some left'
They ate and had some left over, according to the word of the LORD
As one was cutting a beam, the iron axhead fell into the water; he cried 'Alas, master! It was borrowed!'
Elisha cut off a stick and threw it in; the iron floated
Elisha said, 'Pick it up for yourself.' So he reached out his hand and took it

question: What was the blinding of the Syrian army?

scripture_says.text: When the Syrians came down, Elisha prayed, 'Strike this people with blindness.' And He struck them with blindness
Elisha led them to Samaria, saying 'Follow me, and I will bring you to the man you seek'
In Samaria, Elisha prayed 'LORD, open their eyes.' They saw they were in the midst of Samaria
The king fed them and sent them away; the Syrian raiders came no more into Israel

question: What was the dead man revived by Elisha's bones?

scripture_says.text: As they were burying a man, they saw raiders; they threw the man into Elisha's tomb
When the man touched the bones of Elisha, he revived and stood on his feet

question: What was the destruction of Sennacherib's army?

scripture_says.text: That night the angel of the LORD went out and struck 185,000 in the camp of the Assyrians
Sennacherib departed and dwelt at Nineveh, where his sons struck him down with the sword
Isaiah's parallel account of the same event

history_records.text: Sennacherib's own annals mention besieging Jerusalem but not capturing it
Herodotus records a plague of mice destroying Sennacherib's army (possibly indicating disease)

question: What was the shadow going backward on the sundial?

scripture_says.text: Isaiah said, 'Shall the shadow go forward ten degrees, or backward?' Hezekiah chose backward
Isaiah cried to the LORD, and He brought the shadow ten degrees backward on the sundial of Ahaz
The sun returned ten degrees by which it had gone down on the sundial of Ahaz

question: What was the deliverance from the fiery furnace?

scripture_says.text: Nebuchadnezzar commanded the furnace be heated seven times more; they bound Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego
The king saw four men loose, walking unharmed in the fire; the fourth looked like 'a son of the gods'
The fire had no power on their bodies; not a hair was singed, nor their garments affected, nor smell of fire on them
Nebuchadnezzar blessed their God who sent His angel and delivered them

question: What was Daniel in the lions' den?

scripture_says.text: The king commanded Daniel to be cast into the den of lions
Daniel said, 'My God sent His angel and shut the lions' mouths, so that they have not hurt me'
Daniel was taken up from the den; no injury was found on him because he believed in his God
The king cast his accusers into the den; the lions overpowered them before they reached the bottom

question: What was Jonah and the great fish?

scripture_says.text: The LORD had prepared a great fish to swallow Jonah, and he was in its belly three days and three nights
Jonah prayed to the LORD his God from the fish's belly
The LORD spoke to the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land
Jesus said, 'As Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be'

question: What was turning water into wine?

scripture_says.text: Jesus told them to fill six stone waterpots with water; when drawn out, the water had become wine
The master of the feast said, 'You have kept the good wine until now'
This beginning of signs Jesus did in Cana of Galilee, manifesting His glory

history_records.text: Each waterpot held 20-30 gallons (2-3 metretes), totaling 120-180 gallons of wine

question: What was the calming of the storm?

scripture_says.text: A great windstorm arose; the waves beat into the boat; Jesus was asleep on a pillow
He arose and rebuked the wind, and said to the sea, 'Peace, be still!' The wind ceased and there was great calm
